Part 1 — Section 1

Distributed Systems Foundations

Before Spark, understand the distributed systems principles Spark is built upon.

1 — Distributed Systems Foundations

1.1 Cluster Computing Architecture

A compute cluster is a collection of interconnected machines (nodes) that work together to process data in parallel. At the physical level, nodes are organized into racks — groups of 20–40 servers sharing a Top-of-Rack (ToR) network switch — which reside within data centers. Modern Spark clusters map directly onto this hierarchy: a Master/Driver node coordinates work, while Worker nodes in multiple racks perform parallel computation.

The master-worker architecture pattern is foundational: a single master node maintains global state (job DAG, task assignments, metadata), while worker nodes execute individual tasks on local data slices. This is exactly how Spark's Driver–Executor model operates. The cluster is built on the commodity hardware design philosophy — instead of expensive high-end servers, Spark leverages many cheap, failure-prone machines and handles failures in software via RDD lineage recomputation.

Spark is network topology aware. When scheduling tasks, Spark considers rack placement of data blocks (via HDFS) and prefers to schedule computation on nodes where data already resides. This rack-aware scheduling minimizes cross-rack network traffic, which is the scarcest resource in large clusters.

⚙️ Engineering Insight In cloud deployments (AWS EMR, GCP Dataproc), the rack concept maps to Availability Zones. Cross-AZ traffic incurs latency and cost — always co-locate your Spark cluster and data in the same AZ/region.

1.2 Horizontal vs Vertical Scaling

Vertical scaling (scale-up) means adding more CPU, RAM, or faster disks to a single machine. This is simple but hits hardware limits quickly — a single server maxes out at a few TB of RAM and ~128 cores. Costs escalate non-linearly: a 2× bigger server often costs 4× more.

Horizontal scaling (scale-out) means adding more nodes to the cluster. This is Spark's design principle. Adding 10 more worker nodes linearly increases total compute capacity. Cloud elasticity makes this seamless: auto-scaling groups can spin up hundreds of executors for a peak job and release them immediately after.

Spark achieves near-linear horizontal scalability for most workloads because its DAG scheduler can split work into thousands of independent tasks. The trade-off is coordination overhead — more nodes means more shuffle network traffic, more heartbeat RPCs, and more scheduling decisions. Beyond ~5000 executors, diminishing returns appear due to Driver-side scheduling bottlenecks.

1.3 Shared-Nothing Architecture

In a shared-nothing architecture, each node owns its CPU, memory, and local disk independently. No two nodes access the same physical memory or disk. This eliminates contention and locking — every node can proceed in parallel without waiting for shared resources.

Contrast this with shared-disk systems (Oracle RAC) where all nodes read/write a common SAN, or shared-memory systems (SMP) where all CPUs access one memory bus. Shared-nothing trades simplicity for scalability — it scales nearly infinitely but requires explicit data movement (shuffle) when tasks on different nodes need to exchange data.

Spark implements shared-nothing via independent executors: each executor JVM has its own memory heap, its own BlockManager cache, and its own local disk for shuffle spill. Executors never directly access another executor's memory. When data must move between executors (for joins, groupBys), Spark performs a shuffle — serializing data, writing it to disk, transferring it over the network, and deserializing at the destination.

1.4 Data Partitioning Strategies

Partitioning is how distributed systems divide data across nodes. The choice of partitioning strategy directly affects parallelism, data skew, and shuffle cost. Spark supports four strategies:

  • Hash partitioning: hash(key) % N — uniform distribution, used by default in Spark shuffles. Risk: low-cardinality keys (boolean, country code) cause skew.
  • Range partitioning: Partition by sorted key ranges. Good for range queries and sorted output. Risk: uneven data distribution if key ranges aren't balanced.
  • Round-robin partitioning: Cyclically assign records across partitions. Perfect balance, but no key semantics — cannot co-locate same keys.
  • Custom partitioning: User-defined Partitioner class. Used when domain knowledge dictates optimal placement (e.g., geospatial tiling).

Partition count directly affects parallelism: too few partitions → large tasks → OOM risk; too many → scheduling overhead. A good rule of thumb is 128–400 MB of data per partition.

🎯 Interview Tip "Why does hash partitioning cause skew on low-cardinality keys?" — Because hash(true) % N and hash(false) % N each map to only one partition. If 90% of data is true, one partition gets 90% of all data — a classic skew scenario.

1.5 Data Locality

Data locality means scheduling computation where data already resides, avoiding expensive network transfers. Spark's Task Scheduler implements a locality hierarchy with configurable wait times:

  • PROCESS_LOCAL — Data is already in the executor's JVM memory (cache hit). Latency: <1ms.
  • NODE_LOCAL — Data is on the same machine's disk (HDFS block local). Latency: ~1ms.
  • RACK_LOCAL — Data is on a different node in the same network rack. Latency: ~10ms.
  • ANY — Data is anywhere in the cluster. Latency: ~100ms+.

With spark.locality.wait=3s (default), Spark waits up to 3 seconds for a local slot before relaxing to the next level. In cloud object storage (S3, GCS), data locality is largely lost because data is remote by design — the focus shifts to minimizing the amount of data read via predicate pushdown and column pruning.

1.6 Fault Tolerance Mechanisms

In a cluster of thousands of machines, failures are inevitable, not exceptional. Spark handles four categories of failure:

  • Node crash: Worker node dies → executor lost → Spark recomputes affected partitions via RDD lineage
  • Network partition: Node becomes unreachable → treated as crash after heartbeat timeout
  • Straggler node: Node is slow (hardware degradation, noisy neighbor) → speculative execution re-launches task elsewhere
  • Process OOM: Executor JVM runs out of memory → container killed → partition recomputed

Spark's primary fault tolerance mechanism is lineage-based recomputation: because every RDD records the transformations that produced it (the lineage graph), any lost partition can be reconstructed by replaying those transformations on the parent data. This avoids the cost of data replication entirely for intermediate results.

For very long lineage chains, checkpointing truncates the lineage by materializing the RDD to stable storage (HDFS/S3), creating a new "starting point" for recovery. Speculative execution (spark.speculation=true) handles stragglers by launching duplicate tasks and using whichever finishes first.

1.7 Replication Strategies

Full replication copies each data block to N nodes (HDFS default: 3 replicas). This provides high availability — any 2 of 3 nodes can fail and data remains accessible. The cost: 3× storage and 3× write amplification. Erasure coding is a space-efficient alternative: data is encoded with parity blocks using Reed-Solomon codes, achieving fault tolerance at ~1.5× storage overhead instead of 3×, but with higher CPU cost for reconstruction.

Spark itself does not replicate intermediate data by default — it relies on source storage (HDFS, S3) for input data durability, and uses lineage recomputation for intermediate fault tolerance. The exception: when you persist with MEMORY_ONLY_2 or MEMORY_AND_DISK_2, Spark replicates cached blocks to 2 executors for resilience.

1.8 Network Communication in Distributed Systems

Distributed systems rely on Remote Procedure Calls (RPCs) to coordinate — the Driver sends task descriptions to Executors, Executors report heartbeats and results back. The efficiency of this communication layer directly impacts cluster performance.

Serialization overhead is a critical factor. Every object sent over the network must be serialized to bytes and deserialized at the destination. Java's default serialization is notoriously slow and bloated; Kryo serialization is 2–10× faster and produces 2–5× smaller payloads. Spark's Tungsten engine goes further by using an off-heap binary format (UnsafeRow) that avoids serialization entirely for internal operations.

Spark uses Netty for all network communication — a high-performance, non-blocking, asynchronous I/O framework. The RPC layer handles heartbeats, task dispatch, shuffle block transfers, and broadcast variable distribution. Key configs: spark.rpc.message.maxSize (default 128 MB — increase for large broadcast variables) and spark.network.timeout (default 120s — increase for slow networks).

1.9 CAP Theorem Basics

The CAP theorem states that a distributed data system can guarantee at most two of three properties simultaneously:

  • Consistency (C): All nodes see the same data at the same time — every read returns the most recent write.
  • Availability (A): Every request receives a response (success or failure) within a bounded time.
  • Partition Tolerance (P): The system continues to operate even when network partitions split nodes into isolated groups.

Since network partitions are unavoidable in real clusters, the practical choice is between CP (sacrifice availability for consistency) and AP (sacrifice consistency for availability). Spark is an AP system — it prioritizes availability and partition tolerance. If an executor is unreachable, Spark recomputes lost partitions elsewhere rather than waiting for the disconnected node. This means intermediate results may temporarily diverge, but the final output is always consistent because Spark's deterministic lineage guarantees reproducibility.

For exactly-once semantics in Structured Streaming, additional coordination is needed — this is provided by transactional sinks like Delta Lake and idempotent checkpoint mechanisms.

🎯 Interview Tip "Where does Spark sit in CAP?" — Spark is an AP system for batch processing. It prioritizes availability (recomputing on failures) and partition tolerance (executors are independent). For streaming exactly-once semantics, additional mechanisms (transactional sinks, idempotent checkpointing) layer CP guarantees on top.

Section 1 — Key Takeaways

ConceptCore IdeaSpark's Implementation
Cluster ArchitectureMaster coordinates, workers execute in parallelDriver + Executors across racks
Horizontal ScalingAdd more nodes for more throughputNear-linear with executor count
Shared-NothingEach node has private CPU/mem/diskIndependent executors, shuffle for data exchange
PartitioningSplit data across nodes for parallelismHash (shuffle), range, round-robin, custom
Data LocalityCompute where data livesPROCESS_LOCAL → NODE_LOCAL → RACK_LOCAL → ANY
Fault ToleranceRecover from node/process failuresRDD lineage recomputation + checkpointing
ReplicationCopy data for durabilityRelies on HDFS/S3; optional _2 storage levels
NetworkEfficient serialized communicationNetty async I/O + Kryo/Tungsten serialization
CAP TheoremPick 2 of 3: C, A, PAP system — recompute on failure
⚙️ Engineering Insight Every performance problem in Spark traces back to these distributed systems fundamentals: shuffle = network cost (shared-nothing penalty), skew = bad partitioning, OOM = partition too large, slow task = lost locality. Master these foundations and Spark debugging becomes systematic, not guesswork.
Part 2 — Section 2

Introduction to Apache Spark

Understanding what Spark is, where it came from, and why it dominates the big data landscape.

2 — Introduction to Apache Spark

2.1 What Is Apache Spark?

Apache Spark is an open-source, unified analytics engine for large-scale data processing. Unlike its predecessor Hadoop MapReduce, Spark keeps intermediate results in memory between stages — eliminating the constant disk I/O that made MapReduce slow for iterative and interactive workloads.

Spark is general-purpose: a single engine handles batch ETL, real-time streaming, machine learning, graph processing, and SQL analytics. It exposes a polyglot API — Scala (native), Java, Python (PySpark), R (SparkR), and SQL — allowing data engineers, data scientists, and analysts to work in their language of choice while the same optimized engine runs underneath.

At its core, Spark is a distributed DAG execution engine: user code is compiled into a Directed Acyclic Graph of stages and tasks, optimized by the Catalyst optimizer, and executed in parallel across a cluster of machines.

2.2 History and Origin

Spark was born in 2009 at UC Berkeley's AMPLab as a research project by Matei Zaharia. The core idea was simple but powerful: what if intermediate data could live in memory instead of being written to HDFS disk after every operation?

The landmark 2012 paper — "Resilient Distributed Datasets: A Fault-Tolerant Abstraction for In-Memory Cluster Computing" — formalized the RDD abstraction that made this possible. From there, Spark's evolution was rapid and transformative.

2.3 Hadoop MapReduce Limitations

To understand why Spark was created, you must understand the pain of Hadoop MapReduce:

  • Rigid two-phase model: Every job must fit into Map → Shuffle → Reduce. Multi-step pipelines require chaining multiple MR jobs, each reading/writing HDFS.
  • Disk I/O amplification: Every intermediate result is written to HDFS — even between stages of the same logical pipeline. A 3-stage job does 3× full disk writes.
  • No iterative support: ML algorithms that need 100 passes over data must re-read HDFS 100 times.
  • Verbose API: Even simple word-count requires 50+ lines of Java boilerplate.
  • High latency: Job startup overhead (JVM spin-up, YARN negotiation) makes interactive queries impractical.
  • No unified engine: Separate systems needed for SQL (Hive), streaming (Storm), ML (Mahout), each with different APIs and operational overhead.
🎯 Interview Tip When asked "Why is Spark faster than MapReduce?" — the answer is NOT just "in-memory." It's the combination of: (1) in-memory intermediate storage, (2) DAG execution avoiding unnecessary stages, (3) Catalyst query optimization, (4) Tungsten binary format reducing GC overhead, and (5) pipelining narrow transforms within a stage.

2.4 Spark vs Hadoop MapReduce

DimensionHadoop MapReduceApache Spark
Processing modelDisk-based (HDFS I/O every stage)In-memory (disk spill only on overflow)
SpeedBaseline10–100× faster for iterative workloads
API levelLow-level Java Mapper/ReducerHigh-level SQL / DataFrame / RDD
Iterative supportPoor — re-reads HDFS each iterationNative — data cached in memory
StreamingNot nativeStructured Streaming (native)
ML supportMahout (limited, slow)MLlib (native, distributed)
SQL supportHiveQL (slow MR compilation)Spark SQL (Catalyst optimizer)
Fault toleranceHDFS replicationRDD lineage recomputation
DeploymentYARN onlyStandalone, YARN, Kubernetes

2.5 The Spark Ecosystem

Spark is not a single tool — it's a layered ecosystem with a shared engine at the base and specialized libraries on top. All higher-level libraries compile down to Spark Core operations, meaning they share the same optimizations, fault tolerance, and cluster management.

2.5.1 Spark Core

The foundation layer providing task scheduling, memory management, fault tolerance, and I/O. The RDD API lives here. All higher-level libraries (SQL, Streaming, MLlib, GraphX) compile their operations down to Spark Core primitives. The BlockManager manages distributed storage and caching across the cluster.

2.5.2 Spark SQL

Structured data processing using DataFrames and Datasets with full ANSI SQL support. The Catalyst optimizer applies rule-based and cost-based query optimization automatically. The Tungsten engine generates JVM bytecode for whole-stage execution. Connects to the Hive Metastore for table catalog management. This is the most widely used Spark API in production.

2.5.3 Structured Streaming

Stream processing using the same DataFrame API as batch — same code, streaming semantics. Default mode is micro-batch (seconds-level latency); experimental continuous mode achieves sub-millisecond latency. Native sources include Kafka, Kinesis, Delta Lake, and file systems. Supports stateful operations like aggregations, stream-stream joins, and deduplication.

2.5.4 MLlib

Distributed machine learning at scale with the ML Pipelines API: chains of Transformer → Estimator → Pipeline. Includes algorithms for classification, regression, clustering, and collaborative filtering. Feature engineering tools: tokenization, TF-IDF, scaling, encoding. Deep integration with pandas via Apache Arrow for pandas UDFs and efficient data exchange.

2.5.5 GraphX

Graph-parallel computation on top of the RDD API. Supports property graphs with attributes on both vertices and edges. Built-in algorithms include PageRank, Triangle Counting, Connected Components, and ShortestPaths. The Pregel API enables iterative graph algorithms. Note: largely superseded by the GraphFrames library which works with DataFrames and benefits from Catalyst optimization.

2.6 Why Spark Became the Dominant Big Data Engine

Spark didn't just replace MapReduce — it became the default compute engine for the entire data platform. Six factors drove this dominance:

  • Unification: One engine for batch ETL, SQL, streaming, and ML eliminates the operational complexity of running separate systems (Hive + Storm + Mahout + custom glue code).
  • Speed: The in-memory execution model eliminates the disk I/O bottleneck that made MapReduce slow for iterative and interactive workloads.
  • Developer experience: Python-first ecosystem (PySpark) matches the workflow of data scientists and ML engineers. The DataFrame API is intuitive for anyone who knows SQL or pandas.
  • Cloud-native: Deep integrations with AWS EMR, Databricks, GCP Dataproc, and Azure HDInsight make Spark available as a managed service with zero infrastructure setup.
  • Community: Largest open-source big data project with 1,000+ contributors and massive StackOverflow/GitHub presence.
  • Commercial backing: Databricks (founded by Spark creators) drives rapid innovation, enterprise adoption, and table format standards (Delta Lake).
⚙️ Engineering Insight At Netflix, Uber, and LinkedIn — Spark processes petabytes daily across batch ETL, streaming pipelines, ML feature engineering, and ad-hoc analytics. The key advantage is not raw speed — it's the ability to hire one team that knows one engine to handle all these workloads instead of specialists for five different systems.
⚠️ Common Pitfall "Spark is always faster than everything" — this is false. For sub-second interactive SQL, Trino/Presto is faster (no JVM startup overhead, MPP execution). For sub-millisecond streaming, Apache Flink is better (true event-by-event processing). Spark wins on versatility and unified platform, not on every individual dimension.
Part 3 — Section 3

Spark Architecture Deep Dive

The Driver, Executors, Cluster Manager, and how they orchestrate distributed computation.

3 — Spark Architecture Deep Dive

3.1 High-Level Architecture Overview

Every Spark application consists of three fundamental components: a Driver Program that runs user code and coordinates the job, Executors that perform the actual computation and cache data, and a Cluster Manager that brokers resources between them. Understanding how these pieces interact is the foundation for debugging, tuning, and designing Spark-based systems.

3.2 The Driver — The Brain of a Spark Application

The Driver is a single JVM process that hosts the SparkSession and SparkContext. It is the control plane of every Spark application — responsible for parsing user code, building the logical plan, running the Catalyst optimizer, constructing the DAG of stages and tasks, scheduling tasks via the Task Scheduler, tracking executor health via heartbeats, and collecting results back to the user.

The Driver is a single point of failure in client mode: if the Driver process crashes or its host machine goes down, the entire application fails. In cluster mode on YARN, the ApplicationMaster provides some restart capability, but in-flight task state is lost. This is why production batch jobs always run in cluster mode where the Driver runs inside the cluster, insulated from client-side failures.

Driver memory sizing is critical: the Driver must hold the query plan graph, all broadcast variables, and any data pulled back via collect(). Default is only 1 GB — far too low for production. Tune via spark.driver.memory (typically 4–16 GB). Also set spark.driver.maxResultSize to control the maximum size of serialized results returned from collect().

⚠️ Common Pitfall Driver failure = total application failure in client mode. If you close your laptop lid during a PySpark notebook run, the Driver dies and the entire job is lost. Always use --deploy-mode cluster for production batch jobs so the Driver runs inside the cluster, independent of the client machine.

3.3 Executors — The Workhorses

Executors are long-lived JVM processes launched on worker nodes at application startup. Each executor runs for the full duration of the application (unless killed by Dynamic Allocation or OOM). They perform two functions: run tasks assigned by the Driver in thread slots, and store cached data (RDD/DataFrame partitions) via the BlockManager.

Each executor has a fixed number of CPU cores (spark.executor.cores, typically 4–5) and a fixed heap size (spark.executor.memory, typically 8–32 GB). Tasks run as threads within the executor JVM — one task per core. Executors communicate results and heartbeats back to the Driver via Netty RPC.

Dynamic Allocation (spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true) allows Spark to add executors when there's pending work and remove idle executors after a timeout. This is essential for shared clusters and cost optimization — but requires the External Shuffle Service so that shuffle data survives executor removal.

3.4 Cluster Manager — Resource Negotiation Layer

The Cluster Manager sits between the Driver and Worker Nodes, responsible for allocating compute containers, monitoring node health, and enforcing resource quotas. Spark is cluster-manager-agnostic — it plugs into multiple backends via a common interface.

3.4.1 Spark Standalone Mode

Spark's built-in cluster manager — zero external dependencies. Consists of a Master process and Worker processes on each node. Simple web UI at port 8080 for monitoring. Best for dedicated Spark-only clusters. Limited multi-tenancy and no queue-based resource isolation.

3.4.2 YARN (Yet Another Resource Negotiator)

Hadoop's resource manager — the industry standard in on-premise deployments. Architecture: ResourceManager (global), NodeManagers (per node), ApplicationMaster (per Spark app). The ApplicationMaster negotiates executor containers from the ResourceManager. Supports multi-tenancy via YARN queues and capacity/fair scheduler. Configure with spark.yarn.queue.

3.4.3 Kubernetes

Container orchestration platform — the preferred choice for cloud-native deployments. The Spark Driver runs as a K8s Pod; executors are dynamically launched as Pods. Native support since Spark 2.3, production-ready from Spark 3.1+. Benefits: fine-grained resource isolation via namespaces, auto-scaling via Cluster Autoscaler, and pod-level security via ServiceAccounts.

3.5 Worker Nodes

Worker nodes are the physical or virtual machines that host executor JVM processes. Each worker runs one or more executors depending on its resource capacity. Workers connect to shared storage (HDFS, S3, GCS) via storage connectors, and use node-local storage (SSD/HDD attached to the node) for shuffle spill files and disk-persisted RDD blocks.

In cloud environments, worker nodes are typically ephemeral compute instances (e.g., AWS EC2, GCP Compute Engine) that can be added or removed based on workload. Using spot/preemptible instances for workers (with External Shuffle Service) can reduce costs by 60–80%.

3.6 Application Master Container (YARN-specific)

On YARN, each Spark application gets an ApplicationMaster (AM) container — a special YARN container that acts as the liaison between the Spark application and the YARN ResourceManager.

  • In cluster mode: the AM hosts the Driver JVM itself. The Driver runs inside the cluster.
  • In client mode: the AM is a lightweight process that only negotiates executor containers. The Driver stays on the client machine.

The AM communicates with YARN ResourceManager to request, release, and track executor containers. It also handles container failures by requesting replacement containers.

⚙️ Engineering Insight Use client mode for interactive development (Jupyter, spark-shell) where you need to see logs in real-time. Use cluster mode for production batch jobs (Airflow, scheduled ETL) where the client can disconnect. On Kubernetes, cluster mode is always used — the Driver itself runs as a Pod.

3.7 Edge Node Pattern

An edge node (also called gateway node) is a dedicated machine for submitting Spark jobs to a cluster. It is not a worker node and not a master node — it's a client-side submission host that runs spark-submit, Jupyter notebooks, or Zeppelin.

This pattern prevents driver processes from consuming resources on head/master nodes and provides a controlled access point into the cluster. Common in enterprise Hadoop environments. The edge node has Spark client binaries, HDFS/YARN configurations, and network access to the cluster but does not run executors.

3.8 Driver Program Lifecycle

Understanding the complete lifecycle of a Spark application — from spark-submit to resource release — helps debug startup failures, hangs, and teardown issues.

🎯 Interview Tip "Walk me through what happens when you run spark-submit." — This is a classic system design interview question. The answer should cover: (1) Client contacts Cluster Manager, (2) AM container allocated, (3) Driver JVM starts with SparkSession, (4) Executor containers negotiated and launched, (5) User code parsed → Catalyst optimizes → DAG created → stages split at shuffle boundaries → tasks dispatched, (6) Results collected or written, (7) Resources released. Knowing this end-to-end flow demonstrates deep Spark understanding.
⚙️ Engineering Insight The most common production startup failures: (1) YARN queue full — app waits indefinitely for containers, (2) Docker image not found on K8s — executor pods stuck in ImagePullBackOff, (3) Driver OOM on startup — large broadcast table loaded before executors are ready. Always check Driver logs first, then Cluster Manager UI, then executor logs.

3.9 Architecture Configuration Quick Reference

ConfigDefaultRecommendedPurpose
spark.driver.memory1g4g–16gDriver JVM heap
spark.driver.maxResultSize1g2g–4gMax size of collect() result
spark.executor.memory1g8g–32gExecutor JVM heap
spark.executor.cores14–5CPU cores per executor
spark.executor.memoryOverhead10% of executor.memory2g–6gOff-heap (Python, Netty)
spark.dynamicAllocation.enabledfalsetrue (shared clusters)Auto-scale executors
spark.shuffle.service.enabledfalsetrue (with dynamic alloc)External Shuffle Service
spark.default.parallelismtotal cores2–3× total coresDefault RDD partitions
Production spark-submit exampleBash
spark-submit \
  --master yarn \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --name "ProductionETLJob" \
  --num-executors 20 \
  --executor-cores 4 \
  --executor-memory 16g \
  --driver-memory 8g \
  --conf spark.executor.memoryOverhead=4g \
  --conf spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=400 \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.minExecutors=5 \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.maxExecutors=50 \
  --conf spark.shuffle.service.enabled=true \
  --conf spark.serializer=org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer \
  --conf spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true \
  --py-files dependencies.zip \
  main_job.py \
  --input s3://bucket/input/ \
  --output s3://bucket/output/
⚠️ Common Pitfall Setting --executor-cores too high (e.g., 16) causes HDFS throughput degradation — HDFS client has a bug with more than 5 concurrent threads per JVM. Keep executor cores at 4–5 and use more executors instead. Similarly, executor memory above 32 GB leads to long GC pauses — prefer more medium-sized executors over fewer large ones.
Part 3 — Section 4

Spark Application Deployment

Local mode, client mode, cluster mode — and production-grade deployment across YARN and Kubernetes.

4 — Spark Application Deployment

4.1 Deployment Modes Overview

Spark supports three deployment modes that differ in where the Driver process runs relative to the cluster. This single decision impacts fault tolerance, log visibility, network latency, and suitability for different workloads. Choosing the wrong mode is one of the most common mistakes in production Spark setups.

4.2 Local Mode

In local mode, everything runs in a single JVM on the developer's machine. There is no cluster, no network communication, and no separate executor processes. Tasks run as threads within the same process as the Driver.

  • local — 1 thread (sequential)
  • local[N] — N threads (simulates N cores)
  • local[*] — one thread per available CPU core

Local mode is perfect for unit testing, local development, and debugging pipelines. It cannot simulate distributed failures, true shuffle behavior across nodes, or real parallelism. Never use local mode for performance benchmarks — results are meaningless compared to cluster behavior.

Local mode examplesPySpark
# Local mode — single JVM, no cluster needed
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# 1 thread — sequential execution
spark = SparkSession.builder.master("local").appName("test").getOrCreate()

# 4 threads — simulates 4 cores
spark = SparkSession.builder.master("local[4]").appName("test").getOrCreate()

# All available cores
spark = SparkSession.builder.master("local[*]").appName("test").getOrCreate()

# Useful for pytest-based unit testing
def test_my_transform():
    spark = SparkSession.builder.master("local[2]").getOrCreate()
    df = spark.createDataFrame([(1, "a"), (2, "b")], ["id", "val"])
    result = my_transform(df)
    assert result.count() == 2
    spark.stop()

4.3 Client Mode

In client mode, the Driver runs on the machine that submits the job (the client or edge node), while Executors run on cluster worker nodes. The Driver must remain alive for the entire duration of the job — if the client machine is shut down, the SSH session drops, or the network is interrupted, the entire job fails.

Client mode is ideal for interactive sessions: Jupyter notebooks, spark-shell, pyspark REPL — anywhere you need to see output and logs in real time. The trade-off is that network latency between the client and cluster affects task dispatch and result collection speed.

4.4 Cluster Mode

In cluster mode, the Driver is submitted to and runs inside the cluster — inside the ApplicationMaster container on YARN, or as a Pod on Kubernetes. The client machine that submitted the job can safely disconnect after submission.

This is the production standard for all batch ETL jobs, scheduled pipelines, and CI/CD-triggered jobs. The Driver benefits from cluster-local networking to executors (lower latency), and the application survives client-side failures. The trade-off: logs are only accessible via the Cluster Manager UI, YARN logs command, or log aggregation system — not directly in your terminal.

4.5 Deployment Mode Comparison

FeatureLocalClientCluster
Driver locationSame JVM as tasksClient machineInside cluster (AM/Pod)
Client can disconnect?NoNo — job diesYes — job continues
Log visibilityDirect stdoutDirect stdoutCM UI / log aggregation
Network latencyNone (local)Client ↔ clusterCluster-local (fast)
Fault toleranceNoneLow — client SPOFHigh — cluster managed
Use caseDev, testingNotebooks, debuggingProduction, batch, CI/CD
spark-submit flag--master local[*]--deploy-mode client--deploy-mode cluster
🎯 Interview Tip "When would you use client mode in production?" — Almost never for batch jobs. The only production use case is interactive analytics (Jupyter/Zeppelin) where data scientists need immediate output. For scheduled pipelines, always use cluster mode. On Kubernetes, cluster mode is the only mode — the Driver runs as a Pod.

4.6 spark-submit Deep Dive

spark-submit is the universal entry point for launching Spark applications. It handles packaging, dependency shipping, resource negotiation, and deployment mode selection. Understanding every flag is essential for production operations.

Production spark-submit — YARN cluster modeBash
spark-submit \
  --master yarn \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --name "DailyRevenueETL" \
  --num-executors 20 \
  --executor-cores 4 \
  --executor-memory 16g \
  --driver-memory 8g \
  --conf spark.executor.memoryOverhead=4g \
  --conf spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=400 \
  --conf spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true \
  --conf spark.serializer=org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.minExecutors=5 \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.maxExecutors=50 \
  --conf spark.shuffle.service.enabled=true \
  --conf spark.yarn.queue=production \
  --py-files utils.zip,models.zip \
  main_etl.py \
  --date "2024-01-15" \
  --output "s3://bucket/gold/revenue/"
Production spark-submit — Kubernetes cluster modeBash
spark-submit \
  --master k8s://https://k8s-api-server:443 \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --name "DailyRevenueETL" \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.container.image=registry/spark:3.5-python3.10 \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.namespace=spark-prod \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.authenticate.driver.serviceAccountName=spark-sa \
  --conf spark.executor.instances=20 \
  --conf spark.executor.cores=4 \
  --conf spark.executor.memory=16g \
  --conf spark.driver.memory=8g \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.executor.podTemplateFile=/opt/spark/executor-pod.yaml \
  --conf spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.impl=org.apache.hadoop.fs.s3a.S3AFileSystem \
  --conf spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true \
  local:///opt/spark/jobs/main_etl.py \
  --date "2024-01-15"

4.7 Standalone Cluster Setup

Spark's built-in cluster manager requires no external dependencies. It consists of a Master daemon and Worker daemons started via shell scripts.

Standalone cluster setup commandsBash
# Start Master
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-master.sh
# Web UI at http://master-host:8080

# Start Workers (run on each worker node)
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-worker.sh spark://master-host:7077

# Submit to standalone cluster
spark-submit \
  --master spark://master-host:7077 \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --executor-memory 16g \
  --total-executor-cores 40 \
  my_app.py

# Stop everything
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-all.sh

4.8 YARN Cluster Deployment

YARN deployment requires Hadoop configuration files (HADOOP_CONF_DIR) pointing to the cluster's yarn-site.xml, core-site.xml, and hdfs-site.xml. YARN manages resources through queues — each team/project is assigned a queue with CPU and memory limits, preventing one job from starving others.

YARN configuration essentialsBash
# Required: HADOOP_CONF_DIR must contain YARN config files
export HADOOP_CONF_DIR=/etc/hadoop/conf

# Submit to a specific YARN queue
spark-submit \
  --master yarn \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --conf spark.yarn.queue=data-engineering \
  --conf spark.yarn.maxAppAttempts=2 \
  --conf spark.yarn.am.memory=2g \
  main_etl.py

# View application logs after completion
yarn logs -applicationId application_1234567890_0001

# Monitor via YARN ResourceManager UI
# http://resourcemanager-host:8088

4.9 Production Cluster Design Considerations

Getting the cluster sizing right is the difference between a $500/month Spark bill and a $50,000/month one. Here are the key design decisions:

⚙️ Engineering Insight The golden executor formula: On a 16-core, 64 GB worker node, configure 3 executors × 5 cores × 19 GB each (leaving 1 core and 7 GB for OS + YARN NodeManager). This gives optimal HDFS throughput per executor (≤5 threads), manageable GC pauses (≤20 GB heap), and full node utilization. This sizing works well for 80% of batch workloads.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Co-location matters in cloud: If your Spark cluster is in us-east-1 but your S3 data is in us-west-2, every byte read crosses regions — adding 50–200ms latency per request and significant egress cost. Always deploy Spark in the same region and availability zone as your primary data source.
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you size a Spark cluster for a 10 TB daily ETL job?" — Approach: (1) Estimate compressed Parquet read at ~3:1, so ~3.3 TB raw data. (2) With 200 MB per partition target → ~16,500 tasks. (3) With 4-core executors → 4 tasks/executor → need ~4,100 executor-slots over the job. (4) If job must complete in 1 hour, and each task takes ~2 min → need ~140 executors running concurrently. (5) At 4 cores × 16 GB = ~140 × 20 GB = 2.8 TB cluster RAM. (6) Use dynamic allocation with max 150 executors, spot instances, AQE enabled. Show this kind of back-of-envelope reasoning.
Part 3 — Section 5

SparkSession

The unified entry point to every Spark application — from SparkContext to SparkSession and beyond.

5 — SparkSession

5.1 Evolution of Entry Points

In Spark 1.x, developers faced a confusing set of separate entry points: SparkContext for core RDD operations, SQLContext for DataFrame/SQL queries, and HiveContext for Hive metastore connectivity. Each had to be initialized independently, often leading to boilerplate code and confusion about which context to use where.

Spark 2.0 unified all three into a single object — SparkSession — which internally creates and manages all the underlying contexts. This was one of the most impactful API improvements in Spark's history, dramatically simplifying application bootstrapping.

5.2 SparkContext (Spark 1.x Legacy)

SparkContext was the original entry point to Spark in 1.x. It represents the connection to a Spark cluster and is used to create RDDs, accumulators, and broadcast variables. Critically, only one SparkContext per JVM is allowed — this is a hard singleton enforced by Spark.

In Spark 2.x+, SparkContext still exists under the hood but is automatically created and managed by SparkSession. You access it via spark.sparkContext when needed for low-level RDD operations or broadcast variables.

5.3 SQLContext and HiveContext (Spark 1.x Legacy)

SQLContext introduced the DataFrame API and basic SQL query support in Spark 1.3. HiveContext extended it with Hive Metastore connectivity and HiveQL support. Both required separate initialization from SparkContext, leading to code like:

Spark 1.x — ugly, verbose initialization (AVOID)PySpark (Legacy)
# Spark 1.x — DON'T do this anymore
from pyspark import SparkConf, SparkContext
from pyspark.sql import SQLContext, HiveContext

conf = SparkConf().setAppName("MyApp").setMaster("yarn")
sc = SparkContext(conf=conf)             # Step 1: Create SparkContext
sqlCtx = SQLContext(sc)                  # Step 2: Create SQLContext
hiveCtx = HiveContext(sc)                # Step 3: Create HiveContext

# Which one do I use? SQLContext or HiveContext?
# Answer: HiveContext if you need Hive tables, SQLContext otherwise
# Confusing → Spark 2.0 unified everything

5.4 SparkSession (Spark 2.x+) — The Modern Way

SparkSession is the single unified entry point for all Spark functionality since Spark 2.0. It internally creates and manages SparkContext, SQLContext, and HiveContext — you never need to touch them directly. It uses a builder pattern for clean, fluent initialization.

Key features:

  • Builder pattern: chain .appName(), .master(), .config(), .enableHiveSupport()
  • getOrCreate(): returns existing session if one already exists in the JVM — safe for notebooks and shared environments
  • Catalog API: programmatic access to databases, tables, views, functions via spark.catalog
  • Config API: read/write Spark configuration at runtime via spark.conf
  • Reader API: spark.read (batch) and spark.readStream (streaming) for data ingestion
  • UDF registration: spark.udf.register() for SQL-callable UDFs
SparkSession — Modern PySpark initializationPySpark
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("ProductionETL") \
    .master("yarn") \
    .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400") \
    .config("spark.serializer", "org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer") \
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true") \
    .enableHiveSupport() \       # Connect to Hive Metastore for table catalog
    .getOrCreate()               # Returns existing session or creates new one

# Access underlying SparkContext for RDD/broadcast operations
sc = spark.sparkContext

# Read data — DataFrameReader API
df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/events/")

# Run SQL on registered table
df.createOrReplaceTempView("events")
result = spark.sql("SELECT user_id, COUNT(*) FROM events GROUP BY user_id")

# Access catalog — list tables, databases
spark.catalog.listDatabases()
spark.catalog.listTables("default")

# Runtime config — read and write dynamically
spark.conf.get("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions")   # → "400"
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "200")
SparkSession — Scala initializationScala
import org.apache.spark.sql.SparkSession

val spark = SparkSession.builder()
  .appName("ProductionETL")
  .master("yarn")
  .config("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400")
  .config("spark.serializer", "org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer")
  .enableHiveSupport()
  .getOrCreate()

import spark.implicits._  // Enables $ column syntax and Dataset encoders

val sc = spark.sparkContext  // Access SparkContext

// Read data
val df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/events/")

// Type-safe Dataset API (Scala only)
case class Event(userId: String, eventType: String, ts: Long)
val ds: Dataset[Event] = df.as[Event]  // Compile-time type checking

5.5 SparkSession Internals

Under the hood, SparkSession is a relatively thin wrapper that wires together the heavy-lifting components. Here's what it manages internally:

  • SparkContext: The actual cluster connection. One per JVM. SparkSession delegates all job submission to it.
  • SessionState: Contains the SQL parser, analyzer, optimizer (Catalyst), planner, and the session catalog. Each SparkSession has its own SessionState, enabling isolated SQL namespaces.
  • SharedState: Shared across all SparkSessions in the same JVM — includes the global temp view catalog, Hive client, and cached file status.
  • RuntimeConfig (spark.conf): Mutable at runtime. Some configs can be changed between queries (e.g., shuffle.partitions); others are fixed at session creation (e.g., master).

5.6 Multiple Sessions in One Application

SparkSession.newSession() creates a new logical session that shares the same underlying SparkContext (and therefore the same cluster connection and executors) but has independent SQL configuration and temporary views. This is how multi-tenant notebook environments (Databricks, Jupyter Hub) isolate users.

Creating isolated sessionsPySpark
# Primary session
spark = SparkSession.builder.appName("MainApp").getOrCreate()
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400")

# Create isolated child session — shares cluster, independent config
session2 = spark.newSession()
session2.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "50")

# Verify isolation
print(spark.conf.get("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions"))    # → "400"
print(session2.conf.get("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions"))  # → "50"

# Temp views are also isolated
df = spark.range(10)
df.createOrReplaceTempView("my_table")

spark.sql("SELECT * FROM my_table").show()   # ✓ Works
# session2.sql("SELECT * FROM my_table")     # ✗ AnalysisException — not visible
⚙️ Engineering Insight In Databricks, each notebook gets its own SparkSession via newSession() but shares the same SparkContext and cluster. This means config changes in one notebook don't affect others, but all notebooks compete for the same executor resources. If one notebook hogs all executors with a massive shuffle, other notebooks' queries will queue.

5.7 SparkSession API Quick Reference

APIReturnsPurpose
spark.readDataFrameReaderRead batch data (parquet, csv, json, jdbc, delta)
spark.readStreamDataStreamReaderRead streaming data (kafka, file, delta)
spark.sql("...")DataFrameExecute SQL query against registered tables/views
spark.table("name")DataFrameLoad a table from catalog by name
spark.catalogCatalogList/manage databases, tables, views, functions
spark.confRuntimeConfigGet/set Spark configuration at runtime
spark.udf.register()—Register Python/Scala UDF for use in SQL
spark.sparkContextSparkContextAccess low-level RDD API, broadcast, accumulators
spark.streamsStreamingQueryManagerManage active streaming queries
spark.newSession()SparkSessionCreate isolated child session (shared SparkContext)
spark.stop()—Shut down session and SparkContext, release resources
spark.createDataFrame()DataFrameCreate DataFrame from Python list, pandas DF, or RDD
spark.range(n)DataFrameCreate DataFrame with single column "id" from 0 to n-1
⚠️ Common Pitfall Calling spark.stop() in a notebook kills the SparkContext for all sessions in that JVM — including other users' sessions on a shared cluster. In notebook environments, never call spark.stop() unless you own the entire cluster. On Databricks, the platform manages session lifecycle — explicit stop is unnecessary.
🎯 Interview Tip "What's the difference between SparkSession and SparkContext?" — SparkSession is the user-facing unified API (Spark 2+) that wraps SparkContext. SparkContext is the low-level cluster connection singleton that manages RDDs, broadcasts, and accumulators. In modern Spark you always start with SparkSession; SparkContext is an implementation detail you access only when needed for RDD operations or broadcast variables.
Part 4 — Section 6

Lazy Evaluation

The single most important concept in Spark — nothing runs until you ask for results.

6 — Lazy Evaluation

6.1 The Lazy Execution Model

Spark uses a lazy evaluation model: when you call a transformation like filter(), groupBy(), or select(), Spark does not execute anything immediately. Instead, it records the operation as a node in an internal logical plan (DAG). Execution is triggered only when an action is called — show(), count(), write(), collect().

This is fundamentally different from eager execution frameworks like pandas, where every operation runs immediately and materializes a full intermediate result in memory. Lazy evaluation gives Spark the opportunity to see the entire pipeline before running anything, which enables powerful optimizations that eager systems cannot perform.

6.2 Transformations (Lazy Operations)

Transformations return a new DataFrame/RDD without executing anything. They simply add a node to the logical plan. Transformations are divided into two categories based on their data dependency pattern:

  • Narrow transformations: Each output partition depends on exactly one input partition. No data moves between executors. These are pipelined within a single stage. Examples: filter, select, map, union.
  • Wide transformations: Each output partition depends on multiple input partitions — requiring a shuffle (data redistribution across the network). These create new stage boundaries. Examples: groupBy, join, repartition, sort.
TransformationTypeShuffle?Description
filter() / where()NarrowNoRow-level filtering
select() / withColumn()NarrowNoColumn projection / computation
map() / flatMap()NarrowNoElement-wise function (RDD API)
union()NarrowNoCombine two DataFrames
groupBy() + agg()WideYesGroup and aggregate
join()Wide*UsuallyCombine on key (*broadcast = no shuffle)
repartition()WideYesShuffle-based repartition
distinct()WideYesDeduplicate rows
sort() / orderBy()WideYesGlobal sort (full shuffle)
coalesce()NarrowNo*Reduce partitions (local merge, no shuffle)

6.3 Actions (Eager — Trigger Execution)

Actions force Spark to execute the entire DAG and return or persist results. Only when an action is called does the Catalyst optimizer compile the plan, the DAG Scheduler create stages, and the Task Scheduler dispatch work to executors.

ActionReturnsDescription
show(n)None (prints)Display top N rows to console
count()LongCount total rows
collect()List[Row]Return ALL rows to Driver — ⚠️ OOM risk
take(n) / head(n)List[Row]Return first N rows to Driver
first()RowReturn first row
write.save()NoneWrite results to storage (Parquet, Delta, etc.)
foreach()NoneApply function to each row (side effect)
reduce()ValueAggregate all rows with a function (RDD API)
toPandas()pandas.DataFrameCollect all data as pandas DF — ⚠️ OOM risk
⚠️ Common Pitfall collect() and toPandas() pull all data to the Driver JVM. On a 100 GB DataFrame this instantly causes Driver OOM. Always use write() for large result sets, or take(1000) / show() for samples. If you must convert to pandas, filter to a small subset first.

6.4 DAG Construction — Building the Plan

Each transformation appends a node to the internal Directed Acyclic Graph (DAG). The DAG is a tree of logical operations with edges representing data dependencies. When an action is called, Spark's planning pipeline converts this DAG through four phases: Unresolved Logical Plan → Analyzed Plan → Optimized Plan → Physical Plan, then the DAG Scheduler splits the physical plan into stages at shuffle boundaries.

6.5 How Lazy Evaluation Improves Performance

Lazy evaluation isn't just a design choice — it directly enables four critical optimizations that would be impossible in eager execution:

  • Predicate pushdown: Catalyst moves filter() conditions as close to the data source as possible. A Parquet file scan can skip entire row groups that don't match the filter — reading 10% of the data instead of 100%.
  • Projection pruning: If your final result uses only 3 of 200 columns, Spark reads only those 3 columns from Parquet. Columnar formats skip the other 197 entirely — massive I/O savings.
  • Pipeline fusion: Multiple narrow transformations (filter → map → project) are fused into a single pass over the data within one stage. No intermediate materialization.
  • Plan rewriting: Catalyst can reorder joins, simplify boolean expressions, fold constants, and eliminate redundant operations — all before any data moves.
Lazy evaluation in practice — nothing runs until .show()PySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

# Step 1: Lazy — schema inferred, no data read
df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/events/")

# Step 2: Lazy — adds filter node to DAG
df2 = df.filter("event_type = 'purchase' AND year = 2024")

# Step 3: Lazy — adds projection node
df3 = df2.select("user_id", "amount", "timestamp")

# Step 4: Lazy — adds groupBy + agg nodes
df4 = df3.groupBy("user_id").agg(
    F.sum("amount").alias("total_spend"),
    F.count("*").alias("purchase_count")
)

# NOTHING HAS EXECUTED YET — zero data read, zero CPU used
# The DAG has 4 nodes: Scan → Filter → Project → Aggregate

# Step 5: ACTION — THIS triggers the entire pipeline
df4.show(20)
# Catalyst optimizes: pushes filter into Parquet scan,
# reads only 3 columns, fuses filter+project into one pass,
# then shuffles for groupBy, aggregates, returns top 20 rows

# Inspect what Catalyst did
df4.explain("formatted")
# Look for: PushedFilters: [EqualTo(event_type, purchase), EqualTo(year, 2024)]
# Look for: ReadSchema: struct<user_id:string, amount:double, timestamp:long>
⚙️ Engineering Insight Always call df.explain("formatted") before running expensive jobs. This shows you the optimized physical plan without executing anything. Check that: (1) predicate pushdown is active (look for PushedFilters), (2) only needed columns are in the ReadSchema, (3) there are no unexpected CartesianProduct or BroadcastNestedLoopJoin operators, and (4) joins are using the expected strategy (BHJ vs SMJ).
🎯 Interview Tip "Why does Spark use lazy evaluation?" — Three reasons: (1) Optimization opportunity — Catalyst can rewrite the entire plan before execution, applying pushdown, pruning, and fusion. (2) No wasted work — if a later action only needs a subset of data, Spark reads only that subset. (3) Pipeline efficiency — narrow transformations are fused into a single pass, avoiding intermediate materialization. The key insight: lazy evaluation lets Spark optimize globally instead of being forced to execute each step locally.
Part 4 — Section 7

Spark Execution Model

From user code to running tasks — every step in Spark's query planning and execution pipeline.

7 — Spark Execution Model

7.1 End-to-End Execution Pipeline

When an action is called on a DataFrame, Spark's execution engine processes the request through a seven-step pipeline. Each step transforms the representation of the computation, progressively lowering it from a high-level logical description to physical tasks running on executor cores. Understanding this pipeline is the key to reading explain() output, debugging slow queries, and tuning performance.

7.2 Step 1 — User Code

The user writes DataFrame transformations, SQL queries, or RDD operations. This code is parsed by Spark's SQL parser (based on ANTLR4 grammar for SQL strings) or the DataFrame API layer (which constructs plan nodes directly). Both paths produce the same internal representation: an unresolved logical plan tree.

7.3 Step 2 — Unresolved Logical Plan

The initial AST (Abstract Syntax Tree) generated from user code. At this stage, column names and table references are not yet validated — they exist as string tokens. The plan tree captures the structure of the query (filter, project, aggregate, join) but knows nothing about whether the referenced tables or columns actually exist.

7.4 Step 3 — Analyzed Logical Plan (Resolution)

Spark's Analyzer walks the unresolved plan and resolves every identifier against the Catalog (Hive Metastore, in-memory catalog, or Unity Catalog). This step validates table names, column references, function signatures, and applies type coercion rules (e.g., INT + LONG → LONG). If any reference is invalid, Spark throws an AnalysisException — this is the most common error for beginners ("Column 'xyz' does not exist").

7.5 Step 4 — Optimized Logical Plan (Catalyst)

This is where the magic happens. The Catalyst Optimizer applies a battery of rule-based transformations in multiple passes until a fixed point is reached (no more rules can improve the plan). Key optimizations:

  • Predicate pushdown: Move filter conditions to the earliest possible point — ideally into the data source scan
  • Column pruning: Eliminate columns that are never referenced downstream
  • Constant folding: Evaluate constant expressions at plan time (1 + 2 → 3)
  • Boolean simplification: x AND TRUE → x, x OR FALSE → x
  • Join reordering: Rearrange multi-way joins to minimize intermediate result sizes (CBO)
  • Subquery flattening: Convert correlated subqueries to more efficient joins

The output is a logically equivalent but dramatically more efficient plan. See Section 13 for the complete Catalyst deep dive.

7.6 Step 5 — Physical Plan(s)

The Spark Planner converts the optimized logical plan into one or more concrete physical execution strategies. For example, a logical Join can become a BroadcastHashJoinExec, SortMergeJoinExec, or ShuffleHashJoinExec depending on table sizes. Multiple candidate physical plans are generated, and the Cost-Based Optimizer (CBO) selects the optimal one based on table statistics.

The selected plan is a tree of SparkPlan nodes — concrete operator implementations with codegen support. Whole-Stage Code Generation (Tungsten) fuses compatible operators into single JVM methods for maximum CPU efficiency.

7.7 Step 6 — DAG of Stages

The DAG Scheduler takes the selected physical plan and translates it into a graph of Stages. Stage boundaries are drawn at every wide dependency (shuffle) — where data must be redistributed across executors. All narrow transformations between two shuffles are fused into a single stage that executes as a pipeline.

Stage types: ShuffleMapStage writes shuffle data for downstream stages; ResultStage is the final stage that returns results or writes output. Stages form their own DAG — a parent stage must complete all shuffle writes before its child stage can begin reading.

7.8 Step 7 — Tasks

Each partition of each stage becomes exactly one task — the smallest unit of work in Spark. A task is a serialized closure (function + data partition reference) sent from the Driver to an Executor's thread pool. The Executor deserializes the task, runs it against the local data partition, and reports the result (or shuffle output metadata) back to the Driver.

Task lifecycle: Pending → Running → Completed / Failed. Failed tasks are retried up to spark.task.maxFailures=4 times. If all retries fail, the entire stage (and therefore the job) fails.

Inspecting the execution plan at each phasePySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/orders/") \
    .filter("status = 'completed' AND year = 2024") \
    .groupBy("customer_id") \
    .agg(F.sum("amount").alias("total"))

# Show ALL plan phases — parsed → analyzed → optimized → physical
df.explain("extended")

# Show only the physical plan with codegen info
df.explain("formatted")
# Look for: *(1) = WholeStageCodegen stage 1
#           *(2) = WholeStageCodegen stage 2
# *(n) prefix means operators are fused by Tungsten

# Show CBO-estimated statistics in plan
df.explain("cost")
# Shows: Statistics(sizeInBytes=2.4 GiB, rowCount=1.0E+8)

# Programmatic plan access (advanced)
logical_plan = df._jdf.queryExecution().analyzed()
optimized_plan = df._jdf.queryExecution().optimizedPlan()
physical_plan = df._jdf.queryExecution().sparkPlan()
print(optimized_plan.toString())
⚙️ Engineering Insight The explain("formatted") output is your most powerful debugging tool. A production workflow: (1) Write your query. (2) Call explain("formatted"). (3) Verify PushedFilters are active. (4) Verify ReadSchema includes only needed columns. (5) Check join strategy is BroadcastHashJoin for small dimension tables. (6) Check for unexpected CartesianProduct. (7) Only then call the action. This 30-second check prevents 30-minute failed jobs.
⚠️ Common Pitfall The number of shuffle partitions (post-shuffle tasks) defaults to spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=200. For a 1 TB dataset this means each post-shuffle task processes 5 GB — far too large, causing OOM. For a 10 MB dataset, 200 tasks means each processes 50 KB — absurdly small, wasting scheduling overhead. Always tune this: set to 400–2000 for large data, 10–50 for small data, or enable AQE (spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true) to auto-tune it at runtime.
🎯 Interview Tip "Walk me through a Spark SQL query execution." — The seven steps: (1) User writes SQL/DataFrame code. (2) Parser creates unresolved logical plan (AST). (3) Analyzer resolves names and types against catalog. (4) Catalyst optimizer applies predicate pushdown, column pruning, constant folding, join reordering. (5) Spark Planner generates physical plan candidates; CBO picks the cheapest. (6) DAG Scheduler splits the physical plan into stages at shuffle boundaries. (7) Task Scheduler dispatches tasks (one per partition) to executor threads with locality preferences. Being able to explain this pipeline — and relate each step to df.explain("extended") output — demonstrates senior-level Spark understanding.
Part 4 — Section 8

Spark DAG Scheduler

How Spark splits a physical plan into stages at shuffle boundaries and manages stage dependencies.

8 — Spark DAG Scheduler

8.1 Role of the DAG Scheduler

The DAG Scheduler is a Driver-side component that sits between the Catalyst/Planner output and the Task Scheduler. Its job is to translate the selected physical plan into a graph of stages — determining which operations can be pipelined together and where stage boundaries (shuffles) must be inserted.

Specifically, the DAG Scheduler:

  • Walks the physical plan tree backwards from the final output, identifying shuffle dependencies
  • Groups narrow transformations between shuffles into single stages
  • Computes stage dependencies: which parent stages must complete before a child stage can start
  • Submits stages to the Task Scheduler in topological order (parents first)
  • Handles stage failures: re-submits failed stages (and their parents if shuffle outputs are lost)
  • Tracks shuffle output locations via the MapOutputTracker

8.2 Stage Creation — Shuffle Boundaries

The core algorithm of the DAG Scheduler is stage splitting. The rule is simple but fundamental: every time the DAG Scheduler encounters a ShuffleDependency (wide dependency) while walking the plan backwards, it creates a new stage boundary. All narrow transformations between two shuffle points are grouped into a single stage.

This produces two types of stages:

  • ShuffleMapStage: Reads input data (from source or previous shuffle), applies narrow transforms, and writes shuffle output files to local disk. Each task produces one shuffle file per downstream partition.
  • ResultStage: The final stage in the job. Reads from the last shuffle, applies remaining transforms, and returns results to the Driver or writes to storage.

8.3 How Stages Are Split — The Algorithm

The DAG Scheduler uses a backwards walk algorithm to split the physical plan into stages:

  1. Start from the final RDD/DataFrame operation (the action's target)
  2. Walk backwards through the dependency graph
  3. At each node, check the dependency type:
    • NarrowDependency (1:1 partition mapping) → keep in the current stage, continue walking
    • ShuffleDependency (N:N partition mapping) → create a new stage boundary. The current stage becomes the child; the parent operations form a new parent stage.
  4. Repeat until all operations are assigned to stages
  5. Submit stages bottom-up: submit leaf stages (no parent dependencies) first, then child stages as parents complete

8.4 Stage Dependencies

Stages themselves form a DAG of dependencies — a "stage DAG" layered on top of the operation DAG. A child stage cannot begin execution until all parent stages have completed their shuffle map output (all partition files are written and registered with the MapOutputTracker).

Key implications:

  • Independent parent stages run in parallel. In a join between two tables, the scan stages for both tables can execute simultaneously because they have no dependency on each other.
  • Stage failure recovery is scoped. If a stage fails (e.g., executor OOM during shuffle write), only that stage is re-executed — not the entire job. However, if a parent stage's shuffle output has been lost (executor crash after completing), the parent stage must be re-run to regenerate the shuffle files.
  • Skipped stages appear in the Spark UI when cached/persisted data makes recomputation unnecessary.

8.5 Task Parallelism per Stage

The number of tasks in a stage is determined by the number of input partitions for that stage:

  • Source scan stages: tasks = number of files or HDFS blocks (data-driven). A Parquet dataset with 500 files → 500 tasks.
  • Post-shuffle stages: tasks = spark.sql.shuffle.partitions (default 200). This is the most impactful performance config in Spark.
⚙️ Engineering Insight The default spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=200 is the single most common performance bottleneck in production Spark jobs. For a 1 TB dataset, 200 partitions means each task processes 5 GB — far too large, causing OOM and long GC pauses. For a 50 MB dataset, 200 partitions means 250 KB per task — absurd overhead. Set it explicitly based on your data size, or enable AQE (spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true) which auto-coalesces shuffle partitions at runtime based on actual data sizes.
Tuning shuffle partitionsPySpark
# Option 1: Manual tuning — set based on data size
# Rule of thumb: target 128-256 MB per partition
# 1 TB dataset → 1024 GB / 200 MB = ~5000 partitions
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "5000")

# Option 2: Let AQE auto-tune (recommended for Spark 3.0+)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes", "128m")
# AQE starts with 200 then merges small partitions → target ~128 MB each

# Check actual partition count after execution
df_result = df.groupBy("key").count()
df_result.write.parquet("output/")
# Spark UI → Stages tab → Stage N → shows actual tasks completed
⚠️ Common Pitfall When a stage fails and the DAG Scheduler re-submits it, any parent stage's shuffle output that was stored on a now-dead executor must also be recomputed. This means a single executor crash can cascade into re-running 2 or 3 stages. The fix: enable the External Shuffle Service (spark.shuffle.service.enabled=true) so shuffle files are served by a persistent process that survives executor failures.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does the DAG Scheduler decide where to split stages?" — Answer: It walks the physical plan tree backwards from the action. At each node it checks the dependency type. NarrowDependency (1:1 partition mapping) stays in the current stage. ShuffleDependency (all-to-all) creates a new stage boundary. All narrow operations between two shuffles are fused into one stage. Independent parent stages (e.g., scan of two tables for a join) run in parallel. The key formula: number of stages = number of shuffles + 1.
Part 4 — Section 9

Spark Task Scheduler

Locality-aware task assignment, scheduling modes, speculative execution, and the TaskSetManager.

9 — Spark Task Scheduler

9.1 Role of the Task Scheduler

The Task Scheduler is the last step in the Driver's scheduling pipeline. It receives TaskSets (one per stage) from the DAG Scheduler and is responsible for assigning individual tasks to available executor core slots, tracking their completion, retrying failures, and enforcing data locality preferences.

While the DAG Scheduler thinks in terms of stages and dependencies, the Task Scheduler thinks in terms of individual tasks and executor slots. It is the bridge between logical scheduling and physical execution.

9.2 TaskSet Manager

The TaskSetManager is created for each stage and manages all tasks within that stage. It maintains the state machine for every task:

  • Pending: Task is queued, waiting for a free executor slot with acceptable locality
  • Running: Task dispatched to an executor thread, actively processing
  • Completed: Task finished successfully, results reported to Driver
  • Failed: Task threw an exception or executor crashed → retry scheduled (up to spark.task.maxFailures=4)

The TaskSetManager implements locality-aware scheduling — it knows which executors/nodes hold each task's preferred data and waits briefly for a local slot before falling back to a less-local one. It also implements speculative execution for straggler detection.

9.3 Locality-Aware Scheduling

Spark's Task Scheduler prefers to launch tasks on executors/nodes where the task's input data already resides. This minimizes network transfer — the most expensive operation in distributed computing. Spark defines five locality levels, tried in order from best to worst:

The locality wait mechanism works as follows: when a task is ready to launch, the scheduler first checks if any executor slot is available at the best locality level. If not, it waits for spark.locality.wait seconds (default 3s). If still no slot, it relaxes to the next level and waits again. This cascades until the task is placed. You can tune per-level waits with spark.locality.wait.process, spark.locality.wait.node, and spark.locality.wait.rack.

9.4 Scheduling Modes — FIFO vs FAIR

When multiple Spark jobs (actions) are submitted within the same application, the scheduling mode determines how resources are shared between them:

FAIR scheduling configurationPySpark + XML
# Enable FAIR scheduling
spark.conf.set("spark.scheduler.mode", "FAIR")

# Assign jobs to specific pools with different priorities
spark.sparkContext.setLocalProperty("spark.scheduler.pool", "high_priority")
df_critical.write.parquet("output/critical/")

spark.sparkContext.setLocalProperty("spark.scheduler.pool", "low_priority")
df_batch.write.parquet("output/batch/")

# fairscheduler.xml — define pool weights
# Place in classpath or set spark.scheduler.allocation.file
# <allocations>
#   <pool name="high_priority">
#     <schedulingMode>FAIR</schedulingMode>
#     <weight>3</weight>         <!-- 3× resources -->
#     <minShare>4</minShare>     <!-- at least 4 cores -->
#   </pool>
#   <pool name="low_priority">
#     <schedulingMode>FAIR</schedulingMode>
#     <weight>1</weight>         <!-- 1× resources -->
#     <minShare>1</minShare>
#   </pool>
# </allocations>

9.5 Speculative Execution

Speculative execution (also called "backup tasks") is Spark's defense against straggler tasks — tasks that are running significantly slower than their peers due to hardware degradation, noisy neighbors in cloud VMs, or data skew. When enabled, Spark monitors running tasks and launches a duplicate copy of any task that exceeds the median duration by a configurable multiplier. The first copy to finish wins; the other is killed.

⚠️ Common Pitfall Speculative execution can cause duplicate writes for non-idempotent sinks. If Task 3 and its speculative copy Task 3' both write to the same output file/partition, you get corrupted or duplicate data. Only use speculation with idempotent sinks (Delta Lake, Iceberg which use atomic commits) or operations that are purely in-memory (aggregations, shuffles). For file-based sinks like raw Parquet on S3, Spark's commit protocol normally handles this, but edge cases exist.
ConfigDefaultDescription
spark.speculationfalseEnable/disable speculative execution
spark.speculation.multiplier1.5Launch speculative copy when task exceeds median × this
spark.speculation.quantile0.75Start checking after this fraction of tasks complete
spark.speculation.minTaskRuntime100msDon't speculate tasks shorter than this
spark.task.maxFailures4Max retries before task (and stage) fails
spark.scheduler.modeFIFOInter-job scheduling: FIFO or FAIR
spark.locality.wait3sWait time before relaxing locality level
spark.locality.wait.processspark.locality.waitWait time for PROCESS_LOCAL specifically
spark.locality.wait.nodespark.locality.waitWait time for NODE_LOCAL specifically
spark.locality.wait.rackspark.locality.waitWait time for RACK_LOCAL specifically
⚙️ Engineering Insight Cloud S3/GCS clusters: Set spark.locality.wait=0s because there is no data locality benefit — all reads go over the network to object storage regardless of which executor runs the task. Waiting 3 seconds per task per locality level wastes time. On HDFS-backed on-premise clusters, keep the default 3s to benefit from block locality.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Spark handle straggler tasks?" — Two mechanisms: (1) Speculative execution — launches duplicate tasks when one is running 1.5× slower than median. First to finish wins. Enable with spark.speculation=true. (2) Task retry — if a task fails (OOM, executor crash), it's retried up to spark.task.maxFailures=4 times, potentially on a different executor. For chronic stragglers caused by data skew (not hardware), speculation doesn't help — you need AQE skew join or manual salting (Section 21).
Part 4 — Section 9

Spark Task Scheduler

Locality-aware task assignment, scheduling modes, speculative execution, and the TaskSetManager.

9 — Spark Task Scheduler

9.1 Role of the Task Scheduler

The Task Scheduler is the last step in the Driver's scheduling pipeline. It receives TaskSets (one per stage) from the DAG Scheduler and is responsible for assigning individual tasks to available executor core slots, tracking their completion, retrying failures, and enforcing data locality preferences.

While the DAG Scheduler thinks in terms of stages and dependencies, the Task Scheduler thinks in terms of individual tasks and executor slots. It is the bridge between logical scheduling and physical execution.

9.2 TaskSet Manager

The TaskSetManager is created for each stage and manages all tasks within that stage. It maintains the state machine for every task:

  • Pending: Task is queued, waiting for a free executor slot with acceptable locality
  • Running: Task dispatched to an executor thread, actively processing
  • Completed: Task finished successfully, results reported to Driver
  • Failed: Task threw an exception or executor crashed → retry scheduled (up to spark.task.maxFailures=4)

The TaskSetManager implements locality-aware scheduling — it knows which executors/nodes hold each task's preferred data and waits briefly for a local slot before falling back to a less-local one. It also implements speculative execution for straggler detection.

9.3 Locality-Aware Scheduling

Spark's Task Scheduler prefers to launch tasks on executors/nodes where the task's input data already resides. This minimizes network transfer — the most expensive operation in distributed computing. Spark defines five locality levels, tried in order from best to worst:

The locality wait mechanism works as follows: when a task is ready to launch, the scheduler first checks if any executor slot is available at the best locality level. If not, it waits for spark.locality.wait seconds (default 3s). If still no slot, it relaxes to the next level and waits again. This cascades until the task is placed. You can tune per-level waits with spark.locality.wait.process, spark.locality.wait.node, and spark.locality.wait.rack.

9.4 Scheduling Modes — FIFO vs FAIR

When multiple Spark jobs (actions) are submitted within the same application, the scheduling mode determines how resources are shared between them:

FAIR scheduling configurationPySpark + XML
# Enable FAIR scheduling
spark.conf.set("spark.scheduler.mode", "FAIR")

# Assign jobs to specific pools with different priorities
spark.sparkContext.setLocalProperty("spark.scheduler.pool", "high_priority")
df_critical.write.parquet("output/critical/")

spark.sparkContext.setLocalProperty("spark.scheduler.pool", "low_priority")
df_batch.write.parquet("output/batch/")

# fairscheduler.xml — define pool weights
# Place in classpath or set spark.scheduler.allocation.file
# <allocations>
#   <pool name="high_priority">
#     <schedulingMode>FAIR</schedulingMode>
#     <weight>3</weight>         <!-- 3× resources -->
#     <minShare>4</minShare>     <!-- at least 4 cores -->
#   </pool>
#   <pool name="low_priority">
#     <schedulingMode>FAIR</schedulingMode>
#     <weight>1</weight>         <!-- 1× resources -->
#     <minShare>1</minShare>
#   </pool>
# </allocations>

9.5 Speculative Execution

Speculative execution (also called "backup tasks") is Spark's defense against straggler tasks — tasks that are running significantly slower than their peers due to hardware degradation, noisy neighbors in cloud VMs, or data skew. When enabled, Spark monitors running tasks and launches a duplicate copy of any task that exceeds the median duration by a configurable multiplier. The first copy to finish wins; the other is killed.

⚠️ Common Pitfall Speculative execution can cause duplicate writes for non-idempotent sinks. If Task 3 and its speculative copy Task 3' both write to the same output file/partition, you get corrupted or duplicate data. Only use speculation with idempotent sinks (Delta Lake, Iceberg which use atomic commits) or operations that are purely in-memory (aggregations, shuffles). For file-based sinks like raw Parquet on S3, Spark's commit protocol normally handles this, but edge cases exist.
ConfigDefaultDescription
spark.speculationfalseEnable/disable speculative execution
spark.speculation.multiplier1.5Launch speculative copy when task exceeds median × this
spark.speculation.quantile0.75Start checking after this fraction of tasks complete
spark.speculation.minTaskRuntime100msDon't speculate tasks shorter than this
spark.task.maxFailures4Max retries before task (and stage) fails
spark.scheduler.modeFIFOInter-job scheduling: FIFO or FAIR
spark.locality.wait3sWait time before relaxing locality level
spark.locality.wait.processspark.locality.waitWait time for PROCESS_LOCAL specifically
spark.locality.wait.nodespark.locality.waitWait time for NODE_LOCAL specifically
spark.locality.wait.rackspark.locality.waitWait time for RACK_LOCAL specifically
⚙️ Engineering Insight Cloud S3/GCS clusters: Set spark.locality.wait=0s because there is no data locality benefit — all reads go over the network to object storage regardless of which executor runs the task. Waiting 3 seconds per task per locality level wastes time. On HDFS-backed on-premise clusters, keep the default 3s to benefit from block locality.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Spark handle straggler tasks?" — Two mechanisms: (1) Speculative execution — launches duplicate tasks when one is running 1.5× slower than median. First to finish wins. Enable with spark.speculation=true. (2) Task retry — if a task fails (OOM, executor crash), it's retried up to spark.task.maxFailures=4 times, potentially on a different executor. For chronic stragglers caused by data skew (not hardware), speculation doesn't help — you need AQE skew join or manual salting (Section 21).
Part 5 — Section 10

RDD Internals

The foundational abstraction — Resilient Distributed Datasets, their five properties, lineage graph, and fault tolerance model.

10 — RDD Internals

10.1 What Is an RDD?

The Resilient Distributed Dataset (RDD) is the foundational abstraction in Apache Spark — the primitive upon which everything else is built. Even DataFrames and Datasets compile down to RDD operations under the hood. Understanding RDDs is essential for debugging, low-level optimization, and comprehending Spark's fault tolerance model.

An RDD has three defining characteristics:

  • Resilient: Fault-tolerant via a lineage graph — if a partition is lost (executor crash), Spark can recompute it by replaying the chain of transformations that produced it from the original source data.
  • Distributed: Data is split into partitions that are spread across executor JVMs on multiple nodes. Each partition is processed independently and in parallel.
  • Immutable: Once created, an RDD cannot be modified. Every transformation produces a new RDD — this immutability is what makes the lineage graph reliable for recomputation.

RDDs are typed at runtime only (no compile-time schema) and operate on opaque JVM objects. This means Spark cannot optimize RDD operations the way Catalyst optimizes DataFrames — no predicate pushdown, no column pruning, no code generation. This is why the DataFrame/Dataset API is strongly preferred in modern Spark.

10.2 RDD Internal Properties — The Five Pillars

Every RDD in Spark carries exactly five defining properties. These are the interface that Spark's scheduler uses to plan and execute operations. Understanding these properties is key to understanding how Spark works at the lowest level.

#PropertyReturnsPurposeExample
1getPartitions()Array[Partition]Defines how data is splitHadoopRDD: 1 partition per HDFS block
2compute(p, ctx)Iterator[T]Produces data for a partitionFilteredRDD: iterates parent, applies filter
3getDependencies()Seq[Dependency]Links to parent RDDsNarrowDependency or ShuffleDependency
4partitionerOption[Partitioner]Key distribution strategyHashPartitioner(200) after groupBy
5preferredLocations(p)Seq[String]Data locality hints["node1.cluster", "node3.cluster"]

10.3 RDD Lineage Graph
