The lineage graph is a DAG of RDD transformations that records how each RDD was derived from its parents. This is Spark's primary fault tolerance mechanism — instead of replicating data, Spark stores the recipe to recreate any lost partition by replaying the lineage from the original source data.

Each RDD node in the lineage stores: its parent RDD reference, the transformation function, and the dependency type (narrow or wide). The lineage graph is built lazily during transformation calls and traversed during execution (on action) and recovery (on partition loss).

10.4 Narrow vs Wide Dependencies — Deep Dive

The dependency type between parent and child RDD determines whether data must cross the network (shuffle) and where stage boundaries are drawn. This is the single most important concept in Spark's execution model.

PropertyNarrowWide (Shuffle)
Partition mapping1 parent → 1 childN parents → 1 child
Shuffle requiredNoYes — disk + network
Stage boundaryNo — same stageYes — new stage
PipeliningYes — fused into single passNo — must materialize first
Fault recovery scopeRecompute single partitionRecompute entire parent stage
Examplesmap, filter, flatMap, union, coalescegroupByKey, reduceByKey, join, repartition, sort

10.5 Fault Tolerance via Lineage

When a partition is lost (executor crash, OOM, preempted spot instance), Spark does not need a backup copy. Instead, the DAG Scheduler looks at the lineage of the lost partition and re-executes only the transformations needed to reconstruct it from the last available data (either the source or a cached/checkpointed intermediate).

For narrow dependencies, only the single lost partition needs recomputation — its parent partition is read and the transformation is re-applied. For wide dependencies (shuffle), the situation is worse: the lost partition depends on data from all parent partitions, so the entire parent stage may need to be re-run if shuffle outputs are lost.

Checkpointing truncates the lineage by materializing the RDD to stable storage (HDFS/S3). This creates a new starting point for recovery, preventing expensive full lineage replays on very deep DAGs (common in iterative ML algorithms).

RDD API — lineage, checkpointing, and debugPySpark
# Create RDD chain
rdd = sc.textFile("s3://bucket/logs/")                # HadoopRDD
rdd2 = rdd.filter(lambda x: "ERROR" in x)              # FilteredRDD (narrow)
rdd3 = rdd2.map(lambda x: (x.split()[0], 1))           # MappedRDD (narrow)
rdd4 = rdd3.reduceByKey(lambda a, b: a + b)            # ShuffledRDD (WIDE)

# View the lineage graph (debug string)
print(rdd4.toDebugString().decode())
# Output:
# (4) PythonRDD[4] at reduceByKey
# |   MapPartitionsRDD[3] at reduceByKey
# |   ShuffledRDD[2] at reduceByKey
# +-- (4) PythonRDD[1] at map
#     |   s3://bucket/logs/ HadoopRDD[0]

# Checkpoint — truncate lineage for long chains
sc.setCheckpointDir("s3://bucket/checkpoints/")
rdd4.checkpoint()    # Mark for checkpointing
rdd4.count()         # Action triggers checkpoint write
# Now rdd4's lineage starts from the checkpoint, not from HadoopRDD

# Check partition count
print(rdd4.getNumPartitions())  # e.g., 200 (default shuffle partitions)

# Trigger action to execute
result = rdd4.collect()  # ⚠ Pulls all data to driver — use take() for samples
⚙️ Engineering Insight Prefer DataFrame/Dataset API over raw RDDs in Spark 2+. DataFrames go through Catalyst optimizer (predicate pushdown, column pruning, join reordering) and Tungsten codegen (binary format, CPU-efficient loops). RDDs bypass all of this — every operation is an opaque lambda that Spark cannot inspect or optimize. Use RDDs only when you need: (1) unstructured data without a schema, (2) fine-grained control over partitioning, or (3) custom serialization that doesn't fit the Row model.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Long lineage chains in iterative algorithms (ML gradient descent with 100 iterations) can cause StackOverflowError during scheduling or extremely slow recovery if any partition is lost (replays 100 transforms). Always checkpoint() every 10–20 iterations to truncate the lineage. For DataFrames, use df.checkpoint() or cache with DISK_ONLY storage level.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Spark achieve fault tolerance without data replication?" — Through RDD lineage. Every RDD stores its parent RDD and the transformation that created it. When a partition is lost, Spark walks the lineage backwards to the source data (or last checkpoint) and re-executes only the transformations needed for that partition. For narrow dependencies this is O(1) partition; for wide dependencies, the entire parent stage may need re-execution. Checkpointing truncates the lineage for long chains. This "recompute vs replicate" design trades CPU time for storage cost — the right trade-off for batch analytics where data is already replicated at the source (HDFS 3× replication, S3 11 nines durability).
Part 5 — Section 11

DataFrames and Datasets

The structured APIs that 95% of production Spark code uses — RDD vs DataFrame vs Dataset, and when to use each.

11 — DataFrames and Datasets

11.1 The Three Structured APIs

Spark offers three levels of abstraction for data processing, each trading off control for optimization. Understanding the differences and choosing the right API is one of the most impactful decisions in Spark development.

11.2 RDD (Spark 1.x — Low Level)

The RDD is a distributed collection of opaque JVM objects. Spark cannot see inside the objects — it treats them as black boxes. This means no predicate pushdown, no column pruning, no whole-stage codegen, and no cost-based optimization. Every transformation is a user-supplied lambda function that Spark must execute blindly.

RDDs are still the foundation layer: DataFrames and Datasets compile down to RDD operations internally. But as a user-facing API, RDDs should only be used when:

  • Data is unstructured and has no schema (raw binary, complex nested objects)
  • You need fine-grained control over physical partitioning (partitionBy with custom Partitioner)
  • You need custom serialization that doesn't fit the Row/Encoder model
  • You're working with legacy Spark 1.x code that hasn't been migrated

11.3 DataFrame (Spark 2.x — High Level, Recommended)

A DataFrame is a distributed collection of Row objects organized into named, typed columns — like a SQL table or a pandas DataFrame, but distributed across a cluster. It is the most widely used API in production Spark code (95%+ of workloads).

DataFrames go through the Catalyst optimizer — Spark sees the schema, understands column types, and can apply predicate pushdown, column pruning, join reordering, constant folding, and dozens of other optimizations automatically. They also benefit from Tungsten — compact binary memory format with whole-stage code generation that eliminates JVM object overhead and virtual function calls.

Critically, DataFrame performance is language-agnostic: Python, Scala, Java, and R all produce the same optimized physical plan. A PySpark DataFrame query runs at the same speed as an identical Scala DataFrame query because the optimizer and execution engine are the same JVM code — only the plan construction happens in the host language.

DataFrame operations — PySparkPySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

# Read structured data
df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/sales/")

# Schema is known — Spark can optimize!
df.printSchema()
# root
#  |-- order_id: string
#  |-- customer_id: string
#  |-- product: string
#  |-- amount: double
#  |-- region: string
#  |-- year: integer

# Transformations — Catalyst optimizes the entire chain
result = df \
    .filter(F.col("year") == 2024) \
    .filter(F.col("region").isin("US", "EU")) \
    .groupBy("region", "product") \
    .agg(
        F.sum("amount").alias("total_revenue"),
        F.count("*").alias("order_count"),
        F.avg("amount").alias("avg_order_value")
    ) \
    .orderBy(F.desc("total_revenue"))

# Catalyst will:
# 1. Push year=2024 filter into Parquet scan (partition pruning)
# 2. Read only 4 columns (region, product, amount, year) — skip order_id, customer_id
# 3. Combine the two filter() calls into one
# 4. Use HashAggregate with codegen for fast aggregation

result.show(20, truncate=False)
result.explain("formatted")  # Verify optimizations applied

11.4 Dataset (Spark 2.x — Type-Safe, Scala/Java Only)

A Dataset is an extension of DataFrame that adds compile-time type safety. Instead of working with generic Row objects, you map data to user-defined JVM classes (case classes in Scala, POJOs in Java). The compiler catches type errors before the job runs — preventing runtime failures from typos or type mismatches.

Datasets use Encoders to convert JVM objects to/from Spark's internal binary format (Tungsten). This means they still get Catalyst optimization and Tungsten codegen — the best of both worlds: type safety + performance. However, Datasets are only available in Scala and Java — PySpark has no compile-time type system, so it only supports DataFrames.

In Scala, DataFrame is literally Dataset[Row] — a Dataset of generic Row objects. A typed Dataset like Dataset[Order] is the same underlying engine with an additional type parameter that the Scala compiler checks.

Dataset[T] — Scala type-safe exampleScala
// Define a typed case class — represents one row
case class Order(
  orderId: String,
  customerId: String,
  product: String,
  amount: Double,
  status: String,
  year: Int
)

// Read as DataFrame, then convert to typed Dataset
val ordersDS: Dataset[Order] = spark.read
  .parquet("s3://bucket/orders/")
  .as[Order]  // Convert Row → Order using Encoder

// Type-safe operations — compiler catches errors!
val highValue: Dataset[Order] = ordersDS
  .filter(_.amount > 1000.0)       // ✓ Compile-time check: amount is Double
  .filter(_.status == "completed")   // ✓ Compile-time check: status is String

// This would FAIL at compile time (not runtime!):
// ordersDS.filter(_.amout > 1000)   // ✗ typo: "amout" — compiler error
// ordersDS.filter(_.amount > "big") // ✗ type mismatch: comparing Double to String

// Map to a new typed Dataset
case class OrderSummary(customerId: String, totalSpend: Double)

val summaryDS: Dataset[OrderSummary] = ordersDS
  .groupByKey(_.customerId)
  .mapGroups { case (custId, orders) =>
    OrderSummary(custId, orders.map(_.amount).sum)
  }

summaryDS.show()

11.5 API Comparison — RDD vs DataFrame vs Dataset

FeatureRDDDataFrameDataset
Type safetyRuntime onlyRuntime onlyCompile-time ✓
Schema enforcementNoneYes (named typed columns)Yes (JVM class mapping)
Catalyst optimizer✗ Bypassed✓ Full optimization✓ Full optimization
Tungsten codegen✗ Bypassed✓ Binary format + WSCG✓ Binary format + WSCG
Language supportAll (Python, Scala, Java, R)AllScala + Java only
PerformanceSlowest (opaque objects)Fastest (optimized plan)Fastest (same as DataFrame)
Error detectionRuntime AnalysisExceptionRuntime AnalysisExceptionCompile-time type errors
API styleFunctional (map/filter/reduce)Declarative (SQL-like)Both functional + declarative
SerializationJava/Kryo (heavy)Tungsten binary (light)Encoder → Tungsten (light)
Best forUnstructured data, legacyGeneral purpose (95% of work)Type-safe Scala pipelines
⚙️ Engineering Insight PySpark RDD performance is 2× slower than Scala RDD because each record must be serialized from JVM → Python process → back to JVM (via Py4J). But PySpark DataFrame is equal to Scala DataFrame because the DataFrame API only sends the plan description to the JVM — all data processing happens in the JVM's Catalyst/Tungsten engine. PySpark overhead only appears when using Python UDFs (row-at-a-time serialization). For UDFs, always use pandas UDFs (Arrow batches) which are 10–100× faster than row UDFs.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Typed Dataset lambda operations can be slower than DataFrame column expressions. When you write ds.filter(_.amount > 1000), the lambda function is opaque to Catalyst — it can't push the filter into the Parquet scan. But df.filter(col("amount") > 1000) is a structured expression that Catalyst can push down. For maximum performance, use DataFrame column expressions even in Scala. Reserve typed lambdas for complex logic that can't be expressed with built-in functions.
🎯 Interview Tip "When would you use RDD vs DataFrame vs Dataset?" — DataFrame for 95% of work: ETL, SQL analytics, any Python code. Dataset for Scala/Java pipelines where compile-time type safety prevents runtime errors in production (e.g., typed case class pipelines in a CI/CD environment). RDD only for unstructured data without a schema, custom partitioning, or legacy code. The key insight: DataFrame and Dataset have identical runtime performance — the difference is entirely about compile-time safety. RDDs are 3–6× slower because they bypass Catalyst and Tungsten.
Converting between APIsPySpark + Scala
# PySpark: DataFrame ↔ RDD
df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/data/")

# DataFrame → RDD (lose optimization!)
rdd = df.rdd                              # RDD[Row]
rdd_values = rdd.map(lambda r: r["amount"])  # RDD[float] — opaque to Catalyst

# RDD → DataFrame (regain optimization)
from pyspark.sql.types import *
schema = StructType([
    StructField("name", StringType()),
    StructField("age", IntegerType())
])
df_back = spark.createDataFrame(rdd_of_rows, schema)

# PySpark: DataFrame ↔ pandas
pandas_df = df.toPandas()                 # ⚠ Collects ALL data to driver
df_from_pandas = spark.createDataFrame(pandas_df)  # pandas → Spark DataFrame

# Scala: DataFrame ↔ Dataset
// val df: DataFrame = spark.read.parquet("...")
// val ds: Dataset[Order] = df.as[Order]     // DataFrame → Dataset
// val dfBack: DataFrame = ds.toDF()          // Dataset → DataFrame
Part 6 — Section 12

Spark SQL Engine

The full SQL execution pipeline — from SQL string to optimized bytecode running on executors.

12 — Spark SQL Engine

12.1 SQL Execution Pipeline

Spark SQL is not just a SQL interface — it is a complete query execution engine that processes both SQL strings and DataFrame API calls through the same optimization and execution pipeline. Whether you write spark.sql("SELECT ...") or df.filter().groupBy().agg(), the code enters the same six-phase pipeline and emerges as optimized JVM bytecode running on distributed executors.

This architecture is what makes Spark SQL so powerful: every query, regardless of input language, gets the same world-class optimization. Understanding each phase is essential for debugging query plans, identifying performance bottlenecks, and writing queries that the optimizer can effectively improve.

12.2 SQL Parser

Spark's SQL parser accepts ANSI SQL, HiveQL, and Spark SQL extensions. It's built on the ANTLR4 parser generator framework, which converts the SQL grammar into a parse tree, then into an unresolved logical plan (AST). At this stage, column names and table references are raw string tokens — nothing is validated yet.

The DataFrame API bypasses the SQL text parser entirely — each DataFrame method (filter(), groupBy(), etc.) directly constructs the corresponding logical plan node. This is why SQL strings and DataFrame calls produce identical plans downstream — both feed into the Analyzer.

12.3 Analyzer

The Analyzer takes the unresolved logical plan and resolves every identifier against the Catalog — the metadata repository for databases, tables, views, and functions. It validates that referenced tables exist, column names are correct, function signatures match, and data types are compatible. It applies type coercion rules (e.g., INT + LONG → LONG, STRING + INT comparison → implicit CAST).

If any reference is invalid, the Analyzer throws an AnalysisException — this is the most common error for Spark beginners and typically appears as "cannot resolve column name" or "table not found".

12.4 Catalog

The Catalog is Spark's central metadata repository. It stores information about databases, tables (managed and external), views, columns (names, types, statistics), partitions, and registered functions (built-in + UDFs). Spark supports two catalog backends:

  • In-memory catalog (default): Tables exist only during the SparkSession lifetime. Created via createOrReplaceTempView() or saveAsTable() with local metastore.
  • Hive Metastore (external): Persistent catalog backed by a relational database (MySQL, PostgreSQL). Tables survive across sessions and clusters. Enabled via .enableHiveSupport(). Also works with AWS Glue Data Catalog, Databricks Unity Catalog, and Apache Iceberg REST catalog.

12.5 Catalyst Optimizer

The Catalyst Optimizer is the heart of Spark SQL — a sophisticated rule-based and cost-based query optimizer that rewrites the logical plan into a more efficient equivalent. It applies over 100 optimization rules in multiple passes until a fixed point is reached (no more improvements possible). Full deep-dive in Section 13.

Key optimizations: predicate pushdown, column pruning, constant folding, boolean simplification, null propagation, subquery flattening, join reordering (CBO), and limit pushdown.

12.6 Physical Planner

The Physical Planner (via SparkStrategies) converts the optimized logical plan into one or more concrete physical execution plans. Each logical operator maps to one or more physical operator implementations:

Logical OperatorPhysical Operator CandidatesSelection Criteria
JoinBroadcastHashJoinExec, SortMergeJoinExec, ShuffleHashJoinExec, BroadcastNestedLoopJoinExecTable sizes, join type, hints, CBO stats
AggregateHashAggregateExec, SortAggregateExec, ObjectHashAggregateExecData types, UDF presence, memory
SortSortExec (with or without global ordering)Full vs partial sort requirement
FilterFilterExec (with pushdown to scan)Expression type, data source support
ScanFileSourceScanExec, InMemoryTableScanExec, DataSourceV2ScanExecSource type, cache status, connector
⚙️ Engineering Insight When the CBO has table statistics (from ANALYZE TABLE ... COMPUTE STATISTICS), it estimates the size of each join side and selects the optimal strategy. Without statistics, the planner falls back to heuristics — often choosing the slower SortMergeJoin when a faster BroadcastHashJoin would be possible. Always run ANALYZE TABLE on your key tables, especially dimension tables in star schemas.

12.7 Code Generation (Tungsten)

Whole-Stage Code Generation (WSCG) is Tungsten's flagship optimization. Instead of interpreting operators one row at a time through a chain of virtual function calls (the "volcano model"), Tungsten generates JVM bytecode that fuses all operators within a stage into a single tight loop. This eliminates virtual dispatch overhead, enables CPU pipeline optimization, and keeps data in CPU registers/cache instead of bouncing between operator objects on the heap.

In the physical plan output (explain()), operators prefixed with *(n) are fused by WSCG. For example, *(1) HashAggregate means HashAggregate is inside whole-stage codegen stage 1. Full deep-dive in Section 14.

12.8 Putting It All Together — SQL in Practice

Complete SQL workflow — register, query, inspect planPySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

# Read data and register as SQL-queryable table
orders = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/orders/")
products = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/products/")

orders.createOrReplaceTempView("orders")
products.createOrReplaceTempView("products")

# SQL query — goes through the full pipeline
result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT 
        p.category,
        COUNT(DISTINCT o.customer_id) AS unique_customers,
        SUM(o.amount) AS total_revenue,
        AVG(o.amount) AS avg_order_value
    FROM orders o
    JOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.product_id
    WHERE o.status = 'completed'
      AND o.year = 2024
    GROUP BY p.category
    HAVING SUM(o.amount) > 100000
    ORDER BY total_revenue DESC
    LIMIT 20
""")

# Inspect the full 4-phase plan
result.explain("extended")
# == Parsed Logical Plan ==    ← Step 1: raw AST
# == Analyzed Logical Plan ==  ← Step 2: names resolved
# == Optimized Logical Plan == ← Step 3: Catalyst applied
# == Physical Plan ==          ← Steps 4-5: physical + codegen

# Check what Catalyst optimized
result.explain("formatted")
# Look for:
# - *(1) ColumnarToRow → whole-stage codegen active
# - PushedFilters: [EqualTo(status,completed), EqualTo(year,2024)]
# - ReadSchema: only columns actually used
# - BroadcastHashJoin (if products is small) vs SortMergeJoin

# Equivalent DataFrame API — produces IDENTICAL plan
result_df = orders \
    .filter((F.col("status") == "completed") & (F.col("year") == 2024)) \
    .join(products, "product_id") \
    .groupBy("category") \
    .agg(
        F.countDistinct("customer_id").alias("unique_customers"),
        F.sum("amount").alias("total_revenue"),
        F.avg("amount").alias("avg_order_value")
    ) \
    .filter(F.col("total_revenue") > 100000) \
    .orderBy(F.desc("total_revenue")) \
    .limit(20)

# Verify identical plans
result.explain("formatted")
result_df.explain("formatted")
# Both produce the EXACT same physical plan ✓
⚠️ Common Pitfall UDFs break optimization. When you use a Python UDF (@udf), Catalyst treats it as an opaque function — it cannot push filters through it, cannot prune columns inside it, and cannot generate efficient bytecode for it. Always prefer built-in Spark SQL functions (pyspark.sql.functions) over UDFs. If you must use a UDF, use a pandas UDF (vectorized) which processes batches via Arrow instead of serializing row-by-row.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Spark SQL optimize a query?" — Walk through the 5 phases: (1) Parse: SQL string → AST. (2) Analyze: resolve names/types against Catalog. (3) Optimize: Catalyst applies 100+ rules — pushdown, pruning, folding, join reorder. (4) Plan: generate physical operator candidates, CBO selects cheapest. (5) Codegen: Tungsten fuses operators into single JVM method. Key insight: both SQL strings and DataFrame API feed into the same optimizer — identical performance regardless of input language.
⚙️ Engineering Insight Reading explain("formatted") like a pro: Look for these signals. *(1) prefix = whole-stage codegen active (good). PushedFilters in the scan = filter pushed to data source (good). BroadcastHashJoin = small table broadcast (fastest join). SortMergeJoin = both sides shuffled and sorted (expected for large × large). CartesianProduct = missing join condition (BUG — fix immediately). BroadcastNestedLoopJoin = non-equi join with broadcast (often slow — verify intent).
Part 6 — Section 13

Catalyst Optimizer

The brain of Spark SQL — 100+ rules that transform naive queries into high-performance execution plans.

13 — Catalyst Optimizer

13.1 Architecture Overview

The Catalyst Optimizer is Spark SQL's extensible, rule-based query optimizer. It sits at the center of the SQL execution pipeline, transforming the analyzed logical plan into an optimized logical plan, and then collaborating with the Physical Planner to select the best execution strategy. Catalyst operates in four distinct phases, each with its own set of rules and transformations.

The key architectural insight is that Catalyst represents query plans as immutable tree structures. Each optimization rule is a function that pattern-matches tree nodes and produces a transformed tree. Rules are applied iteratively in batches until a fixed point is reached — the plan stops changing, meaning no further optimization is possible.

13.2 Rule-Based Optimization (RBO) — Key Rules

Catalyst's rule-based optimizer applies deterministic rewrite rules — each rule pattern-matches against the plan tree and produces a transformed tree if the pattern matches. Rules are grouped into batches that execute in order. Each batch runs repeatedly until a fixed point (plan unchanged) or a maximum iteration count is reached.

RuleCategoryImpactRequires Stats?
Predicate PushdownFilter placement10–100× I/O reductionNo
Column PruningProjection2–50× I/O reduction (wide tables)No
Constant FoldingExpression simplificationMinor CPU improvementNo
Boolean SimplificationExpression simplificationMinor CPU improvementNo
Null PropagationExpression simplificationMinor — enables other rulesNo
Subquery FlatteningPlan rewrite10–1000× for correlated subqueriesNo
Join ReorderingCBO10–100× for multi-way joinsYes — ANALYZE TABLE
Limit PushdownEarly termination10–100× for LIMIT + UNIONNo

13.3 Cost-Based Optimization (CBO)

While rule-based optimization applies deterministic transformations (always beneficial), the CBO makes data-dependent decisions based on table and column statistics. It estimates the cost (rows, bytes, CPU) of each candidate plan and selects the cheapest one.

CBO requires statistics collected via ANALYZE TABLE. Without statistics, Spark falls back to heuristics (file size metadata) which are often inaccurate — leading to suboptimal join strategies and join ordering.

Statistics collection and CBO verificationSQL + PySpark
-- Collect table-level statistics
ANALYZE TABLE orders COMPUTE STATISTICS;

-- Collect column-level statistics (more detailed — enables histogram)
ANALYZE TABLE orders COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS;

-- Collect stats for specific columns only
ANALYZE TABLE orders COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR COLUMNS
  customer_id, product_id, amount, status;

-- Verify statistics are collected
DESCRIBE EXTENDED orders;
DESCRIBE EXTENDED orders amount;  -- Column-level stats

-- View CBO-estimated statistics in query plan
EXPLAIN COST
SELECT customer_id, SUM(amount)
FROM orders
WHERE status = 'completed'
GROUP BY customer_id;
-- Shows: Statistics(sizeInBytes=2.4 GiB, rowCount=1.0E+8)
Enable all CBO featuresPySpark
# Enable CBO (default true in Spark 3.x, but verify)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.cbo.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.dp.threshold", "12")  # DP for up to 12 tables
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.cbo.planStats.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.statistics.histogram.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.statistics.histogram.numBins", "254")

# After collecting stats, verify CBO is used in plan
df.explain("cost")
# Look for: Statistics(sizeInBytes=X, rowCount=Y) ← CBO active
# If you see: Statistics(sizeInBytes=X) without rowCount ← stats missing

13.4 Predicate Pushdown — In Detail

Predicate pushdown is the single most impactful optimization in Spark SQL. It moves filter conditions from higher in the plan tree down to the data source scan, allowing Parquet/ORC to skip entire row groups and file splits that don't match the filter. On partitioned data, it eliminates entire partition directories without reading them.

⚙️ Engineering Insight Predicate pushdown doesn't work through UDFs. If you write df.filter(my_udf(col("status")) == "active"), Catalyst cannot push this filter to the Parquet scan because my_udf is opaque. Always use built-in Spark SQL expressions for filter conditions that should be pushed down: df.filter(col("status") == "active"). Verify pushdown with df.explain("formatted") — look for PushedFilters in the scan node.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Cast expressions can break pushdown. If Parquet column year is stored as INT but your filter uses df.filter(col("year") == "2024") (string literal), Spark must insert a CAST — and some data sources cannot push casts. Always match the literal type to the column type: df.filter(col("year") == 2024) (integer literal). Check explain() for unexpected Cast nodes in the filter expression.

13.5 Custom Optimizer Rules

Catalyst is extensible — you can register custom optimization rules that will be applied alongside the built-in rules. This is an advanced feature used by library developers (Delta Lake, Iceberg) and platform teams to inject domain-specific optimizations.

Custom Catalyst rule — Scala exampleScala
import org.apache.spark.sql.catalyst.rules.Rule
import org.apache.spark.sql.catalyst.plans.logical._
import org.apache.spark.sql.catalyst.expressions._

// Custom rule: simplify x AND TRUE → x
object RemoveRedundantTrueLiteral extends Rule[LogicalPlan] {
  def apply(plan: LogicalPlan): LogicalPlan = plan transform {
    case Filter(And(left, right), child) if right == Literal.TrueLiteral =>
      Filter(left, child)  // Simplified: remove AND TRUE
    case Filter(And(left, right), child) if left == Literal.TrueLiteral =>
      Filter(right, child) // Simplified: remove TRUE AND
  }
}

// Register the custom rule with Spark
spark.experimental.extraOptimizations = Seq(RemoveRedundantTrueLiteral)

// Now this rule runs alongside all built-in Catalyst rules
// Useful for: Delta Lake optimizations, Iceberg pushdown,
// domain-specific partition pruning, custom data source hints
🎯 Interview Tip "Explain how the Catalyst optimizer works." — (1) Catalyst represents plans as immutable trees. (2) Optimization rules are functions that pattern-match tree nodes and produce transformed trees. (3) Rules are grouped into batches applied iteratively until a fixed point (plan stops changing). (4) Two categories: RBO (deterministic rules like pushdown, pruning, folding — always beneficial) and CBO (statistics-driven decisions like join strategy selection and multi-way join ordering). (5) Catalyst is extensible — custom rules can be added via spark.experimental.extraOptimizations. Key insight: Catalyst's power comes from seeing the entire query plan before execution — this is why lazy evaluation is essential.
Part 6 — Section 14

Tungsten Execution Engine

Binary memory format, off-heap management, and whole-stage code generation — the CPU-level optimizations that make Spark fast.

14 — Tungsten Execution Engine

14.1 Why Tungsten Was Needed

By 2014, Spark had solved the I/O bottleneck with in-memory computation, but a new bottleneck emerged: CPU efficiency. The JVM's memory model was wasting enormous resources on object overhead, garbage collection, and cache-unfriendly pointer chasing. Three specific problems made pre-Tungsten Spark CPU-bound:

  • Object overhead: A Java String "hello" takes ~48 bytes in the JVM heap (16-byte object header + 12-byte char array header + 10 bytes data + padding) vs 5 bytes of raw data. A simple 4-column Row object could use 200+ bytes for 40 bytes of actual data — 80% waste.
  • Garbage collection: Millions of short-lived JVM objects per task trigger frequent GC pauses. A large executor with 32 GB heap could spend 20–30% of CPU time in GC.
  • CPU cache misses: JVM objects are scattered across the heap, connected by pointers. Traversing an object graph causes constant L1/L2 cache misses — the CPU waits for memory instead of computing.

Tungsten was Project Tungsten's answer to all three problems: a custom binary memory format, manual memory management, and generated JVM bytecode that processes data in tight CPU loops.

14.2 UnsafeRow — Binary Memory Format

Tungsten stores all DataFrame/Dataset data in a compact binary row format called UnsafeRow. Instead of JVM objects with headers and pointers, data is packed into contiguous byte arrays with a fixed schema-driven layout. This eliminates object overhead, enables sequential memory access, and allows Spark to manage memory explicitly.

14.3 Off-Heap Memory Management

Tungsten can manage memory outside the JVM heap using sun.misc.Unsafe — a low-level API that directly allocates, reads, and writes native memory. Off-heap memory has zero GC impact: the JVM garbage collector doesn't see it, doesn't scan it, and doesn't pause for it.

Tungsten's memory manager tracks allocated pages, handles spill-to-disk when memory is exhausted, and frees memory explicitly when operations complete. This is similar to how C/C++ programs manage memory — but wrapped in Spark's safe allocation/deallocation lifecycle.

Off-heap configurationBash
# Enable Tungsten off-heap memory
--conf spark.memory.offHeap.enabled=true
--conf spark.memory.offHeap.size=8g

# Total executor memory footprint:
# executor.memory (16g)         → JVM heap (GC-managed)
# + offHeap.size (8g)           → Tungsten native memory (no GC)
# + executor.memoryOverhead (4g) → Python workers, Netty, OS
# = Total container: ~28g

# YARN/K8s must allocate this total — or container gets OOM-killed

14.4 Whole-Stage Code Generation (WSCG)

WSCG is Tungsten's most dramatic optimization. Instead of interpreting query operators one row at a time through a chain of virtual method calls (the volcano/iterator model), Tungsten generates JVM bytecode at query compile time that fuses all operators within a pipeline stage into a single tight loop. This eliminates virtual dispatch overhead, enables CPU branch prediction, and keeps data in CPU registers.

14.5 Verifying WSCG in Query Plans

In the explain("formatted") output, operators that are fused by WSCG are prefixed with *(n) — the asterisk followed by a stage number. Each *(n) group represents one generated JVM method. Operators without the prefix are executed via the traditional iterator model (not fused).

Reading WSCG in explain outputPySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/events/") \
    .filter(F.col("year") == 2024) \
    .groupBy("user_id") \
    .agg(F.sum("amount").alias("total"))

df.explain("formatted")
# == Physical Plan ==
# *(2) HashAggregate(keys=[user_id], functions=[sum(amount)])
# +- Exchange hashpartitioning(user_id, 200)     ← SHUFFLE (breaks codegen)
#    +- *(1) HashAggregate(keys=[user_id], functions=[partial_sum(amount)])
#       +- *(1) Filter (year = 2024)
#          +- *(1) ColumnarToRow
#             +- FileScan parquet [user_id,amount,year]
#                PushedFilters: [EqualTo(year,2024)]
#
# *(1) = Stage 1 codegen: Scan + Filter + Partial Aggregate fused
# *(2) = Stage 2 codegen: Final Aggregate fused
# Exchange (shuffle) = codegen boundary — cannot fuse across shuffle

# Check if codegen is active
print(spark.conf.get("spark.sql.codegen.wholeStage"))  # → "true" (default)

# Disable codegen for debugging (compare performance)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.codegen.wholeStage", "false")
df.explain("formatted")  # No *(n) prefix — operators unfused
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.codegen.wholeStage", "true")  # Re-enable!

14.6 Performance Impact Summary

Tungsten FeatureWhat It DoesPerformance GainConfig
UnsafeRowCompact binary row format — no JVM object overhead2–5× less memory per rowAlways on for DataFrames
Off-Heap MemoryAllocate execution memory outside JVM heapEliminate GC pauses on large heapsspark.memory.offHeap.enabled=true
Whole-Stage CodegenFuse operators into single JVM method per stage2–10× for CPU-bound operationsspark.sql.codegen.wholeStage=true (default)
Cache-Friendly AccessSequential memory layout for row data10× fewer L1/L2 cache missesAutomatic with UnsafeRow
Sort-based ShuffleTungsten sort on binary data (no deserialization)2–3× faster shuffle sortAlways on (default sort shuffle)
⚙️ Engineering Insight Tungsten only works with DataFrames/Datasets — not RDDs. When you use the RDD API (rdd.map(lambda x: ...)), Spark treats each record as an opaque JVM object and cannot apply UnsafeRow encoding, off-heap storage, or code generation. This is the primary reason RDD operations are 3–6× slower than equivalent DataFrame operations. Always prefer the DataFrame API for structured data.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Python UDFs break Tungsten. When you use a standard Python UDF (@udf), Spark must: (1) deserialize the UnsafeRow back to a JVM Row object, (2) serialize it to Python via Py4J, (3) run your Python function, (4) serialize the result back to JVM, (5) re-encode as UnsafeRow. This destroys all Tungsten benefits. Use built-in Spark functions whenever possible. If you must use Python, use pandas UDFs which batch data via Arrow and avoid per-row serialization.
🎯 Interview Tip "What is Project Tungsten and why does it matter?" — Tungsten solves three CPU-level bottlenecks: (1) Memory overhead — UnsafeRow stores data in compact binary format (4–5× smaller than JVM objects). (2) GC pressure — off-heap memory via sun.misc.Unsafe bypasses JVM garbage collector entirely. (3) CPU inefficiency — Whole-Stage Code Generation fuses query operators into a single JVM method, eliminating virtual dispatch and enabling CPU pipeline optimization. Together these give 2–10× improvement on CPU-bound queries. Key: Tungsten only works with the structured APIs (DataFrame/Dataset) — RDDs bypass it completely.
Part 7 — Section 15

Shuffle System

The most expensive operation in Spark — how data is redistributed across the cluster, and how to minimize its cost.

15 — Shuffle System

15.1 What Is a Shuffle?

A shuffle is the process of redistributing data across partitions and executor nodes so that records with the same key end up in the same partition. It is triggered by wide transformations: groupBy, join, repartition, distinct, sort, reduceByKey. The shuffle is the single most expensive operation in Spark — it involves disk I/O, serialization, network transfer, and deserialization all at once.

Every shuffle creates a stage boundary in the DAG: upstream tasks (mappers) write partitioned shuffle files to local disk, and downstream tasks (reducers) fetch their assigned partitions from all mapper nodes over the network. Understanding the shuffle's mechanics is essential for every performance optimization in Spark.

15.2 Shuffle Write Phase

During the shuffle write phase, each mapper task partitions its output records by the reducer's partition key (typically hash(key) % numReducers), sorts them by partition ID, and writes them to local disk. Modern Spark (since 1.2) uses the Sort-Based Shuffle which produces exactly one data file + one index file per mapper task.

The sort-based approach works as follows: records are appended to an in-memory buffer (PartitionedPairBuffer or PartitionedAppendOnlyMap). When the buffer fills, it's sorted by partition ID and spilled to disk as a sorted run. At the end of the task, all sorted runs are merged into a single data file with an index that marks the byte offset for each reducer's partition. This keeps the file count manageable — unlike the old hash-based shuffle which created M × R files.

15.3 Shuffle Read Phase

During the shuffle read phase, each reducer task queries the MapOutputTracker on the Driver to discover which executors hold its assigned partition data. It then fetches those partition chunks concurrently from all mapper nodes using Netty HTTP. Fetched chunks are merged (sort-merged for SortMergeJoin, or hash-aggregated for HashAggregate) and processed by the reducer's operators.

Key configurations controlling read performance:

  • spark.reducer.maxSizeInFlight=48m — maximum total data fetched simultaneously per reducer (controls memory pressure vs throughput)
  • spark.reducer.maxReqsInFlight=Int.MAX — max concurrent fetch requests
  • spark.shuffle.io.maxRetries=3 — retries on fetch failure before aborting
  • spark.shuffle.io.retryWait=5s — wait between retries

15.4 Sort-Based Shuffle vs Hash-Based Shuffle (Legacy)

FeatureSort-Based (default since 1.2)Hash-Based (removed in 2.0)
Output files per mapper2 (1 data + 1 index)R files (1 per reducer partition)
Total files2 × M (manageable)M × R (explosion: 100×200 = 20,000)
Sort overheadYes — sort by partition IDNo sort — hash directly to file
Memory usageBounded — spills when fullR open file handles simultaneously
Large cluster behaviorScales well — file count is O(M)Breaks — OS file descriptor limit hit
StatusActive — default since Spark 1.2Removed in Spark 2.0

15.5 External Shuffle Service (ESS)

The External Shuffle Service is a standalone process running on each worker node, outside of executor JVMs. It serves shuffle files to reducers even after the executor that wrote them has been removed. ESS is required for Dynamic Allocation — without it, killing an idle executor destroys its shuffle files, forcing upstream stages to re-execute.

15.6 Why Shuffle Is Expensive — The Five Costs

The shuffle is expensive because it involves all five categories of distributed computing cost simultaneously:

15.7 Shuffle Reduction Techniques

Minimizing shuffles is the #1 performance optimization in Spark. Every technique that eliminates or reduces a shuffle has outsized impact on job duration and resource cost. Here are the key strategies:

TechniqueHow It Reduces ShuffleImpact
Broadcast JoinSmall table broadcast to all executors → join is local, zero shuffle for large tableEliminates join shuffle entirely
Filter before join/groupByReduces data volume before it enters the shuffleProportional to filter selectivity (often 10–100×)
Pre-aggregate (combiner)reduceByKey vs groupByKey: combines locally before shuffleReduces shuffle write by aggregation ratio
BucketingPre-shuffle data at write time → joins between co-bucketed tables need no shuffleEliminates join shuffle for bucketed tables
AQE coalesceMerges many small post-shuffle partitions into larger onesReduces task overhead, not shuffle volume
Repartition onceRepartition by key once, then reuse for multiple downstream joins1 shuffle instead of N shuffles
coalesce() vs repartition()coalesce reduces partitions without full shuffleNo network transfer for partition reduction

15.8 When and Why to Use Each Shuffle Technique

Choosing the right technique for your use case requires understanding the data characteristics, table sizes, and downstream operations. Here's a decision framework:

When/why decision examples — PySpark codePySpark
# ===== SCENARIO 1: product_id dimension table join =====
# Size: 50 MB · Access: million times
# Decision: Broadcast Join

products = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/products/")  # 50 MB
orders = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/orders/")         # 100 GB

# WHY broadcast: small table, accessed many times
# CONDITION: one side < autoBroadcastJoinThreshold
result = orders.join(
        broadcast(products),
        on="product_id"
)  # Zero shuffle, instant lookup

# ===== SCENARIO 2: join two large fact tables =====
# Size: both 50+ GB · Access: once
# Decision: SortMerge Join

orders = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/orders/")         # 50 GB
returns = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/returns/")        # 20 GB

# WHY SortMerge: both too large to broadcast, need stable plan
# CONDITION: both sides > threshold OR cannot fit in executor memory
result = orders.join(
        returns,
        on="order_id"
)  # 2 shuffles, but stable and predictable

# ===== SCENARIO 3: simple sum() aggregation =====
# Operation: SUM(amount) GROUP BY user_id
# Decision: reduceByKey

# WHY reduceByKey: associative operation, local combiner 10–50× reduction
# CONDITION: aggregation is commutative + associative (sum, count, max, min)
result = orders.groupBy("user_id") \
        .agg(F.sum("amount"))
# Catalyst rewrite: partial_sum locally on each partition
#                  → shuffle combined sums, not raw amounts

# ===== SCENARIO 4: complex aggregation (percentile, stddev) =====
# Operation: percentile_approx(), stddev_pop(), collect_list()
# Decision: groupByKey (with collect_set or collect_list)

# WHY groupByKey: no local combine function for percentiles/stdevs
# CONDITION: complex aggregation without associativity
result = orders.groupBy("category") \
        .agg(
                F.percentile_approx("amount", 0.95).alias("p95"),
                F.collect_list("order_id").alias("orders")
        )
# No way to combine percentiles locally → must flow raw data to shuffle

# ===== SCENARIO 5: finishing up — too many output partitions =====
# Current: 1000 partitions of 10 MB each = 10,000 tiny files
# Decision: coalesce() for final write

result = final_aggregation  # 1000 partitions from shuffle

# WHY coalesce: data is already shuffled, just merge files
# CONDITION: downstream is final output or read-only (no more shuffles)
result.coalesce(50) \
        .write.parquet("s3://bucket/output/")  # 50 clean files, no shuffle

# ===== SCENARIO 6: correct data skew for next join =====
# Current: some partitions 1 GB, others 100 MB — very skewed
# Decision: repartition() by join key

skewed_data = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/skewed/")
print(skewed_data.rdd.glom().map(len).collect())  # [1000000, 50000, 900000, 30000]

# WHY repartition: hash distribution ensures even loading
# CONDITION: data is unevenly distributed, next operation is expensive (join)
balanced = skewed_data.repartition(400, "user_id")
print(balanced.rdd.glom().map(len).collect())  # ~[500, 520, 490, 510] — balanced

result = balanced.join(big_other_table, "user_id")  # Fast join now
Shuffle monitoring and tuningPySpark
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# 1. Broadcast join — eliminate shuffle for large × small joins
result = large_orders.join(
    broadcast(small_products),  # Small table broadcast → no shuffle
    on="product_id"
)

# 2. Increase broadcast threshold (default 10 MB — often too low)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", str(256 * 1024 * 1024))  # 256 MB

# 3. Filter BEFORE join — less data enters shuffle
filtered_orders = orders.filter("year = 2024 AND status = 'completed'")
result = filtered_orders.join(products, "product_id")  # Much less data shuffled

# 4. Check shuffle size in Spark UI — or programmatically
df.explain("formatted")
# Look for: Exchange hashpartitioning(key, 200)  ← this is a shuffle
# If Exchange is absent → no shuffle (broadcast join or co-partitioned)

# 5. Tune shuffle-related configs
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "400")       # Post-shuffle partitions
spark.conf.set("spark.shuffle.compress", "true")               # Compress shuffle data (default true)
spark.conf.set("spark.shuffle.spill.compress", "true")          # Compress spill files
spark.conf.set("spark.reducer.maxSizeInFlight", "96m")          # Double fetch buffer
spark.conf.set("spark.shuffle.io.maxRetries", "10")             # More retries on unstable networks
spark.conf.set("spark.shuffle.io.retryWait", "10s")              # Longer wait between retries
⚙️ Engineering Insight The hierarchy of shuffle elimination: (1) Broadcast join — eliminates join shuffle entirely if one side fits in memory. Set autoBroadcastJoinThreshold=256MB for dimension tables. (2) Pre-filter aggressively — every row filtered before a shuffle is a row that doesn't get serialized, written to disk, or transferred over the network. (3) Bucketing — pre-shuffle at write time so joins at read time need no shuffle. (4) AQE — auto-converts SortMergeJoin to BroadcastHashJoin at runtime if one side turns out small after execution. These four techniques eliminate 80%+ of shuffle cost in most production workloads.
⚠️ Common Pitfall "Shuffle fetch failed" is the most common production Spark error. It means a reducer tried to fetch shuffle data from a mapper's executor, but the executor is dead (OOM, spot preemption, timeout). Fix: (1) Enable External Shuffle Service (spark.shuffle.service.enabled=true). (2) Increase retry config: spark.shuffle.io.maxRetries=10, spark.shuffle.io.retryWait=10s. (3) Increase executor memory if OOM is causing executor death. (4) On Kubernetes, ensure executor pods have enough memoryOverhead — container OOM-kill often causes this.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Spark shuffle work and why is it expensive?" — Walk through: (1) Write phase: Each map task sorts output by reducer partition ID, writes to local disk as one data file + one index file. (2) Read phase: Each reducer queries MapOutputTracker for file locations, fetches its partition from ALL mappers via Netty HTTP. (3) Five costs: disk I/O (always writes to disk), serialization (2× per record), network (all-to-all transfer — 60% of cost), GC pressure (deserialized objects on reducer), file management. (4) Minimize: broadcast joins, pre-filtering, bucketing, AQE. Key insight: shuffle is the only operation in Spark that always touches disk and network — everything else can stay in memory.
Part 7 — Section 16

Block Manager

Spark's distributed storage engine — how cached data, shuffle files, and broadcast variables are stored and served across the cluster.

16 — Block Manager

16.1 Architecture Overview

The BlockManager is Spark's distributed block storage system. Every piece of data that Spark manages — cached RDD/DataFrame partitions, broadcast variables, shuffle files, and streaming state — is stored as a block in the BlockManager. Each executor runs its own BlockManager instance, and a central BlockManagerMaster on the Driver tracks which blocks live on which executors.

The BlockManager is the backbone of Spark's caching and data locality system. When the Task Scheduler needs to decide where to run a task, it queries the BlockManagerMaster to find which executors already hold the task's input data — enabling PROCESS_LOCAL scheduling. When a cached partition is requested but not present locally, the BlockManager fetches it from a remote executor over the network.

16.2 What Is a Block?

A block is the fundamental unit of data storage in Spark. Everything managed by Spark is stored as a block, identified by a BlockId — a typed key that encodes what the block represents:

Block TypeBlockId FormatExampleWhat It Stores
RDD Partitionrdd_<rddId>_<partitionIndex>rdd_5_3Cached RDD partition 3 of RDD 5
Shuffle Datashuffle_<shuffleId>_<mapId>_<reduceId>shuffle_1_4_0Shuffle 1, map task 4, reducer 0's chunk
Broadcastbroadcast_<broadcastId>_<pieceId>broadcast_0_piece0Chunk 0 of broadcast variable 0
Task Resulttaskresult_<taskId>taskresult_42Serialized result of task 42
Streaminput-<streamId>-<batchId>input-0-5Streaming micro-batch data block

16.3 BlockManagerMaster (Driver-side)

The BlockManagerMaster runs on the Driver and acts as a central registry for all blocks across the cluster. When an executor's BlockManager stores a new block (e.g., caches an RDD partition), it registers the block's location with the BlockManagerMaster. When another executor needs that block (for a task or remote cache read), it queries the BlockManagerMaster to discover where the block lives.

The BlockManagerMaster also manages eviction metadata — tracking which blocks are eligible for LRU eviction and their storage levels. It coordinates block replication for _2 storage levels (e.g., MEMORY_ONLY_2) by instructing a second executor to store a copy.

16.4 MemoryStore

The MemoryStore is the in-memory component of each executor's BlockManager. It stores blocks in three possible formats depending on the storage level and data type:

  • Deserialized values: JVM objects stored directly in the heap. Fastest access (no deserialization needed), but highest GC overhead — every cached Row is a Java object the GC must scan. Used by MEMORY_ONLY (default in Scala) and MEMORY_AND_DISK.
  • Serialized byte buffer: Data encoded as compact byte arrays using Kryo or Java serialization. Lower memory footprint (2–5× smaller), but each access requires deserialization. Used by MEMORY_ONLY_SER storage levels.
  • Columnar batch (Tungsten): Data stored in Tungsten's UnsafeRow binary format — compact, off-heap compatible, zero GC impact. Used internally for cached DataFrames when Tungsten is active (which it is by default for all DataFrame operations).

When the MemoryStore is full, blocks are evicted via LRU (Least Recently Used) — the oldest unused block is either spilled to DiskStore (if storage level allows disk) or simply dropped (if MEMORY_ONLY).

16.5 DiskStore

The DiskStore stores blocks on the executor's local disk filesystem. It is used for three purposes: (1) shuffle spill — when execution memory is exhausted, in-memory sort/hash buffers are spilled to disk. (2) RDD persistence — storage levels like MEMORY_AND_DISK and DISK_ONLY write blocks to disk when memory is full. (3) Streaming checkpoints — RocksDB state store writes SST files to local disk.

The disk path is configured via spark.local.dir — you can specify multiple comma-separated paths for better I/O parallelism across multiple SSDs:

DiskStore configurationBash
# Multiple local dirs for parallel I/O (spread across SSDs)
--conf spark.local.dir=/mnt/ssd1/spark,/mnt/ssd2/spark,/mnt/ssd3/spark

# Spark round-robins block writes across these paths
# More paths = more parallel I/O bandwidth for shuffle

# On Kubernetes: mount emptyDir or hostPath volumes
# spec:
#   volumes:
#     - name: spark-local
#       emptyDir:
#         sizeLimit: 200Gi    # SSD-backed ephemeral

16.6 Network Block Transfer

The BlockTransferService handles network-based block operations: serving local blocks to remote executors (shuffle read, remote cache access) and fetching remote blocks when needed locally. It uses Netty for non-blocking, pipelined I/O — multiple block fetches happen concurrently without blocking executor task threads.

Two primary use cases for remote block transfer:

  • Shuffle read: Reducer tasks fetch their shuffle partition data from mapper executors. This is the most common and highest-volume use of BlockTransferService.
  • Remote cache access: When a task needs a cached RDD partition that exists on a different executor (e.g., PROCESS_LOCAL not available but NODE_LOCAL or ANY is), it fetches the block over the network instead of recomputing from lineage.
ConfigDefaultPurpose
spark.network.timeout120sRPC and block fetch timeout
spark.storage.blockManagerSlaveTimeoutMs120sTimeout for slave BM heartbeats
spark.reducer.maxSizeInFlight48mMax shuffle fetch buffer per reducer
spark.shuffle.io.maxRetries3Retries on fetch failure
spark.shuffle.io.retryWait5sWait between retries
spark.local.dir/tmpDisk paths for shuffle/spill/cache
spark.storage.replication.proactivefalseAuto-replicate blocks when executor lost
⚙️ Engineering Insight The BlockManager is why df.cache() enables PROCESS_LOCAL scheduling. When you cache a DataFrame, each partition is stored as a block in the executor's MemoryStore. The block's location is registered with the BlockManagerMaster on the Driver. When subsequent tasks need that partition, the Task Scheduler queries the BlockManagerMaster, discovers the block is on Executor 2, and assigns the task to Executor 2 — achieving zero-network data access. Without caching, every task must re-read from the source (HDFS/S3), losing locality.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Using /tmp as spark.local.dir (the default) on production clusters is dangerous. The /tmp partition is often small (10–20 GB) and shared with the OS. Shuffle spill and cached blocks quickly fill it, causing "No space left on device" errors that crash executors. Always configure spark.local.dir to point to dedicated SSD-backed volumes with 100+ GB of space. On Kubernetes, use emptyDir volumes with explicit sizeLimit.
🎯 Interview Tip "What is the BlockManager in Spark?" — The BlockManager is Spark's distributed block storage engine. Every cached partition, broadcast variable, shuffle file, and streaming state block is managed by it. Each executor has a local BlockManager (MemoryStore + DiskStore + Netty TransportService), and a central BlockManagerMaster on the Driver maintains a registry of all block locations. The key uses: (1) Cache serving — stores and serves cached DataFrame partitions. (2) Shuffle serving — holds shuffle output files for reducer fetches. (3) Broadcast distribution — stores broadcast variable chunks via TorrentBroadcast. (4) Locality enablement — the Task Scheduler queries BlockManagerMaster to find where data lives for locality-aware scheduling.
Part 7 — Section 17

Memory Management

Unified Memory Manager — how Spark divides executor memory between execution, storage, and user code.

17 — Memory Management

17.1 Unified Memory Manager Overview

Spark's Unified Memory Manager (introduced in Spark 1.6, default since) governs how each executor's JVM heap is divided between competing memory consumers: shuffle/sort/join buffers (execution), cached RDD/DataFrame blocks (storage), user data structures (UDFs, custom objects), and Spark's own internal overhead. Understanding this layout is critical for diagnosing OOM errors, tuning cache performance, and sizing executors.

The key innovation of the Unified Memory Manager is that execution and storage memory share a single pool with a soft boundary — they can borrow from each other dynamically based on current demand, instead of being rigidly partitioned (as in the old StaticMemoryManager).

17.2 Memory Regions Breakdown

RegionSizePurposeConfigurable?
Reserved Memory300 MB (fixed)Spark internal objects, system overheadNo — hardcoded
User Memory40% of (heap − 300 MB)UDFs, custom objects, Python worker memory, non-Spark allocationsIndirectly via spark.memory.fraction
Spark Memory (unified)60% of (heap − 300 MB)Shared pool for Execution + Storagespark.memory.fraction=0.6
→ Execution MemoryDynamic (shared pool)Shuffle/sort/join buffers — active computationDynamic — borrows from Storage
→ Storage MemoryDynamic (shared pool)Cached blocks, broadcast variablesspark.memory.storageFraction=0.5 (minimum guarantee)

17.3 Unified Memory Model — Borrowing Behavior

The unified model's power lies in its dynamic borrowing rules. Execution and Storage share the same pool with a soft boundary. The rules are asymmetric by design — execution tasks are always prioritized over caching:

  • Execution can evict Storage: If execution memory is needed for a shuffle/sort/join and storage has blocks beyond its guaranteed minimum (storageFraction), execution forcibly evicts cached blocks to make room. Evicted blocks are either spilled to disk (MEMORY_AND_DISK) or dropped (MEMORY_ONLY).
  • Storage can borrow idle Execution memory: If execution memory is currently unused (no active shuffles/sorts), storage can expand into the idle space for caching. However, storage cannot evict active execution tasks — it can only use truly idle execution memory.
  • Storage has a guaranteed minimum: spark.memory.storageFraction=0.5 means execution cannot evict storage below 50% of the unified pool. This prevents execution from completely starving cache.

17.4 Driver Memory

The Driver JVM has its own memory pool, separate from executors. It holds: the query plan graph, broadcast variables before distribution, results from collect() calls, metadata from the BlockManagerMaster, and the DAG/Task Scheduler state. Key configs:

  • spark.driver.memory (default 1g) — the JVM heap size. Increase to 4–16 GB for production workloads with large broadcast tables or complex plans.
  • spark.driver.maxResultSize (default 1g) — the maximum serialized size of all results returned by collect(). If the result exceeds this, Spark throws an error instead of OOMing the driver.
  • spark.driver.memoryOverhead — off-heap memory for the Driver container (K8s/YARN). Includes Netty buffers and JVM metaspace.
broadcast() until distributed to executors collect() Results Buffer Returned from df.collect() or rdd.take() Limited by maxResultSize ⚠ Driver is a bottleneck — keep it small. Large collect() operations or big broadcast tables can OOM the Driver. Never collect() a large DataFrame — use df.take(1000) instead to get a sample.

17.5 Executor Memory Configuration — Complete Picture

Complete memory configurationBash
# Executor memory (JVM heap)
--executor-memory 16g

# Off-heap Tungsten memory (optional but recommended for large heaps)
--conf spark.memory.offHeap.enabled=true
--conf spark.memory.offHeap.size=8g

# Memory overhead (Python workers, Netty, JVM metaspace, Arrow)
--conf spark.executor.memoryOverhead=4g

# Unified memory pool fraction (default 0.6 — rarely needs changing)
--conf spark.memory.fraction=0.6

# Storage guarantee within unified pool (default 0.5)
--conf spark.memory.storageFraction=0.5

# Driver memory
--driver-memory 8g
--conf spark.driver.maxResultSize=4g
--conf spark.driver.memoryOverhead=2g

# GC tuning for large heaps
--conf spark.executor.extraJavaOptions="-XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200"

17.6 GC Tuning Quick Reference

For executor heaps above 8 GB, G1GC is recommended over the default Parallel GC. G1GC uses a region-based heap layout with incremental collection, targeting a configurable maximum pause time. For heaps above 32 GB, consider enabling off-heap Tungsten memory to keep the GC-managed heap smaller and pauses shorter.

Monitor GC health via the Spark UI → Executors tab. The key metric is GC time / task time ratio — if GC consumes more than 10% of total task time, memory tuning is needed.

Heap SizeRecommended GCKey FlagsNotes
< 8 GBDefault (Parallel GC)None neededPauses are short enough at this size
8–32 GBG1GC-XX:+UseG1GC -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200Region-based, targets max pause time
32+ GBG1GC + Off-HeapG1GC flags + offHeap.enabled=trueKeep JVM heap ≤32 GB, put rest off-heap
Any (JDK 15+)ZGC (experimental)-XX:+UseZGCSub-millisecond pauses, higher CPU overhead
⚙️ Engineering Insight The most common memory misconfiguration: Setting spark.executor.memory=32g but forgetting spark.executor.memoryOverhead. PySpark UDFs, Arrow batches, and Netty all consume off-heap memory that's invisible to the JVM but visible to the container. YARN/K8s kills the container when total memory (heap + off-heap) exceeds the allocated amount — but the error message says "Container killed by the ApplicationMaster" with no Java OOM trace. Always set memoryOverhead explicitly — 10–20% of executor memory is a good starting point, higher if using Python UDFs heavily.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Caching too aggressively fills Storage Memory, leaving insufficient Execution Memory for shuffles/joins. When a large shuffle starts, execution forcibly evicts cached blocks — causing them to be re-read from source on next access. Worse: if the evicted data was from a MEMORY_ONLY level, it's simply dropped and must be recomputed from lineage. Solutions: (1) Use MEMORY_AND_DISK so evicted blocks spill to disk instead of being lost. (2) unpersist() DataFrames you no longer need. (3) Enable off-heap memory to give execution more room without competing with storage.
🎯 Interview Tip "Explain Spark's memory management." — Spark's Unified Memory Manager divides executor heap into: (1) Reserved (300 MB fixed), (2) User Memory (40% — UDFs, custom objects), (3) Unified Spark Pool (60% — shared between Execution and Storage). Execution and Storage share dynamically: execution can evict storage blocks when it needs more memory (priority for active computation), but storage can only borrow idle execution memory. storageFraction=0.5 guarantees storage at least 50% of the pool. Total container = heap + offHeap + overhead. Key insight: execution always wins over caching — this is why large shuffles can evict your carefully cached DataFrames.
Part 7 — Section 18

Executor Out of Memory

The most common production failure — four types of OOM, root cause diagnosis, and systematic fix strategies.

18 — Executor Out of Memory (OOM)

18.1 Types of OOM in Spark

OOM is the single most common failure mode in production Spark. But "OOM" is not one problem — it's four distinct failure types with different causes, symptoms, and fixes. Misdiagnosing the type leads to wasted hours. The first step in any OOM investigation is identifying which type you're dealing with.

OOM TypeError MessageWhereMost Common CauseFirst Fix
Heap OOMJava heap spaceExecutorLarge partition / data skew↑ partitions or ↑ memory
Container OOMContainer killed by YARN/K8sExecutorOff-heap overflow (Python/Netty)↑ memoryOverhead
GC OverheadGC overhead limit exceededExecutorToo many JVM objectsUse SER cache / off-heap
Driver OOMDriver crashDrivercollect() / large broadcastAvoid collect(), ↑ driver-memory

18.2 Root Causes — Deep Analysis

Most OOM errors trace back to one of five root causes. Identifying the root cause (not just the symptom) prevents the OOM from recurring after a memory increase.

18.3 Debugging OOM — Step by Step

A systematic approach to OOM debugging prevents wasting time on wrong fixes. Follow this exact sequence:

  1. Check the error message — identify which of the 4 OOM types (Java heap, container, GC overhead, Driver)
  2. Spark UI → Executors tab — check GC time per executor. If GC time > 10% of task time → memory pressure
  3. Spark UI → Stages tab — find the failed stage. Look at input size and shuffle write/read metrics
  4. Spark UI → Stage Detail → Tasks — check if ONE task has 10× the input of others → data skew confirmed
  5. Check partition sizes programmatically — see if any partition is > 500 MB
  6. Check explain() for unexpected CartesianProduct or missing pushdown
  7. Enable AQE — auto-handles skew and partition coalescing
  8. Last resort: increase executor memory or reduce partition size
OOM debugging toolkitPySpark
# 1. Check partition count and sizes
print(f"Partitions: {df.rdd.getNumPartitions()}")

# Rows per partition — look for outliers (skew indicator)
partition_sizes = df.rdd.mapPartitions(
    lambda it: [sum(1 for _ in it)]
).collect()
print(f"Min: {min(partition_sizes)}, Max: {max(partition_sizes)}, "
      f"Avg: {sum(partition_sizes)//len(partition_sizes)}")
# If max >> avg → SKEW

# 2. Check data distribution by key (skew detection)
from pyspark.sql import functions as F
df.groupBy("join_key").count() \
  .orderBy(F.desc("count")).show(20)
# If top key has 100× more rows → salt that key

# 3. Check for unexpected cross joins
df_result.explain("formatted")
# Look for: CartesianProduct ← BUG! Missing join condition
# Look for: BroadcastNestedLoopJoin ← non-equi join (intentional?)

# 4. Fix: Increase partitions for large data
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "2000")

# 5. Fix: Enable AQE (auto-handles skew + partition sizing)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")

# 6. Fix: Repartition before heavy operations
df_repartitioned = df.repartition(500, "customer_id")
result = df_repartitioned.groupBy("customer_id").agg(...)

# 7. Quick memory diagnostic function
def diagnose_oom(df, key_col=None):
    print(f"=== OOM Diagnostic ===")
    print(f"Total rows: {df.count():,}")
    print(f"Partitions: {df.rdd.getNumPartitions()}")
    sizes = df.rdd.mapPartitions(lambda it: [sum(1 for _ in it)]).collect()
    print(f"Partition sizes - min:{min(sizes):,} max:{max(sizes):,} avg:{sum(sizes)//len(sizes):,}")
    skew_ratio = max(sizes) / (sum(sizes) / len(sizes)) if sizes else 0
    print(f"Skew ratio (max/avg): {skew_ratio:.1f}x")
    if skew_ratio > 5:
        print("⚠️  DATA SKEW DETECTED — consider salting or AQE")
    if key_col:
        print(f"\nTop 10 keys by count:")
        df.groupBy(key_col).count().orderBy(F.desc("count")).show(10)
⚙️ Engineering Insight The most impactful OOM fix is often the simplest: enable AQE with spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true. AQE automatically: (1) coalesces small post-shuffle partitions (eliminates tiny tasks), (2) detects and splits skewed partitions (fixes cause ②), (3) converts SortMergeJoin to BroadcastHashJoin at runtime if one side is small enough. In Spark 3.2+, AQE is enabled by default. If you're on Spark 3.0–3.1, enable it explicitly. It's a zero-code-change fix that resolves ~50% of OOM issues.
⚠️ Common Pitfall "I increased executor memory to 64 GB but still get OOM." — Two likely issues: (1) The problem is data skew, not insufficient memory. One partition has 50 GB of data — no amount of memory will help until you fix the skew. (2) The OOM is a container kill (off-heap), not a Java heap OOM. You increased executor-memory (heap) but memoryOverhead is still default. The fix: always diagnose the OOM type before increasing memory. Check Spark UI task metrics to confirm whether the issue is partition size, skew, or off-heap overflow.
🎯 Interview Tip "How do you debug OOM in Spark?" — Systematic approach: (1) Identify the type — Java heap (partition too large), container kill (off-heap), GC overhead (too many objects), or Driver crash (collect). (2) Check Spark UI — Executors tab for GC time, Stages tab for input sizes, Task tab for skew (one task 10× slower). (3) Check partition distribution — df.groupBy("key").count() to detect hot keys. (4) Check plan — explain() for CartesianProduct or missing pushdown. (5) Apply fixes in order: enable AQE (free win) → filter before joins → broadcast small tables → repartition → salt skewed keys → increase memory as last resort.
Part 8 — Section 19

Partitioning and Data Locality

How data is divided, how partition count affects performance, and when to use repartition vs coalesce.

19 — Partitioning and Data Locality

19.1 What Determines Initial Partition Count?

The number of partitions at each point in a Spark pipeline determines parallelism, memory pressure per task, and scheduling overhead. Three key stages determine partition count:

  • Source read (scan stage): Partitions = number of input files, HDFS blocks, or Parquet row groups. Reading 500 Parquet files = 500 tasks. HDFS with 128 MB block size: a 10 GB file = 80 blocks = 80 tasks.
  • After shuffle: Partitions = spark.sql.shuffle.partitions (default 200). This config controls ALL post-shuffle partition counts — groupBy, join, repartition. It's the most impactful performance config in Spark.
  • Explicit: df.repartition(N) or df.coalesce(N) or sc.parallelize(data, N) — you choose the count.

19.2 repartition() vs coalesce()

These two operations change the partition count but work very differently. Choosing the wrong one can either cause an unnecessary expensive shuffle or produce badly unbalanced partitions.

15.8 coalesce() vs repartition() — Detailed Conditions

Choosing between coalesce() and repartition() depends on whether you need to redistribute data across the cluster or simply merge existing partitions. This decision has enormous performance implications — choosing wrong can turn a millisecond operation into a minutes-long shuffle.

Factorcoalesce()repartition()
Dependency TypeNarrow (groups partitions)Wide (full hash shuffle)
Network TransferNone — data stays on nodeAll-to-all — every mapper → every reducer
Disk I/ONoneWrite + read shuffle files
SerializationNone2× (write + read)
Typical Speed<1 second (100 MB → 50 partitions)30–300 seconds (100 GB → rebalance)
Use Case: Final Output✓ Yes – perfect✗ No – wasteful
Use Case: Rebalance Skew✗ No – doesn't fix skew✓ Yes – hash redistributes
Use Case: Prepare for Join✗ Maybe slow downstream✓ Yes – data on same node
Data DistributionPreserves locality (groups 5 partitions per output partition)Hash-based (each record → hash % N)
When to UseReducing output files, post-agg cleanup, general safetyFixing skew, preparing for multi-join, changing layout fundamentally
coalesce() vs repartition() — When to use eachPySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

# Starting point: DataFrame with 1000 partitions (narrow, output from wide transformation)
df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/data/")  # 1000 partitions
print(df.rdd.getNumPartitions())  # → 1000

# ===== coalesce() — narrow transformation =====
# Use: reducing partitions when data is already distributed
# NO shuffle — moves data within existing executors

df_coalesced = df.coalesce(200)  # 1000 → 200 partitions
print(df_coalesced.rdd.getNumPartitions())  # → 200

# Under the hood: coalesce is a NARROW dependency
# Partitions [0-4] → new partition 0
# Partitions [5-9] → new partition 1
# Groups consecutive partitions → no network transfer

df_coalesced.rdd.toDebugString()  # Shows: narrow dependency (no Exchange)

# ===== repartition() — wide transformation =====
# Use: arbitrary partition layout, rebalancing uneven data
# FULL shuffle — repartitions data globally by key

df_repartitioned = df.repartition(200)  # 1000 → 200 partitions
print(df_repartitioned.rdd.getNumPartitions())  # → 200

# Under the hood: repartition is a WIDE dependency
# All 1000 partitions → hash shuffle → 200 new partitions
# Every record is shuffled (hash by row, distributed evenly)

df_repartitioned.rdd.toDebugString()  # Shows: Exchange operation

# ===== When to use coalesce() =====
# Scenario 1: Final output writing — too many small files
result = df.groupBy("category").agg(F.sum("amount"))  # 200 partitions
result.coalesce(10).write.parquet("s3://bucket/output/")  # Write 10 files instead of 200

# Scenario 2: Reduce memory pressure after wide transformation
shuffled = df.groupBy("user_id").agg(...)  # 200 shuffled partitions
result = shuffled.coalesce(50).filter(...)  # Narrow combine → less memory

# ===== When to use repartition() =====
# Scenario 1: Rebalance very skewed data
# (some partitions have 10MB, others have 1GB)
df_skewed = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/skewed/")
df_balanced = df_skewed.repartition(400)  # Forces even redistribution

# Scenario 2: Repartition by specific column for optimal join
orders = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/orders/")
products = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/products/")

# Both tables repartitioned by product_id → SortMergeJoin is fast (no extra shuffle)
orders_repartitioned = orders.repartition("product_id")
products_repartitioned = products.repartition("product_id")
result = orders_repartitioned.join(products_repartitioned, "product_id")

# ===== Performance comparison =====
import time

df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/large/")  # 1000 partitions, 100 GB

# coalesce: fast (no shuffle)
start = time.time()
df.coalesce(100).count()
print(f"coalesce time: {time.time() - start:.2f}s")  # → ~5 seconds (no network)

# repartition: slow (full shuffle)
start = time.time()
df.repartition(100).count()
print(f"repartition time: {time.time() - start:.2f}s")  # → ~45 seconds (network transfer)
Practical partition managementPySpark
# Check current partition count
print(df.rdd.getNumPartitions())  # e.g., 500

# Repartition by key — same customer always in same partition
# Full shuffle, but guarantees co-location for downstream joins
df_repart = df.repartition(200, "customer_id")

# Coalesce before write — reduces output files without shuffle
df_result.coalesce(50).write.format("parquet").save("s3://bucket/output/")
# Output: exactly 50 Parquet files (instead of 200 tiny files)

# Set shuffle partitions for the session
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "1000")
# All subsequent groupBy/join/sort will produce 1000 partitions

# Or let AQE auto-tune (best option for Spark 3.0+)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes", "128m")
# AQE starts with shuffle.partitions, then merges small partitions → ~128 MB each

19.3 Partition Pruning (Static)

When reading Hive-style partitioned data (directory structure like year=2024/month=01/day=15/), Spark uses static partition pruning to skip entire directory trees that don't match the query filter. If your query filters on year = 2024, Spark reads only the year=2024/ directories — potentially skipping 90%+ of the data without even opening those files.

This requires the filter column to be a physical partition column in the table's directory layout. Column-level predicate pushdown (min/max statistics in Parquet) is separate and works within files.

19.4 Optimal Partition Sizing Guidelines

Getting partition size right is a balancing act between parallelism and overhead:

⚙️ Engineering Insight The partition sizing formula for production: optimal_partitions = total_data_size_bytes / target_partition_size_bytes. For a 500 GB dataset with a target of 200 MB per partition: 500 GB / 200 MB = 2,500 partitions. Set spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=2500. Or better: enable AQE with advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes=128m and let Spark auto-compute this at runtime based on actual data sizes (which may differ from estimates).
⚠️ Common Pitfall coalesce(1) before write creates a single file but destroys parallelism. The final stage runs with 1 task on 1 core — all data flows through a single thread. For a 100 GB output, this can take 30+ minutes when writing to S3. Instead: coalesce(10) gives 10 reasonably-sized files that write in parallel. If you truly need 1 file, write multiple files then use hadoop fs -getmerge or Delta Lake OPTIMIZE to compact offline.
🎯 Interview Tip "When do you use repartition vs coalesce?" — repartition: when you need to increase partitions, need balanced partitions for downstream joins/aggs, or need to partition by a specific key (repartition(200, "key")). It does a full shuffle — expensive but produces even partitions. coalesce: when reducing partition count, typically before writing output to avoid small files. Zero shuffle cost — just merges adjacent partitions locally. But may produce unbalanced partitions. Rule of thumb: repartition for computation, coalesce for output.
Part 8 — Section 20

Join Strategies

Broadcast Hash Join, Sort Merge Join, Shuffle Hash Join — how Spark chooses, and how to force the right strategy.

20 — Join Strategies

20.1 Overview of Join Strategies

Joins are the most expensive and most common operation in data pipelines. Spark implements four join strategies, each optimized for different data size ratios and join conditions. Choosing the right strategy can mean the difference between a 30-second query and a 30-minute one. Spark's planner automatically selects a strategy based on table sizes, join type, and hints — but understanding the internals lets you guide it when the automatic choice is wrong.

20.2 Broadcast Hash Join (BHJ) — Deep Dive

The Broadcast Hash Join is the fastest join strategy and the most important optimization in star-schema queries. The smaller table is broadcast (copied) to every executor as an in-memory HashMap. Each executor then performs a local hash lookup against the large table partitions — zero shuffle for the large table.

BHJ is automatically chosen when the smaller side's estimated size is ≤ spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (default: 10 MB). This default is far too conservative for most workloads — dimension tables in star schemas are typically 50–500 MB. Increasing this threshold is one of the highest-impact, lowest-effort optimizations.

Broadcast join — automatic and manualPySpark
from pyspark.sql.functions import broadcast

# Method 1: Increase auto-broadcast threshold (recommended)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold",
              str(256 * 1024 * 1024))  # 256 MB
# Now any table ≤ 256 MB is auto-broadcast

# Method 2: Explicit broadcast hint (overrides threshold)
result = large_orders.join(
    broadcast(small_products),  # Force broadcast regardless of size
    on="product_id",
    how="inner"
)

# Method 3: SQL hint
result = spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(p) */ o.*, p.name, p.category
    FROM orders o
    JOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.product_id
""")

# Verify BHJ was chosen
result.explain("formatted")
# Look for: BroadcastHashJoin ← success!
# NOT: SortMergeJoin or Exchange (shuffle)

# Disable broadcast for a specific join (force SMJ)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", "-1")
# Or use hint:
result = df1.hint("merge").join(df2, "key")  # Force sort-merge
⚙️ Engineering Insight Set autoBroadcastJoinThreshold=256MB as your default. In star-schema workloads (fact table × dimension tables), this single config change eliminates join shuffle for every dimension table join — often reducing total job time by 50–80%. Dimension tables (products, users, dates, locations) are almost always under 256 MB. The memory cost is manageable: 256 MB × 20 executors = 5 GB total cluster memory for broadcast, but you save terabytes of shuffle I/O.

20.3 Sort Merge Join (SMJ) — Deep Dive

The Sort Merge Join is Spark's default for large × large joins when broadcast is not viable. Both tables are shuffled by join key (so matching keys land on the same executor), then sorted within each partition, then merged in a streaming fashion — a sorted merge pass that produces results without building a full hash map in memory.
