SMJ is memory-efficient (streaming, not hash-map-based) and safe (handles any data size). But it's slow: it requires two shuffles + two sorts. If either table's data is already partitioned and sorted by the join key (e.g., from a previous shuffle or bucketed table), Spark can skip the redundant shuffle/sort — check the plan for missing Exchange or Sort nodes.

20.4 Shuffle Hash Join (SHJ)

The Shuffle Hash Join is a middle ground: both sides are shuffled by join key (like SMJ), but instead of sorting, the smaller side is built into a per-partition hash map, and the larger side probes it. This avoids the sort overhead of SMJ — making it faster when the smaller side is significantly smaller than the larger but still too big for broadcast.

Spark prefers SMJ by default because SHJ carries OOM risk: if the smaller side's partition is larger than expected, the hash map overflows. Force SHJ with the hint /*+ SHUFFLE_HASH(table) */ or df.hint("shuffle_hash") when you're confident the smaller side fits in per-partition memory.

20.5 Join Strategy Comparison

StrategyShuffleSortBest ForOOM RiskSpeed
Broadcast Hash (BHJ)None for large sideNoLarge × small (dimension tables)Low (if small fits in memory)★★★★★ Fastest
Sort Merge (SMJ)Both sides shuffledBoth sortedLarge × large (default, safe)Low (streaming merge)★★★ Moderate
Shuffle Hash (SHJ)Both sides shuffledNoLarge × medium (when SMJ too slow)Medium (hash map per partition)★★★★ Fast
Cartesian / BNLJVariesNoNon-equi joins (last resort)High (M × N output)★ Very slow

20.6 Join Selection Decision Tree

Spark's planner follows this decision logic to select a join strategy automatically:

All join hints — SQL and DataFrame APIPySpark + SQL
# DataFrame API hints
result = df1.join(broadcast(df2), "key")               # Force BHJ
result = df1.hint("merge").join(df2, "key")              # Force SMJ
result = df1.hint("shuffle_hash").join(df2, "key")       # Force SHJ
result = df1.hint("shuffle_replicate_nl").join(df2, "key")# Force BNLJ

# SQL hints
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(dim) */ * FROM fact JOIN dim ON fact.k = dim.k
""")
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ SHUFFLE_MERGE(large) */ * FROM large JOIN other ON large.k = other.k
""")
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ SHUFFLE_HASH(medium) */ * FROM big JOIN medium ON big.k = medium.k
""")

# Multiple broadcast hints for star schema
spark.sql("""
    SELECT /*+ BROADCAST(d1), BROADCAST(d2), BROADCAST(d3) */
        f.order_id, d1.product_name, d2.customer_name, d3.date_str
    FROM fact_orders f
    JOIN dim_products d1 ON f.product_id = d1.id
    JOIN dim_customers d2 ON f.customer_id = d2.id
    JOIN dim_dates d3 ON f.date_key = d3.date_key
""")
# All 3 dimension tables broadcast → 3 join shuffles eliminated!

# Always verify the chosen strategy
result.explain("formatted")
# BroadcastHashJoin = ✓ (best for large × small)
# SortMergeJoin     = ✓ (expected for large × large)
# CartesianProduct  = ✗ BUG — missing join condition!
⚠️ Common Pitfall Missing join condition creates a CartesianProduct. If you write df1.join(df2) without specifying on="key", Spark produces a cross join: M × N rows. On two 10K-row tables, that's 100M rows — unexpected and slow. On two 1M-row tables, it's 1 trillion rows — job hangs forever or OOMs. Always check explain() for CartesianProduct or BroadcastNestedLoopJoin — these almost always indicate a missing or incorrect join condition.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Spark choose a join strategy?" — Decision tree: (1) If smaller side ≤ autoBroadcastJoinThreshold (10 MB default) → Broadcast Hash Join (fastest, no shuffle for large side). (2) If equi-join and both sides large → Sort Merge Join (default — 2× shuffle + 2× sort + merge). (3) If equi-join, smaller side fits per-partition, and hint given → Shuffle Hash Join (shuffle + hash, no sort). (4) If non-equi-join → Cartesian/BNLJ (last resort, M×N). Optimization: increase autoBroadcastJoinThreshold to 256 MB for star-schema workloads — eliminates shuffle for all dimension table joins. AQE can also convert SMJ → BHJ at runtime if one side turns out small.
⚙️ Engineering Insight AQE dynamic join strategy: In Spark 3.0+, AQE monitors actual shuffle sizes at runtime. If it discovers that one side of a planned SortMergeJoin is actually small enough for broadcast (the estimates were wrong), it dynamically converts SMJ → BHJ at runtime — no code change needed. This eliminates the most common scenario where SMJ is chosen because the CBO lacked statistics. Enable: spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true.
Part 8 — Section 21

Data Skew Handling

Detecting skewed partitions, the salting technique, and AQE's automatic skew join optimization.

21 — Data Skew Handling

21.1 What Is Data Skew?

Data skew occurs when data is unevenly distributed across partitions — one or a few partitions hold dramatically more data than others. This is the most common cause of Spark job hangs, timeouts, and OOM errors. A job with 200 tasks where 199 finish in 30 seconds but one takes 45 minutes is almost certainly suffering from skew.

Skew is insidious because aggregate metrics look fine — total rows, total shuffle bytes, average task time all seem reasonable. The problem only appears when you look at the distribution: one task processing 100× more data than the average.

21.2 Causes of Skew

  • Low-cardinality join keys: Joining on country_code where "US" has 80% of the data. All US records go to one partition.
  • NULL keys: All NULL values hash to the same partition. A dataset with 30% NULL join keys puts 30% of data in one task.
  • Hot keys in aggregations: One user_id with millions of events (bots, system accounts, popular users).
  • Poorly chosen partition keys: Partitioning by a column with non-uniform distribution (e.g., status where 95% is "active").
  • Exploded data: explode() on array/map columns where one row has 100K elements while others have 5.

21.3 Skew Detection

Detecting skew — 3 methodsPySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

# Method 1: Check key distribution — most reliable
df.groupBy("join_key").count() \
  .orderBy(F.desc("count")).show(20)
# If top key has 100× more rows than average → SKEW confirmed
# Example output:
# |join_key|  count  |
# |   US   |45000000 |  ← HOT KEY (90% of data)
# |   GB   |  2000000|
# |   DE   |  1500000|
# |   FR   |  1000000|

# Method 2: Check partition row counts
partition_counts = df.rdd.mapPartitions(
    lambda it: [sum(1 for _ in it)]
).collect()
print(f"Min: {min(partition_counts):,}")
print(f"Max: {max(partition_counts):,}")
print(f"Avg: {sum(partition_counts)//len(partition_counts):,}")
# Max >> Avg → SKEW

# Method 3: Spark UI → Stages → click stage → Tasks tab
# Sort by Duration descending
# If one task is 10× longer than median → SKEW
# Check "Input Size" column for the slow task

# Method 4: Check for NULL key skew
df.filter(F.col("join_key").isNull()).count()
# If high null count → filter nulls before join or replace with random values

21.4 Skew Fix 1 — Salting

Key salting is the classic manual fix for skew. The idea: append a random suffix to the hot key on the large table, and replicate the corresponding rows in the small table for each suffix. This splits the hot partition into N smaller sub-partitions that distribute across executors.

Salting implementation — complete codePySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

SALT_FACTOR = 16  # Split hot key into 16 sub-partitions

# Step 1: Large table — add random salt suffix to join key
large_df = large_df.withColumn(
    "salted_key",
    F.concat(
        F.col("join_key"),
        F.lit("_"),
        (F.rand() * SALT_FACTOR).cast("int").cast("string")
    )
)
# "US" → randomly becomes "US_0", "US_1", ..., "US_15"

# Step 2: Small table — replicate for each salt value
salt_values = [F.lit(i) for i in range(SALT_FACTOR)]
small_df = small_df.withColumn(
    "salt_explode",
    F.explode(F.array(salt_values))
).withColumn(
    "salted_key",
    F.concat(
        F.col("join_key"),
        F.lit("_"),
        F.col("salt_explode").cast("string")
    )
).drop("salt_explode")
# "US" → exploded to "US_0", "US_1", ..., "US_15" (16 copies)
# Small table grows 16× — acceptable since it's small

# Step 3: Join on salted key
result = large_df.join(small_df, on="salted_key", how="inner")

# Step 4: Drop the salt columns (clean output)
result = result.drop("salted_key")

# Result: "US" data now spread across 16 partitions → 16× faster

21.5 Skew Fix 2 — AQE Skew Join Optimization

Spark 3.0+ introduced AQE skew join handling — an automatic, zero-code-change solution. After the shuffle write phase, AQE analyzes the actual partition sizes at runtime. If any partition exceeds the median by a configurable factor, AQE automatically splits that partition into smaller sub-partitions and replicates the matching data from the other side — exactly like salting, but done transparently by the engine.

AQE skew join configuration — completePySpark
# Enable AQE with skew handling (recommended for ALL Spark 3.0+ jobs)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")

# Skew detection thresholds
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor", "5")
# A partition is "skewed" if size > 5 × median partition size

spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes",
              "268435456")  # 256 MB — minimum size to be considered skewed

# Advisory partition size for splitting
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes", "134217728")
# 128 MB — each sub-partition targets this size

# Also enable coalescing (handles too-many-small-partitions)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")

# That's it! No code changes needed. AQE handles skew automatically.
result = large_df.join(dim_df, "key")  # Skew handled at runtime

21.6 Skew Fix Comparison

TechniqueCode Change?Works ForProsCons
AQE Skew JoinNone — config onlyJoins with skewAutomatic, zero code change, runtime-adaptiveSpark 3.0+ only; doesn't help aggregation skew
Key SaltingYes — salt + explodeJoins + aggregationsWorks on any Spark version; handles aggregation skewComplex code; small table replicated N×; must choose salt factor
Broadcast JoinMinimal — hintLarge × small joinsEliminates shuffle entirely → no skew possibleOnly works if small side fits in memory
Filter Hot KeysYes — separate pathsKnown hot keysProcess hot keys with dedicated logic (broadcast or pre-agg)Requires knowing which keys are hot; two code paths
Repartition by keyMinimalAggregation skewSpread data evenly if key has enough cardinalityDoesn't help if skew is in the key itself
⚙️ Engineering Insight AQE skew join is the first fix to try — always. It requires zero code changes, works at runtime with actual data sizes (not estimates), and handles 80%+ of join skew scenarios. Only fall back to manual salting when: (1) You're on Spark 2.x (no AQE), (2) The skew is in an aggregation, not a join, (3) AQE's splitting isn't aggressive enough for extreme skew (single key = 99% of data). In those cases, combine salting with pre-aggregation: salt the key, aggregate locally per salt, then aggregate globally without the salt.
🎯 Interview Tip "How do you handle data skew in Spark?" — Three-step answer: (1) Detect: Spark UI shows one task 10× longer than others; groupBy("key").count() shows hot keys. (2) First fix — AQE: Enable spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled=true (Spark 3.0+) — automatic split and replicate at runtime, zero code change. (3) If AQE insufficient — Salt: Add random suffix to the hot key on the large table; explode the small table with all suffix values; join on salted key. Key insight: skew is a data distribution problem, not a resource problem — adding more memory won't fix it because all the data still goes to one partition.
Part 8 — Section 22

Cache and Persist

When to cache, which storage level to choose, and how to avoid the memory traps of over-caching.

22 — Cache and Persist

22.1 cache() vs persist() — What's the Difference?

Caching stores a DataFrame's computed partitions in the executor's BlockManager so that subsequent actions reuse the materialized data instead of recomputing from lineage. Spark offers two methods:

  • df.cache() — convenience shorthand that calls persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK) in PySpark (or MEMORY_ONLY in Scala). You get no control over where or how data is stored.
  • df.persist(storageLevel) — explicit control over memory vs disk, serialized vs deserialized, and replication. Always prefer this when you need to tune cache behavior.

Critically, caching is lazy — calling df.cache() only marks the DataFrame for caching. The actual materialization happens only when the first action is triggered. Subsequent actions then read from the cached blocks.

22.2 Storage Levels — Complete Guide

Spark offers seven storage levels that control where (memory, disk, or both), how (serialized or deserialized), and how many copies (replicated or not) cached blocks are stored. Choosing the right level is a trade-off between access speed, memory usage, and fault tolerance.

22.3 When to Cache — Decision Framework

Caching is not free — it consumes Storage Memory that could otherwise be used for Execution Memory (shuffle/join buffers). Cache only when the benefit (skipping recomputation) outweighs the cost (memory consumed). Here's the decision framework:

22.4 Unpersisting — Releasing Memory

Every cache() must eventually be followed by unpersist() to release the occupied Storage Memory. In long-running applications (Spark notebooks, streaming jobs), forgetting to unpersist is the most common cause of progressive memory exhaustion — each notebook cell caches more data until Storage Memory is full, then execution starts failing with OOM.

Complete cache lifecyclePySpark
from pyspark import StorageLevel

# === cache() shorthand — MEMORY_AND_DISK ===
df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/events/") \
    .filter("year = 2024") \
    .join(dim_table, "product_id")

df.cache()          # Mark for caching (lazy — nothing cached yet)
df.count()          # 1st action → materializes cache in BlockManager

# Now subsequent actions read from cache — fast
df.groupBy("category").count().show()    # Reads from cache ✓
df.groupBy("region").sum("revenue").show()  # Reads from cache ✓

# ALWAYS unpersist when done!
df.unpersist()      # Release memory immediately


# === persist() with explicit storage level ===
large_df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/big_table/")

# Serialized — uses 2–5× less memory, slightly slower access
large_df.persist(StorageLevel.MEMORY_AND_DISK_SER)
large_df.count()    # Materialize

# ... use large_df multiple times ...

large_df.unpersist() # Release when done


# === Check cache status via Spark UI ===
# Spark UI → Storage tab → shows all cached DataFrames
# Size in Memory | Size on Disk | Fraction Cached

# Programmatic cache check
print(df.is_cached)           # True/False
print(df.storageLevel)         # StorageLevel(...)

# Check all cached tables
spark.catalog.listTables()     # Shows isTemporary and cache status

# Clear ALL caches in session
spark.catalog.clearCache()     # Nuclear option — clears everything

22.5 Cache Internals — What Actually Happens

When an action triggers cache materialization, here's the internal flow for each partition:

  1. Task computes partition: The task runs the full lineage chain for its partition (read → filter → join → etc.) and produces an iterator of rows.
  2. Unroll into MemoryStore: The BlockManager "unrolls" the iterator into a contiguous memory block. For DataFrames, this is stored as Tungsten UnsafeRow binary format (compact, off-heap capable). For RDDs, as deserialized JVM objects.
  3. Register with BlockManagerMaster: The block's location (executor ID, block ID) is reported to the Driver's BlockManagerMaster for future locality-aware scheduling.
  4. If memory full — LRU eviction: If MemoryStore is full, the least recently used cached block is evicted. For MEMORY_AND_DISK, evicted blocks spill to DiskStore. For MEMORY_ONLY, they're simply dropped (must recompute from lineage on next access).
  5. Subsequent access: When a later task needs this partition, the Task Scheduler queries BlockManagerMaster, discovers it's on Executor 2, and assigns the task to Executor 2 (PROCESS_LOCAL). The task reads directly from MemoryStore — zero I/O.
⚙️ Engineering Insight Cache is most valuable after expensive operations that are reused. The classic pattern: (1) Read raw data from S3. (2) Apply expensive joins, filters, UDFs. (3) cache() the cleaned/joined result. (4) Run multiple analyses on the cached data (groupBy by region, by product, by time). (5) unpersist() when done. This avoids re-reading S3 and re-doing the joins for each analysis — often a 5–10× speedup for the second and subsequent actions.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Forgetting to unpersist() in notebooks is the #1 cause of progressive memory degradation. Each cell runs df.cache() on a new DataFrame, but the old cached DataFrames are never released. After 10–20 cells, Storage Memory is full → execution starts evicting cache for shuffle buffers → subsequent cells slow down dramatically → eventually OOM. Solution: always call df.unpersist() when you're done with a cached DataFrame, or periodically call spark.catalog.clearCache() to release all caches.
🎯 Interview Tip "When should you cache a DataFrame and what storage level to use?" — Cache when: (1) DataFrame is used in multiple downstream actions, (2) it was expensive to compute (join, heavy agg), (3) it fits in cluster memory. Don't cache if used once or cheap to recompute. Storage levels: MEMORY_AND_DISK (default, recommended — evicted blocks spill to disk, never lost), MEMORY_ONLY_SER (memory-tight, 2–5× smaller via serialization), DISK_ONLY (very large data). Always unpersist() when done. Key insight: caching consumes Storage Memory which shares the unified pool with Execution Memory — over-caching can cause shuffle/join OOM.
Part 9 — Section 23

Spark Structured Streaming

Micro-batch processing, watermarks, window operations, exactly-once semantics, and stateful streaming.

23 — Spark Structured Streaming

23.1 Streaming Architecture Overview

Spark Structured Streaming treats a live data stream as an unbounded table — new data arriving from Kafka, Kinesis, or file systems is appended as new rows to this conceptual table. You write queries using the exact same DataFrame/SQL API as batch, and Spark handles the incremental execution automatically.

The key architectural insight: Structured Streaming is not a separate engine — it reuses the entire Spark SQL execution pipeline (Catalyst optimizer, Tungsten codegen, DAG scheduler). A streaming query is essentially a series of incremental micro-batch jobs, each processing only the new data since the last batch.

23.2 Micro-Batch Processing (Default)

In micro-batch mode (the default), the streaming engine runs the query as a series of small batch jobs at configurable trigger intervals. Each micro-batch: (1) discovers new data since last checkpoint, (2) plans and optimizes the incremental query, (3) executes it on the cluster, (4) writes output, (5) commits offsets to the checkpoint. Latency is in the seconds range, bounded by the trigger interval and batch execution time.

Trigger TypeSyntaxBehaviorUse Case
Fixed intervalprocessingTime="30 seconds"Run batch every 30s (or as soon as previous completes)General streaming ETL
Onceonce=True (deprecated)Single batch, then stopBackfill/catch-up
Available NowavailableNow=TrueProcess all available data in multiple batches, then stopScheduled catch-up (Spark 3.3+)
Continuouscontinuous="1 second"Long-running tasks, sub-ms latency (experimental)Ultra-low-latency needs

23.3 Window Operations

Structured Streaming supports three types of time-based windows for aggregations. All use the event-time column (a timestamp in the data), not the processing time.

23.4 Watermarking — Handling Late Data

Watermarking tells Spark how long to wait for late-arriving data before closing a window and evicting its state. Without watermarks, stateful operations (aggregations, deduplication) would hold state forever — unbounded memory growth until OOM.

A watermark of 2 hours means: "data arriving more than 2 hours late relative to the max event time seen so far will be dropped." This allows Spark to clean up state for windows that are older than max_event_time - 2_hours.

Watermarking + Window aggregation — production patternPySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

# Watermark: allow up to 2 hours late data
# After that, window state is evicted and late data dropped
windowed_revenue = events_stream \
    .withWatermark("event_time", "2 hours") \
    .groupBy(
        F.window("event_time", "1 hour"),
        "product_id"
    ) \
    .agg(
        F.sum("revenue").alias("hourly_revenue"),
        F.count("*").alias("event_count"),
        F.approx_count_distinct("user_id").alias("unique_users")
    )

# Write to Delta Lake with exactly-once guarantee
query = windowed_revenue.writeStream \
    .format("delta") \
    .outputMode("append") \
    .option("checkpointLocation", "s3://bucket/checkpoints/revenue/") \
    .option("path", "s3://bucket/streaming/hourly_revenue/") \
    .trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") \
    .start()

query.awaitTermination()

23.5 Exactly-Once Semantics

Structured Streaming provides end-to-end exactly-once processing guarantees when three conditions are met:

  • Replayable source: The source can re-read data from a specific offset (Kafka, Kinesis, file source — all replayable).
  • Idempotent sink: The sink handles duplicate writes safely. Delta Lake uses atomic transactions, Kafka uses idempotent producer IDs, file sinks use atomic renames.
  • Checkpoint location: Spark persists source offsets, batch commit log, and state store snapshots to durable storage (S3/HDFS). On restart, it re-reads from the last committed offset and re-processes exactly the un-committed data.

23.6 Stateful Operations

Structured Streaming supports four types of stateful operations that maintain state across micro-batches:

OperationState StoredWatermark Required?Example
Streaming AggregationRunning counts/sums per key per windowYes (to bound state)groupBy(window, key).sum()
Stream-Stream JoinBuffered events from both sides within time rangeYes (to evict old events)Match impressions with clicks within 1 hour
DeduplicationSet of seen keysYes (to limit key retention)dropDuplicates("event_id")
mapGroupsWithStateCustom per-key state (sessions, FSMs)Optional (timeout-based)User session tracking, fraud scoring
⚙️ Engineering Insight State management is the most operationally complex part of Structured Streaming. State size grows with the number of unique keys × window duration. For a streaming aggregation with 10M unique user_ids and a 24-hour window, state can reach 10+ GB per executor. Use RocksDB state store (Spark 3.2+) for large state — it spills to local SSD instead of holding everything in JVM heap. Configure with spark.sql.streaming.stateStore.providerClass=...RocksDBStateStoreProvider. See Section 48 for the full RocksDB deep dive.
⚠️ Common Pitfall Forgetting watermarks on stateful operations causes unbounded state growth → eventual executor OOM. Without a watermark, Spark keeps state for every key/window forever because it can't know when late data might still arrive. Always set .withWatermark("event_time", "N hours") before any aggregation, join, or deduplication. The watermark should match your SLA for late data tolerance.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Structured Streaming achieve exactly-once?" — Three requirements: (1) Replayable source (Kafka/Kinesis with offset tracking), (2) Checkpoint on durable storage (offsets + state committed per batch — restart re-reads from last committed offset), (3) Idempotent sink (Delta Lake atomic commits, Kafka idempotent producer). On restart, Spark replays from the last committed offset, reprocesses the data, and the idempotent sink deduplicates the output. Key insight: exactly-once is an end-to-end property — if the source isn't replayable or the sink isn't idempotent, you get at-least-once (possible duplicates).
Part 10 — Section 24

Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)

Runtime query re-optimization using actual shuffle statistics — the most impactful Spark 3.x feature. Eliminates manual partition tuning, dynamically selects join strategies, and auto-handles data skew.

24 — Adaptive Query Execution (AQE)

24.1 What Is Adaptive Query Execution?

Adaptive Query Execution (AQE) is a runtime query re-optimization framework introduced in Apache Spark 3.0. Traditional Spark query optimization happens at compile time — Catalyst builds a physical plan before any data is processed, relying on estimated table statistics that are often stale or missing entirely.

AQE solves this by re-optimizing the query plan at stage boundaries (shuffle points) using actual runtime statistics collected from completed stages. After each shuffle stage writes its output, Spark knows the exact bytes per partition — and can re-plan downstream stages accordingly.

The mental model: AQE is like a GPS that recalculates your route every few miles based on actual traffic, instead of predicting all traffic at departure time.

Three independent features:

  • Coalescing Shuffle Partitions — merge many small partitions into fewer, optimally-sized ones
  • Dynamic Join Strategy Selection — switch from Sort-Merge Join to Broadcast Hash Join at runtime when one side turns out to be small
  • Skew Join Optimization — detect and split oversized partitions automatically during joins

Default status: spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true is the default starting from Spark 3.2. In Spark 3.0/3.1, you must enable it explicitly.

⚙️ Engineering Insight AQE is the single most impactful Spark 3.x feature for performance. At Uber and Netflix, enabling AQE reduced median job runtime by 20-30% and eliminated thousands of manual spark.sql.shuffle.partitions configurations. Always enable it in production.
🎯 Interview Tip When asked "How does Spark optimize queries?", mention both compile-time (Catalyst rules + CBO) and runtime (AQE). The key insight: "AQE re-plans using actual statistics from completed stages, not estimates." AQE is the key differentiator making Spark 3.x dramatically better than Spark 2.x.

24.2 Feature 1 — Coalescing Shuffle Partitions

The default spark.sql.shuffle.partitions=200 is a static guess. For a large dataset, 200 might be too few (large partitions → OOM). For a small or heavily-filtered dataset, 200 creates 200 tiny tasks each processing kilobytes — scheduling overhead dominates actual computation.

How AQE coalescing works: After a shuffle stage completes, AQE inspects actual byte sizes of each shuffle partition. It then merges adjacent small partitions until each combined partition reaches the advisory target size (default 64 MB). This is purely a logical merge — reducers simply read more shuffle blocks per task. No additional shuffle is needed.

⚙️ Engineering Insight — The New Best Practice With AQE enabled, set spark.sql.shuffle.partitions high (1000–2000) instead of trying to find the "right" value. AQE will automatically coalesce down to whatever is actually needed. This gives fine-grained shuffle for large data that auto-reduces for small data. AQE can only coalesce (reduce), not split — it cannot increase 200 to 500 if needed.
Coalescing configurationPySpark
# Enable coalescing (on by default in Spark 3.2+)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")

# Target partition size (default 64 MB). Heavy ETL: 128m-256m
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes", "134217728")  # 128 MB

# Set shuffle partitions HIGH — AQE coalesces down automatically
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "2000")

# Minimum partitions after coalescing (floor for parallelism)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.minPartitionNum", "1")

# Prioritize parallelism over advisory size
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.parallelismFirst", "true")

24.3 Feature 2 — Dynamic Join Strategy Selection

At compile time, Catalyst must choose between Broadcast Hash Join (BHJ) and Sort-Merge Join (SMJ) based on estimated table sizes. If Catalyst estimates one side as 50 GB (too large to broadcast), it chooses SMJ. But after aggressive filtering that side might actually be only 3 GB — small enough for BHJ. The estimate was wrong, and the query pays the cost of a full double-shuffle for SMJ.

AQE fixes this: It waits until the shuffle stages before the join complete, sees the actual byte size of each side, and if one side is small enough, converts SMJ → BHJ at runtime — eliminating the shuffle for the larger side entirely.

Dynamic join configurationPySpark
# AQE uses autoBroadcastJoinThreshold for runtime conversion check
# Increase to allow larger tables to be broadcast
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", "268435456")  # 256 MB

# This benefits BOTH compile-time Catalyst AND runtime AQE
# AQE can only UPGRADE SMJ → BHJ, never DOWNGRADE BHJ → SMJ

# Verify in plan: look for "AdaptiveSparkPlan" and "BroadcastHashJoin"
df.explain("formatted")

24.4 Feature 3 — Skew Join Optimization

Data skew during joins means one or a few partitions contain dramatically more data than others. One reduce task processes 5 GB while peers handle 100 MB — that task becomes the bottleneck. Before AQE, the fix was manual salting (tedious code changes). AQE handles this automatically at runtime.

How it works:

  1. Detection: After shuffle, AQE checks each partition: size > median × skewFactor AND size > thresholdInBytes
  2. Split: The skewed partition is split into N sub-partitions (each ≈ advisoryPartitionSize)
  3. Replicate: The corresponding partition from the other join side is replicated to match each sub-partition
  4. Result: N parallel tasks instead of 1 bottleneck task — often 10-100× speedup
Skew join configurationPySpark
# Enable AQE skew join (on by default in Spark 3.2+)
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")

# Partition is skewed if BOTH conditions are true:
# (1) size > median × skewFactor
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor", "5")
# (2) size > thresholdInBytes
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes", "268435456")  # 256 MB

# If skew still not detected, lower the factor or threshold:
# spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor = 3
# spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes = 134217728  # 128 MB
⚙️ Engineering Insight AQE skew join optimization is the single most valuable AQE feature for data engineering. At Uber, enabling it eliminated over 70% of manual salting code in production pipelines. AQE handles skew per-partition at runtime, adapting to actual data distribution that may change daily — much more robust than static salting.

24.5 AQE Internals — QueryStage Materialization Loop

Internally, AQE wraps the physical plan in an AdaptiveSparkPlanExec node. It creates QueryStageExec boundaries at every ShuffleExchange. The execution loop:

  1. Create QueryStages at each ShuffleExchange — these are materialization boundaries
  2. Submit leaf QueryStages for execution (parallel). They read data and write shuffle output.
  3. Wait for completion and collect MapOutputStatistics (exact bytes per partition)
  4. Re-optimize remaining plan using AQEOptimizer rules: CoalesceShufflePartitions, OptimizeSkewedJoin, DynamicJoinSelection
  5. Submit next stages → repeat until all stages complete
Verifying AQE is activePySpark
# Check explain output for AQE indicators
df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/orders/") \
    .join(spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/customers/"), "customer_id") \
    .groupBy("country").count()

df.explain("formatted")
# Look for: "AdaptiveSparkPlan isFinalPlan=true"  ← AQE active
# Look for: "CustomShuffleReader coalesced"         ← partitions merged
# Look for: "BroadcastHashJoin" (was SortMergeJoin)  ← join converted
# Look for: "CustomShuffleReader skewed"             ← skew handled

# Note: before action → "isFinalPlan=false" (lazy, not yet executed)
# After action (df.count()) → "isFinalPlan=true" (all AQE decisions finalized)

24.6 Complete AQE Configuration Reference

ConfigurationDefaultDescription
spark.sql.adaptive.enabledtrue (3.2+)Master switch for AQE
spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabledtrueAuto-coalesce small shuffle partitions
spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes64mTarget size for coalesced partitions (increase to 128m-256m for ETL)
spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.minPartitionNum1Minimum partitions after coalescing
spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.parallelismFirsttruePrioritize parallelism over advisory size
spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabledtrueAuto-handle skewed partitions during joins
spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor5Partition skewed if size > median × this
spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes256mMinimum size to consider a partition skewed
spark.sql.adaptive.localShuffleReader.enabledtrueUse local shuffle reader after BHJ conversion
spark.sql.adaptive.forceApplyfalseForce AQE even for non-shuffle queries (testing)
spark.sql.adaptive.optimizer.excludedRules(none)Comma-separated AQE rules to exclude
Production AQE configuration template — copy thisPySpark
# =========================================
# PRODUCTION AQE CONFIG — copy/paste ready
# =========================================

# Master switch
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true")

# Coalescing — set shuffle partitions HIGH, AQE reduces
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.shuffle.partitions", "2000")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes", "134217728")  # 128 MB

# Skew join
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionFactor", "5")
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.skewedPartitionThresholdInBytes", "268435456")  # 256 MB

# Broadcast join threshold — benefits both Catalyst AND AQE
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", "268435456")  # 256 MB

# Local shuffle reader optimization
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.adaptive.localShuffleReader.enabled", "true")

24.7 When AQE Helps Most vs Least

✅ AQE Helps Most⚠️ AQE Helps Least
Queries with data skew in join keysQueries already manually optimized (broadcast hints, tuned partitions)
Queries where filter selectivity is unpredictableSimple single-stage reads with no shuffle
Complex multi-way joins with hard-to-estimate intermediatesRDD-based operations (bypass Catalyst entirely)
Workloads where shuffle.partitions was never tunedStreaming jobs with predictable data volumes
Ad-hoc analytics with varying data volumesQueries where CBO already has accurate statistics
Star-schema queries with selective dimension filtersVery short queries (<2s) where AQE overhead is noticeable

24.8 Troubleshooting AQE

IssueSymptomFix
AQE not activatingNo AdaptiveSparkPlan in explainQuery has no shuffle (single-stage). Normal — AQE only applies to multi-stage queries.
Coalescing not reducingStill 200 tasks after shuffleSet parallelismFirst=false or increase advisoryPartitionSizeInBytes
Join not converting to BHJSMJ in plan, one side is smallIncrease autoBroadcastJoinThreshold to 256 MB
Skew not handledOne task 10× longer than peersLower skewedPartitionFactor from 5 to 3 or lower threshold
AQE causing regressionQuery slower with AQE onDisable for specific query: SET spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=false
⚠️ Common Pitfall AQE only activates when there is at least one shuffle or broadcast exchange in the query. Single-table reads with no join or aggregation won't trigger AQE. Also, AQE adds a small overhead per stage boundary (~100-500ms for the pause-and-replan). For very short queries (<2s total), this overhead may be noticeable.
🎯 Interview Tip — Full Answer "What is AQE and why is it important?" — AQE is Spark 3's runtime query optimizer. It pauses at shuffle boundaries, collects actual partition statistics via MapOutputTracker, and re-plans downstream stages using AQEOptimizer rules. Three features: (1) coalesce small partitions (merge 200 → 8), (2) switch join strategy based on actual sizes (SMJ → BHJ), (3) split skewed partitions (5 GB → 20 × 250 MB). It eliminates manual tuning of shuffle.partitions, automates broadcast join selection, and handles data skew without salting code. Internally, it wraps the plan in AdaptiveSparkPlanExec with QueryStageExec boundaries at each ShuffleExchange. At scale (Netflix, Uber), it reduced median job runtime by 20-30%.
Part 10 — Section 25

Dynamic Partition Pruning (DPP)

Runtime pruning of fact table partitions using dimension table filter results — the star-schema query accelerator that can reduce I/O from terabytes to gigabytes.

25 — Dynamic Partition Pruning (DPP)

25.1 What Is Dynamic Partition Pruning?

Dynamic Partition Pruning (DPP) is a Spark 3.0+ optimization that prunes partitions of a large fact table at runtime based on the filter results from a small dimension table. It is specifically designed for star-schema queries — the dominant query pattern in data warehousing.

The core problem: In a star-schema join, you often filter a dimension table (e.g., WHERE date_dim.year = 2024 AND date_dim.quarter = 'Q4'), then join the filtered dimension with a massive fact table. Without DPP, Spark reads the entire fact table first, then discards non-matching partitions during the join. With DPP, Spark executes the dimension filter first, broadcasts the resulting keys, and uses them to skip reading irrelevant fact table files entirely.

Impact: For well-partitioned fact tables on cloud storage, DPP can reduce data read from terabytes to gigabytes — a 10-100× I/O reduction that translates directly to faster queries and lower costs.

⚙️ Engineering Insight DPP is most impactful on cloud object storage (S3, GCS, ADLS) where every file listing and every read has latency and cost. A star-schema query on a 2.5 TB fact table that prunes to 62 GB saves not just computation but also $12+ per query in S3 read costs. At Netflix and Uber scale with thousands of daily queries, DPP saves millions annually.

25.2 When DPP Applies — Prerequisites

DPP is automatically applied by Catalyst when all of these conditions are met:

  1. Star-schema join: A large partitioned fact table is joined with a smaller, filtered dimension table.
  2. Join key is a partition column: The join key on the fact table side must be a partition column of the fact table (e.g., order_date_key).
  3. Selective dimension filter: The filter on the dimension table is selective enough (few rows pass) to be useful as a runtime pruning filter.
  4. Broadcast-able dimension: The filtered dimension result is small enough to broadcast. DPP reuses the broadcast join mechanism.
  5. Config enabled: spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled=true (default true in Spark 3.0+).

25.3 DPP Internals — How It Works Step by Step

DPP works by injecting a runtime subquery filter (called DynamicPruningExpression) into the fact table scan. This subquery executes the dimension filter first, collects the matching keys, and uses them to prune fact table partitions before any data is read.

DPP in action — SQL exampleSQL
-- DPP is automatically applied here by Catalyst
-- No manual hints needed — just write the star-schema query

SELECT
    f.order_id,
    f.amount,
    f.customer_id,
    d.date_str,
    d.month_name,
    d.quarter
FROM fact_orders f
JOIN date_dim d ON f.order_date_key = d.date_key
WHERE d.year = 2024
  AND d.quarter = 'Q4'

-- What Spark does internally:
-- 1. Filters date_dim → 92 date_key values for Q4 2024
-- 2. Broadcasts those 92 keys as a DynamicPruningExpression
-- 3. fact_orders scan: ONLY reads partitions matching those 92 keys
-- 4. Skips all other partitions entirely — zero I/O for non-Q4 data
-- 
-- Prerequisite: fact_orders must be PARTITIONED BY (order_date_key)
-- If not partitioned by the join key, DPP cannot apply.

-- Verify DPP in the plan:
EXPLAIN EXTENDED
SELECT f.order_id, d.date_str
FROM fact_orders f
JOIN date_dim d ON f.order_date_key = d.date_key
WHERE d.year = 2024 AND d.quarter = 'Q4';
-- Look for: PartitionFilters: [dynamicpruningexpression(order_date_key#45)]

25.4 DPP vs Static Partition Pruning

Spark has always supported static partition pruning — when you write WHERE order_date = '2024-01-15', Catalyst pushes this filter to the file scan and only reads the matching partition. This requires the filter to be a literal constant known at compile time.

DPP extends this to cases where the pruning values are not known until runtime — they come from the result of another subquery (the dimension filter). This is why it's called "dynamic."

FeatureStatic Partition PruningDynamic Partition Pruning (DPP)
Filter sourceLiteral constant in WHERE clauseRuntime result from dimension subquery
Known when?Compile time (plan creation)Runtime (after dimension filter executes)
ExampleWHERE year = 2024JOIN dim ON key WHERE dim.quarter = 'Q4'
Spark versionAll versionsSpark 3.0+
Query patternDirect filter on partitioned columnStar-schema join with filtered dimension
ConfigurationAlways on (automatic)dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled=true
Plan indicatorPartitionFilters: [year = 2024]DynamicPruningExpression(key IN subquery)

25.5 DPP with Multiple Dimension Tables

DPP can apply to multiple dimension filters simultaneously in a single query. If the fact table is partitioned by multiple columns and each is joined with a filtered dimension, Spark can inject DPP filters for each partition column independently.

DPP with multiple dimensionsSQL
-- Fact table partitioned by BOTH order_date AND country_code
-- DPP applies to BOTH dimensions independently

SELECT
    f.order_id,
    f.amount,
    d.month_name,
    c.country_name
FROM fact_orders f                                   -- PARTITIONED BY (order_date, country_code)
JOIN date_dim d     ON f.order_date = d.date_key       -- DPP filter 1: prunes date partitions
JOIN country_dim c  ON f.country_code = c.country_key  -- DPP filter 2: prunes country partitions
WHERE d.year = 2024
  AND d.quarter = 'Q4'
  AND c.region = 'Europe'

-- Result: Spark reads ONLY partitions where:
--   order_date is in Q4 2024 (92 dates)
--   AND country_code is a European country (~44 codes)
-- Double pruning: 92 × 44 = ~4,000 partitions from millions
-- I/O reduction: potentially 99%+

25.6 DPP Configuration Reference

ConfigurationDefaultDescription
spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabledtrueMaster switch for DPP
spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.useStatstrueUse table statistics to decide whether DPP is beneficial
spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.fallbackFilterRatio0.5If no stats, apply DPP only if estimated filter ratio < this
spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.reuseBroadcastOnlytrueOnly use DPP when the broadcast exchange from a BHJ can be reused (no extra computation)
DPP configuration and verificationPySpark
# DPP is on by default in Spark 3.0+ — usually no config needed
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.enabled", "true")

# Verify DPP in the execution plan
df = spark.sql("""
    SELECT f.order_id, f.amount, d.date_str
    FROM fact_orders f
    JOIN date_dim d ON f.order_date_key = d.date_key
    WHERE d.year = 2024 AND d.quarter = 'Q4'
""")

df.explain("formatted")
# Look for: PartitionFilters: [dynamicpruningexpression(order_date_key#45)]
# This confirms DPP is active

# If DPP is NOT showing up, check:
# 1. Is fact table actually partitioned by the join column?
spark.sql("DESCRIBE EXTENDED fact_orders").show(truncate=False)
# Look for: Partition Columns: [order_date_key]

# 2. Is the dimension small enough for broadcast?
# Check autoBroadcastJoinThreshold — DPP reuses broadcast exchange
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold", "268435456")  # 256 MB

# 3. With reuseBroadcastOnly=true (default), DPP only works
#    when the join itself is a broadcast join. If the dim is too large
#    for broadcast → no DPP. Increase broadcast threshold to fix.

25.7 DPP + AQE — Combined Power

DPP and AQE are complementary features that work together for maximum optimization:

  • DPP reduces the amount of data read from storage (I/O reduction at the source)
  • AQE optimizes the query after data is read (runtime re-planning of stages)

In a star-schema query: DPP prunes 97% of fact table files → the remaining data after reading is much smaller → AQE sees actual sizes and can further optimize (e.g., convert the remaining join to BHJ, coalesce small partitions). The two features stack multiplicatively.

⚙️ Engineering Insight — Table Design for DPP To maximize DPP benefits, partition your fact tables by the most commonly filtered dimension join key. For e-commerce: partition by order_date. For ad-tech: partition by event_date. For multi-tenant SaaS: partition by tenant_id. The partition column must be the join key used with the dimension table, not just any column. If your fact table isn't partitioned or is partitioned by the wrong column, DPP cannot apply — this is the #1 reason DPP silently fails to activate.

25.8 DPP Performance Impact — Real Numbers

MetricWithout DPPWith DPPImprovement
Files scanned5,000 files120 files97.6% fewer
Data read from S32.5 TB62 GB40× less I/O
S3 GET requests~15,000~36042× fewer API calls
Query time18 minutes45 seconds24× faster
S3 cost per query$12.50$0.3042× cheaper
Executor memory peak28 GB/exec4 GB/exec7× less memory
Shuffle data1.8 TB45 GB40× less shuffle

Numbers based on a typical star-schema query on a 2.5 TB Parquet fact table partitioned by date, with a date dimension filter selecting Q4 2024 (25% of the year = 92 out of 365 days).

25.9 Troubleshooting DPP — Why DPP Doesn't Activate

IssueRoot CauseFix
No DynamicPruningExpression in planFact table not partitioned by join keyRepartition fact table by the join key used with dimension
DPP present but not pruningDimension filter is not selective (returns all keys)Add more selective WHERE on dimension table
DPP absent with SMJ joinreuseBroadcastOnly=true and join is SMJ (not broadcast)Increase autoBroadcastJoinThreshold to make dim broadcastable, or set reuseBroadcastOnly=false
DPP absent despite partitioned tableJoin key is a column expression (not raw partition col)Use the exact partition column name in the join condition
DPP active but slow improvementToo many matching partition values (low selectivity)DPP works best when dimension filter is highly selective (<10% of partitions)
DPP active but fact table still fully scannedFact table has bucket pruning but no partition pruningCheck DESCRIBE EXTENDED — verify partition columns, not just bucket columns
⚠️ Common Pitfall — reuseBroadcastOnly The default spark.sql.optimizer.dynamicPartitionPruning.reuseBroadcastOnly=true means DPP only works when the dimension side of the join is a Broadcast Hash Join. If the dimension table is too large for broadcast (exceeds autoBroadcastJoinThreshold), Spark falls back to Sort-Merge Join and DPP silently does NOT apply. Fix: increase the broadcast threshold so dimension tables are broadcast. Dimension tables in star schemas are typically 1-500 MB — well within broadcast range if you increase the threshold to 256 MB.
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Wrong Partition Column DPP prunes based on the physical partition column of the fact table. If your fact table is partitioned by event_date but you join on CAST(event_date AS INT) or date_format(event_date, 'yyyyMMdd'), DPP may not recognize the partition relationship. Use the exact partition column name and type in the join condition.
🎯 Interview Tip "Explain DPP and when it helps." — DPP prunes partitions of a large fact table at runtime using the result of a filtered dimension table join. It's designed for star-schema queries. Spark executes the dimension filter first, broadcasts the matching keys, and uses them to skip fact table files entirely — before any data is read. Prerequisites: (1) fact table partitioned by join key, (2) dimension filter is selective, (3) dimension side uses broadcast join. Combined with AQE, DPP can reduce I/O from terabytes to gigabytes. The key design takeaway: partition your fact tables by the most commonly filtered dimension join key — this is the single most impactful storage design decision for query performance.
🎯 Interview Tip — DPP vs Static Pruning If asked the difference: "Static partition pruning handles literal constants like WHERE year=2024 — known at plan time. DPP handles derived filters from dimension joins — the pruning values aren't known until the dimension subquery executes at runtime. DPP is a Spark 3.0+ feature that brings data-warehouse-style star-schema optimization to the lake."
Part 11 — Section 26

File Formats and Storage

Columnar vs row-based storage, Parquet internals, ORC, Avro, compression strategies, and the small file problem — choosing the right format is the single highest-impact data engineering decision.

26 — File Formats and Storage

26.1 Columnar vs Row-Based Storage

The most fundamental storage decision in data engineering is columnar vs row-based. This choice determines I/O patterns, compression ratios, and query performance for every downstream operation.

Row-based storage (CSV, JSON, Avro) stores all columns of a row together contiguously on disk. Reading a full row is fast (one seek), but reading only a few columns from a wide table means reading all columns — wasted I/O.

Columnar storage (Parquet, ORC) stores all values of a single column together contiguously. Reading only the needed columns is extremely efficient (skip unused columns entirely), and similar values in a column compress dramatically better than mixed-type row data.

26.2 Apache Parquet — The Spark Default

Parquet is the default and recommended file format in the Spark ecosystem. It is a columnar binary format designed for analytical workloads with strong support for schema evolution, predicate pushdown, and efficient compression.

Parquet Key Features:

  • Columnar layout with row groups (128 MB default) — each row group contains column chunks
  • Compression: Snappy (default), GZIP, LZ4, ZSTD — applied per column chunk
  • Encoding: Dictionary encoding, run-length encoding (RLE), bit packing, delta encoding — automatic per column data type
  • Min/max statistics per column chunk and per page — enables predicate pushdown at file/row-group/page level
  • Schema evolution: add/remove columns; backward and forward compatible
  • Nested data: Dremel encoding for complex types (structs, arrays, maps)
  • Splittable: row groups can be processed independently → parallel reading
Parquet read/write in SparkPySpark
# Write Parquet with optimal settings
df.write \
    .format("parquet") \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .option("compression", "snappy")  # snappy (default) | gzip | lz4 | zstd \
    .option("parquet.block.size", "134217728")  # 128 MB row group size \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .save("s3://bucket/warehouse/orders/")

# Read Parquet with predicate pushdown
df = spark.read.parquet("s3://bucket/warehouse/orders/") \
    .filter("year = 2024 AND month = 12") \    # Static partition pruning
    .select("order_id", "customer_id", "amount")  # Column projection (reads only 3 cols)

# Verify pushdown in plan
df.explain()
# Look for: PushedFilters: [IsNotNull(year), EqualTo(year,2024)]
# Look for: ReadSchema: struct<order_id:long,customer_id:string,amount:double>
# (only 3 columns in ReadSchema = projection pushdown working)

26.3 ORC — Hive Ecosystem Default

ORC (Optimized Row Columnar) is functionally similar to Parquet — columnar, compressed, with statistics. It was developed within the Hive project and is the default format in Hive-centric ecosystems. Key differences from Parquet:

  • Better default compression: ZLIB default (higher ratio) vs Parquet's Snappy (faster)
  • Built-in lightweight indexes: Bloom filters on stripes for faster point lookups
  • ACID support: Hive ACID transactions natively (base files + delta files)
  • Stripe-based layout (ORC "stripes" ≈ Parquet "row groups")

In the Spark ecosystem, Parquet is preferred — it has better Spark integration, wider ecosystem support (Arrow, DuckDB, Polars), and is the default for Delta Lake and Iceberg. Use ORC only when working in existing Hive-heavy environments.

26.4 Avro — Row-Based Binary Format

Apache Avro is a row-based binary format with an embedded JSON schema. Unlike Parquet/ORC, Avro stores data row-by-row — making it poor for analytical queries but excellent for serialization and streaming.

  • Schema embedded in file header (self-describing)
  • Best schema evolution: strongest forward/backward compatibility of any format
  • Kafka's default serialization: Avro + Schema Registry is the standard Kafka pattern
  • Write-optimized: fast sequential writes — ideal for event ingestion
  • Not columnar: no column pruning, no predicate pushdown — every query reads all columns

Use case: Avro is for landing / staging / transport, not for analytical querying. A common pattern: write Kafka events as Avro → ingest into Spark → convert to Parquet/Delta for analytics.

26.5 Format Comparison — Decision Matrix

FeatureParquetORCAvroCSVJSON
Storage layoutColumnarColumnarRow-basedRow-based (text)Row-based (text)
Best forAnalytics, MLAnalytics (Hive)Streaming, CDCData exchangeSemi-structured
Column pruning✅ Yes✅ Yes❌ No❌ No❌ No
Predicate pushdown✅ Yes (stats)✅ Yes (stats+bloom)❌ No❌ No❌ No
Compression ratio5-10×5-12×2-4×1× (none)1× (none)
Schema evolutionGoodGoodExcellentNoneFlexible (schema-less)
Splittable✅ Yes✅ Yes✅ Yes✅ Yes✅ (line-delimited)
Human-readable❌ No (binary)❌ No (binary)❌ No (binary)✅ Yes✅ Yes
Spark default✅ YesNoNoNoNo
Write speedModerateModerateFastFastFast
Read speed (analytics)Very fastVery fastSlowVery slowVery slow
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Compression Strategy Use Snappy (default) for most Spark workloads — it's 2-5× faster to decompress than GZIP with only 10-20% worse compression ratio. Use ZSTD when you need the best compression-to-speed trade-off (better ratio than Snappy, faster than GZIP). Use GZIP only when minimizing storage cost is the absolute priority (cold storage archives). Compression choice: spark.sql.parquet.compression.codec=snappy|gzip|lz4|zstd.

26.6 The Small File Problem

The small file problem is one of the most common production issues in data lakes. When writes produce thousands of small files (under 128 MB each), every downstream operation suffers:

  • Too many tasks: Spark creates one task per file — 10,000 small files = 10,000 tasks with scheduling overhead
  • S3 LIST latency: Cloud storage listing APIs are slow with many files — listing 100K files takes seconds
  • NameNode pressure (HDFS): each file consumes NameNode memory — millions of small files exhaust NameNode
  • Poor compression: small files can't take full advantage of column-level encoding and compression
  • Poor predicate pushdown: statistics are per file — many files with overlapping ranges = less effective skipping
Fixing small files in productionPySpark
# Fix 1: Coalesce before write (simplest)
df.coalesce(100).write.format("parquet").save(output_path)

# Fix 2: Repartition to target file size
# If you know total data size: target_files = total_bytes / 256MB
target_files = total_data_bytes // (256 * 1024 * 1024)
df.repartition(max(target_files, 1)).write.parquet(output_path)

# Fix 3: Delta Lake OPTIMIZE (best for production)
spark.sql("OPTIMIZE delta.`s3://bucket/delta/orders/`")

# Fix 4: Delta auto-compaction (Databricks)
spark.conf.set("spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.databricks.delta.autoCompact.enabled", "true")

# Fix 5: Iceberg compaction
spark.sql("CALL iceberg.system.rewrite_data_files('db.orders')")

# Fix 6: maxRecordsPerFile (cap records per output file)
df.write.option("maxRecordsPerFile", 1000000) \
    .format("parquet").save(output_path)
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Streaming Small Files Structured Streaming with a 30-second trigger creates a new file every 30 seconds per partition. For a table partitioned by event_type (10 types), that's 10 × 2,880 = 28,800 files per day. Fix: use Delta Lake with auto-compaction, or run a separate scheduled compaction job that merges small files daily. Alternatively, use trigger(availableNow=True) for batch-scheduled catch-up that produces fewer, larger files.
🎯 Interview Tip "Why Parquet over CSV/JSON?" — Three reasons: (1) Column pruning — read only needed columns (5-20× I/O savings on wide tables), (2) Predicate pushdown — skip entire files/row-groups using min/max statistics (100× I/O savings on filtered queries), (3) Compression — columnar data compresses 5-10× better than row data (same-type values compress efficiently). For an analytical workload on a 100-column table where queries touch 5 columns with a selective filter, Parquet can be 100-1000× faster than CSV.
Part 11 — Section 27

Data Lake Integration

HDFS, Amazon S3, Google Cloud Storage, Azure ADLS — storage layer connectivity and tuning. Plus Delta Lake, Iceberg, and Hudi — the table format layer bringing ACID transactions to the data lake.

27 — Data Lake Integration

27.1 Spark Storage Architecture Overview

Spark is a compute engine that is decoupled from storage. It can read from and write to many storage backends via Hadoop-compatible FileSystem APIs. The storage layer consists of two concerns: (1) the physical storage system where bytes live (HDFS, S3, GCS, ADLS) and (2) the table format that adds metadata, transactions, and schema on top of data files (Delta Lake, Iceberg, Hudi).

27.2 HDFS — Hadoop Distributed File System

HDFS is the original big data storage system, designed for Hadoop clusters. It is a block-based distributed file system where files are split into large blocks (128 MB or 256 MB) and replicated across DataNodes for fault tolerance.

Architecture: NameNode (metadata — tracks which blocks belong to which files and their locations) + DataNodes (store the actual data blocks). Default replication factor is 3 — each block stored on 3 different nodes.

Key advantage for Spark: Data locality. HDFS block locations are known to Spark, so the scheduler places tasks on the nodes where the data already resides — minimizing network transfer. This is the core reason HDFS was the dominant big data storage for a decade.

HDFS configuration for SparkPySpark + Config
# Read from HDFS (default for Hadoop clusters)
df = spark.read.parquet("hdfs://namenode:8020/warehouse/orders/")
df = spark.read.parquet("/warehouse/orders/")   # Shorthand if default FS is HDFS

# Key HDFS configs in spark-defaults.conf or core-site.xml
# fs.defaultFS = hdfs://namenode:8020         # Default filesystem
# dfs.replication = 3                          # Block replication factor
# dfs.blocksize = 134217728                    # 128 MB block size

# Locality tuning — how long Spark waits for data-local slot
spark.conf.set("spark.locality.wait", "3s")              # Default 3 sec
spark.conf.set("spark.locality.wait.node", "3s")         # Wait for NODE_LOCAL
spark.conf.set("spark.locality.wait.rack", "3s")         # Wait for RACK_LOCAL

# HDFS High Availability (HA) — connect via logical service name
# fs.defaultFS = hdfs://mycluster
# dfs.nameservices = mycluster
# dfs.ha.namenodes.mycluster = nn1,nn2
# dfs.namenode.rpc-address.mycluster.nn1 = namenode1:8020
# dfs.namenode.rpc-address.mycluster.nn2 = namenode2:8020

27.3 Amazon S3 — Cloud Object Storage

Amazon S3 is the most widely used cloud object storage. Spark connects via the s3a:// connector (Hadoop's S3A FileSystem). S3 is fundamentally different from HDFS: it is an object store, not a file system — no blocks, no data locality, eventual consistency for some operations.

Key differences from HDFS that affect Spark:

  • No data locality: All reads are network fetches. Tasks can run on any executor — locality tuning is irrelevant.
  • High latency metadata ops: S3 LIST is slow (~100-500ms per request). Many small files → many LIST calls → slow.
  • Strong consistency (since Dec 2020): S3 now provides read-after-write consistency for all operations.
  • Eventual rename: S3 does not support atomic rename. This affects Spark's commit protocol (use S3A committers).
  • Infinite scale: No capacity limits, no NameNode bottleneck — practically unlimited storage.
Amazon S3 configuration for SparkPySpark + Config
# Read/write from S3
df = spark.read.parquet("s3a://my-bucket/warehouse/orders/")
df.write.parquet("s3a://my-bucket/output/results/")

# === S3A CONNECTOR CONFIG ===
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.impl", "org.apache.hadoop.fs.s3a.S3AFileSystem")

# Authentication — Option 1: Instance profile / IAM Role (recommended on EMR/EKS)
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.aws.credentials.provider",
    "com.amazonaws.auth.InstanceProfileCredentialsProvider")

# Authentication — Option 2: Access keys (dev/testing only)
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.access.key", "AKIA...")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.secret.key", "secret...")

# Authentication — Option 3: Web Identity (K8s service account → IAM role)
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.aws.credentials.provider",
    "com.amazonaws.auth.WebIdentityTokenCredentialsProvider")

# === PERFORMANCE TUNING ===
# Enable S3A fast upload (stream directly, don't buffer entire file locally)
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.fast.upload", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.fast.upload.buffer", "bytebuffer")

# Increase read-ahead for large sequential reads
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.readahead.range", "2097152")  # 2 MB

# Connection pool for high parallelism
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.connection.maximum", "200")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.threads.max", "64")

# === COMMIT PROTOCOL (critical for correctness!) ===
# S3 doesn't support atomic rename — use S3A committers
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.committer.name", "magic")     # magic | directory | staging
spark.conf.set("spark.sql.sources.commitProtocolClass",
    "org.apache.spark.internal.io.cloud.PathOutputCommitProtocol")

# S3 endpoint for region/VPC endpoint
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.endpoint", "s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com")
# For S3-compatible storage (MinIO, Ceph):
# spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.endpoint = http://minio:9000
# spark.hadoop.fs.s3a.path.style.access = true
⚠️ Common Pitfall — S3 Commit Protocol S3 does not support atomic directory rename. Spark's default commit protocol uses rename to atomically publish output. On S3 this becomes a slow copy+delete, and can leave partial data visible on failure. Always use the S3A "magic" committer or Delta Lake / Iceberg (which handle commits via their own transaction log, bypassing rename entirely). Without a proper committer, you risk data corruption on job failure.

27.4 Google Cloud Storage (GCS)

Google Cloud Storage is GCP's object storage. Spark connects via the gs:// connector (GCS Hadoop connector). GCS provides strong consistency and has better metadata performance than S3 for LIST operations. It is the default storage on GCP Dataproc (Google's managed Spark).

GCS configuration for SparkPySpark + Config
# Read/write from GCS
df = spark.read.parquet("gs://my-bucket/warehouse/orders/")
df.write.parquet("gs://my-bucket/output/results/")

# GCS connector class
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.gs.impl",
    "com.google.cloud.hadoop.fs.gcs.GoogleHadoopFileSystem")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.AbstractFileSystem.gs.impl",
    "com.google.cloud.hadoop.fs.gcs.GoogleHadoopFS")

# Authentication — Option 1: Service account JSON key
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.google.cloud.auth.service.account.enable", "true")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.google.cloud.auth.service.account.json.keyfile",
    "/path/to/service-account.json")

# Authentication — Option 2: Application Default Credentials (on Dataproc, GKE)
# No config needed — ADC from metadata server is auto-detected

# Performance tuning
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.gs.block.size", "134217728")  # 128 MB logical block
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.gs.http.max.retry", "10")

# GCS connector JAR: gcs-connector-hadoop3-latest.jar
# Pre-installed on Dataproc. For self-managed: add to spark.jars

27.5 Azure ADLS Gen2

Azure Data Lake Storage Gen2 combines Azure Blob Storage with a hierarchical namespace (HNS) — giving it the performance characteristics of a file system while being cloud-native object storage. It is the default storage on Azure HDInsight, Azure Synapse, and Azure Databricks.

Spark connects via the abfss:// (ABFS driver with SSL) or abfs:// URI scheme.

Azure ADLS Gen2 configurationPySpark + Config
# Read/write from ADLS Gen2
df = spark.read.parquet("abfss://container@storageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net/warehouse/orders/")

# Authentication — Option 1: Storage Account Key
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.azure.account.key.storageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net",
    "")

# Authentication — Option 2: Azure AD Service Principal (OAuth2)
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.azure.account.auth.type.storageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net",
    "OAuth")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.azure.account.oauth.provider.type.storageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net",
    "org.apache.hadoop.fs.azurebfs.oauth2.ClientCredsTokenProvider")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.azure.account.oauth2.client.id.storageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net",
    "")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.azure.account.oauth2.client.secret.storageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net",
    "")
spark.conf.set("spark.hadoop.fs.azure.account.oauth2.client.endpoint.storageaccount.dfs.core.windows.net",
    "https://login.microsoftonline.com//oauth2/token")

# Authentication — Option 3: Managed Identity (Azure VMs, AKS)
# Automatically detected when running on Azure infra with MI enabled

27.6 Storage System Comparison

FeatureHDFSAmazon S3Google GCSAzure ADLS Gen2
TypeBlock file systemObject storeObject storeObject store + HNS
URI schemehdfs://s3a://gs://abfss://
Data locality✅ Yes (core strength)❌ No❌ No❌ No
ConsistencyStrongStrong (since 2020)StrongStrong
Atomic rename✅ Yes (O(1))❌ No (copy+delete)❌ No✅ Yes (HNS)
LIST performanceFast (NameNode memory)Slow (100ms+ per req)GoodGood (HNS)
ScalabilityLimited by NameNodeVirtually unlimitedVirtually unlimitedVirtually unlimited
Cost ($/TB/mo)~$30 (hardware)~$23 (Standard)~$20 (Standard)~$18 (Hot)
Managed SparkOn-prem HadoopAWS EMR, DatabricksGCP DataprocAzure HDInsight, Databricks
Best forOn-prem, locality-sensitiveAWS ecosystemGCP ecosystemAzure ecosystem
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Cloud Storage vs HDFS The industry has moved decisively from HDFS to cloud object storage (S3/GCS/ADLS). The reasons: (1) Infinite scalability — no NameNode bottleneck. (2) Pay-per-use — no provisioning. (3) Separation of compute and storage — spin up Spark clusters on-demand, point them at S3. (4) Durability — 99.999999999% (11 nines). The trade-off: loss of data locality. But modern cloud networking (25-100 Gbps within AZs) and columnar formats (read only needed columns) minimize this impact. New Spark projects should use cloud object storage unless there's a compelling reason for HDFS.

27.7 Why Table Formats? — The Problem with Raw Files

Raw Parquet or ORC files on any storage (HDFS, S3, GCS) have no transactional guarantees. Writing 500 Parquet files to S3 is not atomic — if the job fails at file 400, readers see partial data. There is no isolation between concurrent readers/writers. There is no UPDATE or DELETE support. No schema enforcement.

Open table formats solve this with a metadata/transaction log layer on top of data files:

  • ACID transactions: atomic commits — a write fully succeeds or has no effect
  • Snapshot isolation: readers see consistent data even while writers are active
  • Update, Delete, Merge (Upsert): row-level mutations
  • Schema enforcement and evolution: type-safe writes
  • Time travel: query historical versions

27.8 Delta Lake

Delta Lake (by Databricks) adds ACID transactions via a JSON-based transaction log (_delta_log/ directory). Every write creates a new JSON commit file listing files added/removed. The current table state is the cumulative replay of all commits. Checkpoints every 10 commits compact the log for faster reads.

Key features: ACID transactions, time travel (VERSION AS OF), schema enforcement, MERGE/upsert, OPTIMIZE + ZORDER, VACUUM for old file cleanup, Change Data Feed (CDC), optimistic concurrency.

Delta Lake production patternsPySpark
# Write Delta (works on S3, GCS, ADLS, HDFS — same API)
df.write.format("delta").mode("overwrite") \
    .partitionBy("year", "month") \
    .save("s3a://bucket/delta/orders/")        # or gs:// or abfss:// or hdfs://

# MERGE / Upsert (CDC pattern)
from delta.tables import DeltaTable
dt = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, "s3a://bucket/delta/orders/")
dt.alias("t").merge(updates.alias("s"), "t.order_id = s.order_id") \
    .whenMatchedUpdateAll().whenNotMatchedInsertAll().execute()

# Time travel
spark.read.format("delta").option("versionAsOf", 5).load("s3a://bucket/delta/orders/")

# OPTIMIZE (compact small files + Z-order for multi-column skipping)
spark.sql("OPTIMIZE delta.`s3a://bucket/delta/orders/` ZORDER BY (customer_id)")

# VACUUM (remove old files beyond retention)
dt.vacuum(retentionHours=168)  # 7 days

27.9 Apache Iceberg

Apache Iceberg is an engine-agnostic open standard supported equally by Spark, Flink, Trino, Presto, and Hive. Key differentiators: hidden partitioning (partition transforms derived from data columns — users never reference partitions in queries), partition evolution (change partition layout without rewriting data), row-level deletes (V2 delete files), and snapshot branching/tagging (Git-like data versioning).

Iceberg operationsSQL
-- Create with hidden partitioning (users never reference partition columns)
CREATE TABLE catalog.db.orders (
    order_id BIGINT, customer_id STRING, amount DOUBLE, order_date DATE
) USING iceberg
PARTITIONED BY (months(order_date), bucket(16, customer_id))

-- Partition evolution (no data rewrite!)
ALTER TABLE catalog.db.orders ADD PARTITION FIELD days(order_date)

-- MERGE
MERGE INTO catalog.db.orders t USING updates s ON t.order_id = s.order_id
WHEN MATCHED THEN UPDATE SET * WHEN NOT MATCHED THEN INSERT *

-- Compaction
CALL catalog.system.rewrite_data_files('db.orders')

27.10 Apache Hudi

Apache Hudi (from Uber) is optimized for write-heavy CDC and incremental processing. Two table types: Copy-on-Write (CoW) — rewrites files on update (fast reads, slow writes); Merge-on-Read (MoR) — writes delta logs on update, merges at read time (fast writes, slower reads, background compaction merges deltas). Unique strength: incremental queries — read only data changed since last checkpoint, ideal for CDC pipelines (Debezium → Kafka → Hudi).

27.11 Table Format Comparison — Delta vs Iceberg vs Hudi

FeatureDelta LakeApache IcebergApache Hudi
OriginDatabricks (2019)Netflix → Apache (2018)Uber → Apache (2019)
Transaction logJSON in _delta_log/JSON + Avro snapshotsTimeline in .hoodie/
ACID✅ Serializable✅ Snapshot✅ Snapshot
Time travel✅ VERSION / TIMESTAMP✅ Snapshot IDs✅ Point-in-time
Partition evolution❌ Must rewrite✅ Without data rewrite❌ Must rewrite
Hidden partitioning❌ Explicit✅ Partition transforms❌ Explicit
Incremental queries✅ Change Data Feed✅ Incremental scan✅ Best-in-class
Engine supportSpark (best), Flink, TrinoSpark, Flink, Trino, Presto, HiveSpark, Flink, Presto
Storage supportHDFS, S3, GCS, ADLSHDFS, S3, GCS, ADLSHDFS, S3, GCS, ADLS
Best forDatabricks, Spark-firstMulti-engine, vendor-neutralCDC-heavy, write-intensive

27.12 Other Storage Integrations

27.12.1 JDBC / RDBMS Sources

Spark can read from and write to any JDBC-compatible database (PostgreSQL, MySQL, Oracle, SQL Server). Used for: ingesting relational data into the lake, writing aggregated results back to OLTP databases for serving.

JDBC read/writePySpark
# Read from PostgreSQL with parallel partitioned fetch
jdbc_df = spark.read.format("jdbc") \
    .option("url", "jdbc:postgresql://host:5432/mydb") \
    .option("dbtable", "public.orders") \
    .option("user", "spark_reader") \
    .option("password", "${DB_PASSWORD}") \
    .option("partitionColumn", "order_id") \
    .option("lowerBound", "1") \
    .option("upperBound", "10000000") \
    .option("numPartitions", "50") \
    .option("fetchsize", "10000") \
    .load()
# numPartitions=50 → 50 parallel JDBC connections fetching ranges

# Write back to RDBMS
result_df.write.format("jdbc") \
    .option("url", "jdbc:postgresql://host:5432/mydb") \
    .option("dbtable", "public.daily_metrics") \
    .option("user", "spark_writer") \
    .option("password", "${DB_PASSWORD}") \
    .mode("overwrite") \
    .save()
⚠️ Common Pitfall — JDBC Parallelism Without partitionColumn, numPartitions, lowerBound, and upperBound, Spark reads the entire table through a single JDBC connection in one task — extremely slow for large tables. Always set all four options to enable parallel partitioned reads. Each partition opens a separate connection executing WHERE order_id BETWEEN lower AND upper.

27.12.2 Apache Kafka

Kafka integration is core to Spark Structured Streaming. Spark reads Kafka topics as a streaming source and can also write back to Kafka as a sink. Batch mode reading from Kafka is also supported for historical processing.

Kafka integrationPySpark
# Streaming read from Kafka
kafka_stream = spark.readStream.format("kafka") \
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "broker1:9092,broker2:9092") \
    .option("subscribe", "user_events,page_views") \
    .option("startingOffsets", "latest") \
    .option("maxOffsetsPerTrigger", 500000) \
    .option("kafka.security.protocol", "SASL_SSL") \
    .option("kafka.sasl.mechanism", "AWS_MSK_IAM") \
    .load()
# Returns: key, value (binary), topic, partition, offset, timestamp

# Batch read from Kafka (historical replay)
kafka_batch = spark.read.format("kafka") \
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "broker:9092") \
    .option("subscribe", "orders") \
    .option("startingOffsets", "earliest") \
    .option("endingOffsets", "latest") \
    .load()

# Write to Kafka
result_df.selectExpr("CAST(key AS STRING)", "to_json(struct(*)) AS value") \
    .writeStream.format("kafka") \
    .option("kafka.bootstrap.servers", "broker:9092") \
    .option("topic", "enriched_events") \
    .start()

27.12.3 Apache Cassandra

Spark connects to Cassandra via the spark-cassandra-connector. Used for: enriching streaming data with Cassandra lookups, bulk-loading analytics results into Cassandra for serving.

Cassandra integrationPySpark
# spark-submit --packages com.datastax.spark:spark-cassandra-connector_2.12:3.4.1
spark.conf.set("spark.cassandra.connection.host", "cassandra-host")
spark.conf.set("spark.cassandra.auth.username", "spark_user")
spark.conf.set("spark.cassandra.auth.password", "${CASSANDRA_PASSWORD}")

# Read from Cassandra (predicate pushdown on partition key)
df = spark.read.format("org.apache.spark.sql.cassandra") \
    .options(table="user_profiles", keyspace="analytics") \
    .load() \
    .filter("country = 'US'")  # Pushed to Cassandra if 'country' is partition key

# Write to Cassandra
result_df.write.format("org.apache.spark.sql.cassandra") \
    .options(table="daily_metrics", keyspace="analytics") \
    .mode("append").save()

27.12.4 MongoDB

Spark connects to MongoDB via the official mongo-spark-connector. Supports both batch and streaming reads, filter pushdown on indexed fields, and write-back for serving layers.

MongoDB integrationPySpark
# spark-submit --packages org.mongodb.spark:mongo-spark-connector_2.12:10.2.1
df = spark.read.format("mongodb") \
    .option("connection.uri", "mongodb+srv://user:pass@cluster.mongodb.net/") \
    .option("database", "analytics") \
    .option("collection", "events") \
    .load()

# Write results back
result_df.write.format("mongodb") \
    .option("connection.uri", "mongodb+srv://user:pass@cluster/") \
    .option("database", "analytics") \
    .option("collection", "daily_summary") \
    .mode("overwrite").save()

27.12.5 Elasticsearch

Spark reads/writes Elasticsearch indices via the elasticsearch-spark connector. Common pattern: Spark ETL writes aggregated data to ES for fast full-text search and dashboard queries.

Elasticsearch integrationPySpark
# spark-submit --packages org.elasticsearch:elasticsearch-spark-30_2.12:8.12.0
df = spark.read.format("org.elasticsearch.spark.sql") \
    .option("es.nodes", "es-host:9200") \
    .option("es.resource", "orders/_doc") \
    .option("es.read.metadata", "true") \
    .load()

result_df.write.format("org.elasticsearch.spark.sql") \
    .option("es.nodes", "es-host:9200") \
    .option("es.resource", "daily_metrics/_doc") \
    .mode("overwrite").save()

27.12.6 Redis, DynamoDB, and Other Stores

StoreConnectorUse CaseURI / Format
Redisspark-redisFeature store, fast key-value lookups.format("org.apache.spark.sql.redis")
DynamoDBemr-dynamodb-connectorAWS native key-value store.format("dynamodb") on EMR
HBasehbase-sparkWide-column store for random access.format("org.apache.hadoop.hbase.spark")
Snowflakespark-snowflakeCloud data warehouse read/write.format("net.snowflake.spark.snowflake")
BigQueryspark-bigquery-connectorGCP data warehouse read/write.format("bigquery")
Redshiftspark-redshiftAWS data warehouse.format("io.github.spark_redshift_community.spark.redshift")
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Choosing Where to Store What Rule of thumb: Object storage (S3/GCS/ADLS) + table format (Delta/Iceberg) is the system of record for all analytical data. RDBMS (PostgreSQL/MySQL) stores transactional operational data. Kafka is the real-time transport layer. Cassandra/DynamoDB/Redis serve low-latency lookups. Elasticsearch serves search. Spark is the compute engine that reads from any source, transforms, and writes to any sink. The data lake (object storage + table format) is the gravity center.
🎯 Interview Tip "How does Spark connect to different storage systems?" — "Spark uses the Hadoop FileSystem API as its storage abstraction. Each storage backend (HDFS, S3, GCS, ADLS) implements this API via a connector. For S3 it's the s3a:// connector, for GCS it's gs://, for ADLS it's abfss://. The key difference: HDFS provides data locality (tasks scheduled where data lives), while cloud object storage doesn't — but modern cloud networking and columnar formats minimize this gap. On top of any storage, table formats (Delta/Iceberg/Hudi) add ACID transactions, schema enforcement, and time travel via a metadata log layer. For external systems like Kafka, JDBC, Cassandra, Spark uses DataSource V2 connectors with filter/projection pushdown."
Part 12 — Section 28

Spark UI and Debugging

Navigating the Spark Web UI, reading stage/task metrics, diagnosing data skew, GC pressure, shuffle bottlenecks, and systematic debugging strategies for production jobs.

28 — Spark UI and Debugging

28.1 Spark UI Overview

The Spark Web UI is your primary debugging tool for understanding job execution, identifying bottlenecks, and diagnosing failures. It runs at http://driver-host:4040 during application execution. For completed jobs, use the History Server at http://history-server:18080.

28.2 Reading Stage-Level Metrics

The Stages tab is where most debugging happens. Click on a stage to see task-level details. Key metrics to examine:

MetricWhat It ShowsWarning ThresholdFix
Duration (max vs median)Time for slowest vs typical taskMax > 5× median = skewEnable AQE skew join / repartition
