# ============================================
# JOIN — Spark detects co-bucketed → NO shuffle
# ============================================
result = spark.table("bucketed_orders") \
    .join(spark.table("bucketed_customers"), "customer_id")

result.explain()
# Plan shows: SortMergeJoin WITHOUT Exchange (no shuffle!)
# If Exchange IS present → bucketing not detected (check bucket count match)

# Verify bucketing metadata
spark.sql("DESCRIBE EXTENDED bucketed_orders").show(truncate=False)
# Shows: Num Buckets=64, Bucket Columns=[customer_id], Sort Columns=[customer_id]

38.2 Bucketing vs Partitioning vs Z-Ordering

FeaturePartitioningBucketingZ-Ordering
MechanismDirectory-per-value on storageHash into N fixed bucket filesSort by space-filling curve within files
Best forRange queries on time/date columnsRepeated joins on same keyMulti-column filter queries
EliminatesReading irrelevant partitions (I/O)Shuffle on join (network)File scanning (via min/max statistics)
Column cardinalityLow (year/month/country)High (customer_id, order_id)Any (works with stats-based skipping)
Works withAll table formatsHive Metastore tables onlyDelta Lake OPTIMIZE, Iceberg sort order
Data rewrite needed?At write time (natural)At write time (extra sort cost)Post-write via OPTIMIZE command
Sweet spotPartition by time + filter by timeBucket by join key + join repeatedlyZ-order by filter cols + query any combo
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Bucketing Requirements (1) Both tables must have the exact same bucket count — 64 and 128 are NOT compatible. (2) Must use .saveAsTable() — bucketing metadata is stored in the Hive Metastore; .save() to a path loses the metadata. (3) Must read via spark.table("name") — reading via spark.read.parquet(path) bypasses Metastore and bucketing is not detected. (4) Bucketing adds write cost (sort + hash at write time) — only beneficial when the table is joined repeatedly. For one-time joins, repartition at join time is simpler.
🎯 Interview Tip "Explain bucketing and when to use it." — "Bucketing pre-shuffles data at write time by hashing a key column into N fixed bucket files. When two tables are co-bucketed (same column, same bucket count), Spark can join them with zero shuffle — each bucket joins locally. The hash function hash(key) % N ensures the same keys land in the same bucket across both tables. Use bucketing when: (1) the same tables are joined repeatedly on the same key, (2) the join key has high cardinality (not suitable for partitioning). Requirements: saveAsTable() to store metadata in Hive Metastore, same bucket count on both sides. Trade-off: write cost (sort + hash) amortized over many joins."
Part 17 — Section 39

Z-Ordering & Data Clustering

Space-filling curve data layout for multi-column file skipping — how Z-ordering clusters data so that filters on any combination of Z-ordered columns benefit from Parquet min/max statistics.

39 — Z-Ordering and Data Clustering

39.1 What Is Z-Ordering?

Z-ordering (also called Z-curve sorting) is a data clustering technique that uses a space-filling curve to sort multi-dimensional data into a linear order that preserves spatial locality. In data lakes, this means sorting data within files such that rows with similar values across multiple columns end up in the same file.

Why it matters: Parquet files have per-column min/max statistics per row group. When data is randomly distributed, every file's min/max range overlaps with every query filter — no files can be skipped. After Z-ordering, files have tight, non-overlapping min/max ranges — queries that filter on any Z-ordered column can skip 80-95% of files.

Key difference from linear sorting: If you sort by column A only, queries on A are fast (file skipping works) but queries on column B gain nothing (B values are randomly spread). Z-ordering interleaves the bits of A and B so that filters on A, B, or both A+B all benefit from file skipping.

Z-ordering in Delta Lake and IcebergSQL + PySpark
# ============================================
# DELTA LAKE: OPTIMIZE + ZORDER
# ============================================
spark.sql("""
    OPTIMIZE delta.`s3://bucket/delta/events/`
    ZORDER BY (country, event_type, user_id)
""")
# After OPTIMIZE: files contain data clustered by Z-curve
# Queries filtering on ANY subset of these columns skip files

# Query that benefits from Z-ordering:
result = spark.read.format("delta").load("s3://bucket/delta/events/") \
    .filter("country = 'US' AND event_type = 'purchase'")
# Without ZORDER: reads 500 files (random layout)
# After ZORDER:  reads ~10 files (clustered layout) — 50× fewer

# ============================================
# ICEBERG: Write ordering (equivalent concept)
# ============================================
spark.sql("""
    ALTER TABLE iceberg_catalog.db.events
    WRITE ORDERED BY country, event_type
""")
spark.sql("CALL iceberg_catalog.system.rewrite_data_files('db.events')")

# ============================================
# BEST PRACTICES
# ============================================
# • Z-order by 2-4 most frequently filtered columns
# • Run OPTIMIZE periodically (daily for active tables)
# • Combine with PARTITION BY for two-level optimization:
#   PARTITION BY (year, month) + ZORDER BY (country, user_id)
#   → partition pruning first, then Z-order skipping within partitions
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Z-Ordering + Partitioning = Dual-Level Optimization The most effective data layout combines partitioning + Z-ordering. Partition by the most common time-based filter (e.g., PARTITION BY (year, month)). Then Z-order within each partition by the most common non-time filters (e.g., ZORDER BY (country, event_type)). This gives two levels of data skipping: (1) partition pruning eliminates entire directories, (2) Z-ordering eliminates files within the surviving partitions. Combined, you can reduce a 10 TB scan to under 100 GB.
🎯 Interview Tip "What is Z-ordering and how is it different from sorting?" — "Z-ordering uses a space-filling curve (Z-curve) to sort multi-dimensional data into a single linear order that preserves locality across ALL dimensions. If you sort by column A, only queries on A benefit from file skipping. Z-ordering on (A, B, C) means queries on A, B, C, or any combination all benefit from Parquet min/max statistics-based file skipping. It works because the Z-curve interleaves bits from each column, keeping nearby values in multi-dimensional space close in the linear file layout. In Delta Lake: OPTIMIZE ... ZORDER BY (col1, col2). Best practice: Z-order by 2-4 most commonly filtered columns."
Part 18 — Section 40

Spark Observability & Metrics

The Dropwizard metrics system, Prometheus integration, Grafana dashboards, StreamingQueryListener for streaming monitoring, and key metrics every production team should alert on.

40 — Observability and Metrics

40.1 Spark Metrics Architecture

Spark uses the Dropwizard Metrics library internally to collect counters, gauges, histograms, timers, and meters across all components (Driver, Executors, DAG Scheduler, BlockManager, etc.). These metrics can be exported to multiple sinks for monitoring and alerting.

40.2 Key Metrics to Monitor

MetricWhat It MeasuresAlert ThresholdAction
executor.jvm.heap.usedJVM heap usage per executor> 85% of max heapIncrease executor-memory or reduce cache
executor.jvm.GC.timeGC time per executor> 10% of task timeUse G1GC, off-heap, reduce object creation
executor.threadpool.activeTasksActive task count per executorConsistently 0 = idle executorCheck for skew; reduce executor count
executor.shuffleWrite.bytesShuffle data written per executorVery high = shuffle bottleneckAdd broadcast join; pre-filter
executor.shuffleRead.bytesShuffle data read per executorHighly unequal across executors = skewEnable AQE skew join
driver.DAGScheduler.job.activeJobsNumber of concurrent jobsUnexpectedly high = runaway queriesCheck for accidental actions in loops
executor.filesystem.s3.read.bytesS3 data read rateExtremely high = missing partition pruningAdd partition filter; enable DPP
streaming.inputRateStreaming input rows per secondProcessing rate < input rateAdd executors; optimize query
streaming.batchDurationTime to process each micro-batch> trigger interval (falling behind)Increase resources or increase trigger

40.3 Streaming Monitoring with StreamingQueryListener

Programmatic streaming metrics and alertingPySpark
from pyspark.sql.streaming import StreamingQueryListener

class ProductionMetricsListener(StreamingQueryListener):
    def onQueryStarted(self, event):
        print(f"[STREAM] Query started: {event.id} — {event.name}")

    def onQueryProgress(self, event):
        p = event.progress
        input_rate = p.inputRowsPerSecond
        process_rate = p.processedRowsPerSecond
        batch_ms = p.durationMs.get("triggerExecution", 0)

        # Emit to Prometheus / StatsD / CloudWatch
        emit_metric("spark.streaming.input_rate", input_rate)
        emit_metric("spark.streaming.process_rate", process_rate)
        emit_metric("spark.streaming.batch_ms", batch_ms)

        # Alert if falling behind
        if process_rate > 0 and input_rate > process_rate * 1.2:
            alert_pagerduty(f"Streaming lag! Input: {input_rate:.0f}/s > Process: {process_rate:.0f}/s")

        # Alert on state size growth (stateful streaming)
        for state_op in p.stateOperators:
            state_mb = state_op.get("memoryUsedBytes", 0) / 1e6
            if state_mb > 5000:  # > 5 GB state
                alert_slack(f"State size warning: {state_mb:.0f} MB. Check watermark.")

    def onQueryTerminated(self, event):
        if event.exception:
            alert_pagerduty(f"STREAM TERMINATED: {event.exception}")

spark.streams.addListener(ProductionMetricsListener())
Prometheus + Grafana setup for SparkConfig
# spark-metrics.properties (place in $SPARK_HOME/conf/)
*.sink.prometheusServlet.class=org.apache.spark.metrics.sink.PrometheusServletSink
*.sink.prometheusServlet.path=/metrics/prometheus
*.source.jvm.class=org.apache.spark.metrics.source.JvmSource

# prometheus.yml — scrape config
scrape_configs:
  - job_name: 'spark'
    scrape_interval: 15s
    static_configs:
      - targets: ['driver:4040']
    metrics_path: /metrics/prometheus

# For dynamic executor discovery on K8s:
  - job_name: 'spark-executors'
    kubernetes_sd_configs:
      - role: pod
    relabel_configs:
      - source_labels: [__meta_kubernetes_pod_label_spark_role]
        regex: executor
        action: keep
⚙️ Engineering Insight — The Three Metrics That Matter Most For batch jobs: (1) Stage duration distribution — identifies skew (one task >> others). (2) GC time / task time ratio — if >10%, memory tuning needed. (3) Shuffle write bytes — high shuffle = optimization opportunity. For streaming: (1) Processing rate vs input rate — falling behind = add resources. (2) Batch duration vs trigger interval — if batch > trigger, backlog growing. (3) State size — unbounded growth = missing watermark. Monitor these six metrics and you'll catch 90%+ of production issues before they become outages.
🎯 Interview Tip "How do you monitor Spark in production?" — "Three layers: (1) Spark UI / History Server for interactive debugging — stages, tasks, SQL plans, executor metrics. (2) Prometheus + Grafana for continuous monitoring — Spark exposes Dropwizard metrics via PrometheusServletSink at /metrics/prometheus. I create dashboards for GC time, heap usage, shuffle I/O, and active tasks. (3) StreamingQueryListener for streaming — programmatic access to input rate, processing rate, batch duration, and state size. Alerts go to PagerDuty when processing falls behind input or state grows unexpectedly."
Part 18 — Section 41

Advanced Structured Streaming Patterns

Stateful stream processing with mapGroupsWithState, stream-stream joins with watermarks, CDC pipelines (Debezium → Kafka → Delta Lake MERGE), and foreachBatch for custom sink logic.

41 — Advanced Streaming Patterns

41.1 Stream-Stream Join with Watermarks

Stream-stream joins match events from two live streams within a time-bounded window. Both sides buffer unmatched events in state. Watermarks define when old events can be evicted from state — without watermarks, state grows unbounded.

Common pattern: Match ad impressions with clicks within 1 hour. State stores unmatched impressions and clicks. When watermark passes impression_time + 1h + late_tolerance, that impression is evicted (no click arrived = no conversion).

Stream-stream join — impressions ↔ clicksPySpark
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

# Stream A: impressions
impressions = spark.readStream.format("kafka") \
    .option("subscribe", "impressions").load() \
    .select(F.from_json("value", imp_schema).alias("i")).select("i.*") \
    .withWatermark("impression_time", "2 hours")

# Stream B: clicks
clicks = spark.readStream.format("kafka") \
    .option("subscribe", "clicks").load() \
    .select(F.from_json("value", click_schema).alias("c")).select("c.*") \
    .withWatermark("click_time", "2 hours")

# Join: match click within 1 hour after impression
joined = impressions.join(
    clicks,
    F.expr("""
        impression_id = click_impression_id AND
        click_time >= impression_time AND
        click_time <= impression_time + INTERVAL 1 HOUR
    """),
    how="leftOuter"  # Keep unmatched impressions (no conversion)
)
# State bounded by watermarks → old events evicted automatically
# State size ≈ event_rate × retention_window

41.2 CDC Pipeline — Debezium → Kafka → Delta Lake MERGE

The Change Data Capture (CDC) pattern streams database changes (INSERT/UPDATE/DELETE) from an OLTP database through Kafka into a Delta Lake table using foreachBatch with MERGE.

CDC pipeline with foreachBatch + Delta MERGEPySpark
from delta.tables import DeltaTable
from pyspark.sql import functions as F

cdc_schema = StructType([
    StructField("op", StringType()),       # c=create, u=update, d=delete
    StructField("before", MapType(StringType(), StringType())),
    StructField("after", MapType(StringType(), StringType())),
    StructField("ts_ms", LongType())
])

raw_cdc = spark.readStream.format("kafka") \
    .option("subscribe", "db.public.orders").load() \
    .select(F.from_json(F.col("value").cast("string"), cdc_schema).alias("cdc")) \
    .select("cdc.*")

def process_cdc_batch(batch_df, batch_id):
    if batch_df.isEmpty():
        return

    delta_table = DeltaTable.forPath(spark, "s3://bucket/delta/orders/")

    # Upserts (create + update)
    upserts = batch_df.filter("op IN ('c', 'u', 'r')") \
        .select(F.col("after.*"))

    if upserts.count() > 0:
        delta_table.alias("t").merge(
            upserts.alias("s"), "t.order_id = s.order_id"
        ).whenMatchedUpdateAll() \
         .whenNotMatchedInsertAll() \
         .execute()

    # Deletes (soft delete with flag)
    deletes = batch_df.filter("op = 'd'").select(F.col("before.order_id"))
    if deletes.count() > 0:
        delta_table.alias("t").merge(
            deletes.alias("s"), "t.order_id = s.order_id"
        ).whenMatchedUpdate(set={"is_deleted": F.lit(True)}).execute()

raw_cdc.writeStream \
    .foreachBatch(process_cdc_batch) \
    .option("checkpointLocation", "s3://bucket/checkpoints/cdc_orders/") \
    .trigger(processingTime="30 seconds") \
    .start()

41.3 Stateful Processing — mapGroupsWithState

mapGroupsWithState (Scala) and applyInPandasWithState (PySpark 3.4+) enable custom per-key stateful logic — session tracking, fraud scoring, complex event processing. Each key maintains its own state across micro-batches, with configurable timeouts for state expiration.

Pattern: Each micro-batch groups events by key, calls your function with (key, new_events, existing_state), and you return updated state + optional output. State is persisted in the state store (in-memory or RocksDB) across micro-batches.

⚙️ Engineering Insight — State Store Sizing State size = unique_keys × state_per_key × retention_window. For 10M unique user sessions with 1 KB state each and a 24h retention window, state is ~10 GB per executor. For large state (>5 GB per executor), use RocksDB state store (spark.sql.streaming.stateStore.providerClass=...RocksDBStateStoreProvider) — it spills to local SSD instead of holding everything in JVM heap. See Section 48 for RocksDB deep dive.
🎯 Interview Tip "How do stream-stream joins work in Spark?" — "Both sides buffer unmatched events in a state store (RocksDB or in-memory). The join condition includes a time range constraint (e.g., click within 1 hour of impression). Watermarks on both sides define how long to keep buffered events — when the watermark advances past an event's time + the join range, that event is evicted. State size ≈ event_rate × retention_window. Without watermarks, state grows unbounded → OOM. The key design decision is the watermark delay — too short drops valid late data, too long wastes memory."
Part 19 — Section 42

Spark on Kubernetes

Production K8s deployment — Driver/Executor as Pods, SparkOperator, dynamic allocation, spot instances, volume mounts, RBAC, and cost optimization with node pools.

42 — Spark on Kubernetes

42.1 Architecture — Driver and Executors as Pods

On Kubernetes, the Driver runs as a K8s Pod and dynamically creates Executor Pods via the K8s API. Native support since Spark 2.3; production-ready from Spark 3.1+. Benefits: fine-grained resource isolation (Pod-level), auto-scaling via K8s HPA, namespace-level multi-tenancy, and containerized dependency management.

Spark on K8s — spark-submitConfig
spark-submit \
  --master k8s://https://k8s-api-server:443 \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --name revenue-etl \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.container.image=my-registry/spark:3.5.0 \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.namespace=spark-prod \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.authenticate.driver.serviceAccountName=spark-driver \
  --conf spark.executor.instances=20 \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.executor.request.cores=4 \
  --conf spark.executor.memory=16g \
  --conf spark.executor.memoryOverhead=4g \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.enabled=true \
  --conf spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled=true \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.node.selector.spark-role=executor \
  local:///opt/spark/jobs/revenue_etl.py
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Spot Instance Strategy Run Driver on on-demand nodes (never preempted) and Executors on spot/preemptible nodes (60-80% cheaper). Enable spark.dynamicAllocation.shuffleTracking.enabled=true (Spark 3.0+) to avoid needing an External Shuffle Service — Spark tracks which executors hold shuffle data and won't deallocate them until downstream stages complete. This eliminates the ESS operational burden on K8s.
Part 19 — Section 43

Data Lineage & Governance

OpenLineage integration, column-level lineage capture, Delta Lake audit logs, Unity Catalog, DataHub, and building a governed data platform.

43 — Data Lineage and Governance

43.1 Lineage Capture Mechanisms

Data lineage tracks where data comes from, how it was transformed, and where it goes. Three approaches for Spark:

  • OpenLineage Spark Plugin: Open standard. Hooks into Spark's event bus, emits lineage events (start/complete/fail) to Marquez, DataHub, or Atlan. Zero code changes — add as a Spark listener JAR.
  • QueryExecutionListener: Native Spark API. Captures DataFrame transformations at plan level. Can extract column-level lineage from the analyzed plan tree.
  • Delta Lake / Iceberg History: Built-in audit log. Every commit records operation type, user, timestamp, files added/removed, and operation parameters.
OpenLineage integration — zero code changeConfig
# Add to spark-submit — no code changes to your job
--conf spark.extraListeners=io.openlineage.spark.agent.OpenLineageSparkListener
--conf spark.openlineage.transport.type=http
--conf spark.openlineage.transport.url=http://marquez:5000
--conf spark.openlineage.namespace=production

# OpenLineage emits events for every Spark action:
# START  → job began, input datasets identified
# COMPLETE → job succeeded, output datasets + schema captured
# FAIL → job failed, error captured
# → Feeds into DataHub / Marquez / Atlan for lineage graph visualization
Governance ToolTypeSpark Integration
Unity CatalogDatabricks-native catalog + governanceBuilt-in on Databricks; column-level lineage, access control, audit
DataHubOpen-source metadata platformOpenLineage plugin; Spark connector; REST ingestion
Apache AtlasHadoop-native governanceAtlas Spark hook; Hive metastore integration
AtlanCommercial active metadata platformOpenLineage; Spark connector; auto-classification
OpenLineageOpen standard for lineageSpark listener plugin; emits to any compatible backend
Part 20 — Section 44

Cost-Based Optimizer — Advanced

Column statistics, histograms, selectivity estimation, multi-way join reordering via dynamic programming, and CBO configuration for maximum plan quality.

44 — CBO Advanced Internals

44.1 CBO Join Reordering

For multi-way joins (3+ tables), CBO uses a dynamic programming algorithm to find the join order that minimizes total intermediate result size. Without CBO, Spark joins tables in the order they appear in SQL — often catastrophically suboptimal. With accurate statistics, CBO can find a plan that's 100× faster simply by reordering joins.

Example: A 4-table join: A (10M rows) ⋈ B (2M) ⋈ C (500K) ⋈ D (1K). Naive left-deep order: A⋈B = 10M intermediate → ⋈C = 8M → ⋈D = 500K final. CBO optimal: D⋈C = 50K → ⋈B = 100K → ⋈A = 500K. Same result, 16× less intermediate data processed.

44.2 Column Statistics and Histograms

CBO needs column-level statistics to estimate filter selectivity and join output sizes. Statistics collected by ANALYZE TABLE: row count, size in bytes, per-column NDV (number of distinct values), min/max, null count, and optionally equi-height histograms for accurate selectivity on skewed distributions.

CBO statistics and configurationSQL
-- Collect full column statistics
ANALYZE TABLE orders COMPUTE STATISTICS FOR ALL COLUMNS;

-- Enable CBO features
SET spark.sql.cbo.enabled = true;
SET spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled = true;
SET spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.dp.threshold = 12;  -- DP for up to 12 tables
SET spark.sql.statistics.histogram.enabled = true;
SET spark.sql.statistics.histogram.numBins = 254;

-- Verify CBO is using statistics
EXPLAIN COST
SELECT o.order_id, c.name, p.category
FROM orders o
JOIN customers c ON o.customer_id = c.id
JOIN products p ON o.product_id = p.id
WHERE c.country = 'US' AND p.category = 'Electronics';
-- Look for: Statistics(sizeInBytes=..., rowCount=...)
-- CBO should reorder joins based on estimated sizes
🎯 Interview Tip "How does CBO work in Spark?" — "CBO uses column-level statistics (NDV, min/max, histograms) collected via ANALYZE TABLE to estimate the output size of each operator in the plan. For multi-way joins, it uses a dynamic programming algorithm to find the optimal join order that minimizes intermediate result sizes. Without stats, Catalyst uses heuristic estimates (often wrong). With stats, CBO can reorder a 5-table join from 2 hours to 5 minutes. Key configs: spark.sql.cbo.enabled=true, spark.sql.cbo.joinReorder.enabled=true. Always run ANALYZE TABLE on frequently-joined tables."
Part 20 — Section 45

Memory Tuning & GC Optimization

G1GC tuning for large heaps, ZGC for ultra-low pause, off-heap memory strategy, and production JVM configurations for Spark executors.

45 — Advanced Memory and GC

45.1 G1GC Tuning for Spark

G1GC (Garbage-First Garbage Collector) is the recommended GC for Spark executors with heaps over 4 GB. It divides the heap into equal-sized regions and collects the "garbage-first" regions with the most reclaimable space, targeting a configurable max pause time.

Production GC configurationConfig
# G1GC for executors (32 GB heap) — production recommended
spark.executor.extraJavaOptions: \
  -XX:+UseG1GC \
  -XX:MaxGCPauseMillis=200 \
  -XX:InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35 \
  -XX:G1HeapRegionSize=32m \
  -XX:G1ReservePercent=10 \
  -XX:+ParallelRefProcEnabled \
  -XX:+PrintGCDetails -XX:+PrintGCDateStamps

# ZGC for ultra-low pause (JDK 15+, Spark 3.3+)
# spark.executor.extraJavaOptions: -XX:+UseZGC -XX:SoftMaxHeapSize=28g
# ZGC: sub-millisecond pauses regardless of heap size
# Trade-off: ~5-10% higher CPU overhead

# Off-heap memory — bypass GC entirely for execution data
spark.memory.offHeap.enabled=true
spark.memory.offHeap.size=4g
# Tungsten stores shuffle/sort/join buffers in off-heap binary format
# → GC only manages User Memory + Storage, not Execution data
⚙️ Engineering Insight — GC Tuning Rules (1) Heap < 32 GB: G1GC with defaults is usually fine. (2) Heap 32-64 GB: G1GC with InitiatingHeapOccupancyPercent=35 and G1HeapRegionSize=32m. (3) Heap > 64 GB: Avoid. Prefer more executors with smaller heaps. (4) GC time > 10% of task time? Enable off-heap (spark.memory.offHeap.enabled=true). (5) Need zero-pause? ZGC (JDK 15+) — sub-millisecond pauses at any heap size, at 5-10% CPU cost.
Part 21 — Section 46

Spark Security Architecture

Authentication (Kerberos, K8s ServiceAccount), encryption (TLS, shuffle encryption), authorization (ACLs, RBAC), and data encryption at rest.

46 — Spark Security

46.1 Five-Layer Security Model

Production Spark security consists of five concentric layers: Network Encryption (TLS for all RPC), Authentication (shared secret, Kerberos, K8s tokens), Authorization (ACLs, RBAC), Data Encryption (Parquet column encryption, S3 SSE-KMS, shuffle encryption), and Audit (event logs, Delta history, CloudTrail).

Production security configurationConfig
# Authentication between Spark components
spark.authenticate=true
spark.authenticate.secret=${SPARK_AUTH_SECRET}

# Network encryption (TLS for RPC + shuffle)
spark.ssl.enabled=true
spark.ssl.protocol=TLSv1.3
spark.network.crypto.enabled=true    # AES for shuffle data
spark.io.encryption.enabled=true     # Encrypt shuffle files on disk

# Kerberos (HDFS/Hive)
spark.kerberos.principal=spark-user@REALM.COM
spark.kerberos.keytab=/path/to/spark.keytab

# Spark UI authentication
spark.ui.acls.enable=true
spark.admin.acls=admin-user,ops-team

# K8s RBAC: ServiceAccount with minimal permissions
# spark-driver SA: create/delete pods, create services
# No cluster-admin. Namespace-scoped only.
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Shuffle Data Exposure By default, Spark shuffle files are unencrypted on executor local disk and unencrypted over the network. Any process on the worker node can read shuffle files. In regulated environments (HIPAA, PCI, GDPR), enable spark.io.encryption.enabled=true (disk) and spark.network.crypto.enabled=true (network) to encrypt all shuffle data end-to-end. Performance impact: ~5-10% overhead.
Part 24 — Section 51

How to Setup a Spark Cluster

Complete step-by-step guides for every deployment mode — Local development, Standalone cluster, YARN on Hadoop, Kubernetes, AWS EMR, GCP Dataproc, Azure HDInsight, Databricks, and Docker Compose for local multi-node testing.

51 — Spark Cluster Setup Guide

51.1 Setup Decision Tree — Which Mode Do You Need?

51.2 Local Mode — Single Machine Setup

The simplest way to start with Spark. Everything runs in a single JVM on your laptop. No cluster needed — ideal for learning, unit testing, and debugging pipelines.

Local mode setup — from zero to running in 5 minutesBash
# ============================================
# PREREQUISITES
# ============================================
# Java 8/11/17 (OpenJDK recommended)
java -version   # Must show 1.8+ / 11+ / 17+

# Python 3.8+ (for PySpark)
python3 --version

# ============================================
# OPTION A: Install via pip (simplest for PySpark)
# ============================================
pip install pyspark==3.5.3

# Verify
pyspark --version
# Launch interactive shell
pyspark --master local[*]   # Uses all CPU cores

# ============================================
# OPTION B: Download Spark binary distribution
# ============================================
# Download from https://spark.apache.org/downloads.html
wget https://downloads.apache.org/spark/spark-3.5.3/spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3.tgz
tar -xzf spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3.tgz
export SPARK_HOME=$PWD/spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3
export PATH=$SPARK_HOME/bin:$PATH

# Launch PySpark shell
pyspark --master local[*]

# Launch Scala shell
spark-shell --master local[*]

# Submit a job
spark-submit --master local[*] my_job.py

# Spark UI available at http://localhost:4040

# ============================================
# OPTION C: Conda environment (isolated)
# ============================================
conda create -n spark python=3.11 openjdk=17 pyspark=3.5.3 -y
conda activate spark
pyspark
Local mode — Python script examplePySpark
from pyspark.sql import SparkSession

# Create local SparkSession
spark = SparkSession.builder \
    .appName("LocalTest") \
    .master("local[*]") \          # local = 1 thread, local[4] = 4 threads, local[*] = all cores
    .config("spark.driver.memory", "4g") \
    .config("spark.sql.adaptive.enabled", "true") \
    .getOrCreate()

# Test with sample data
df = spark.createDataFrame([
    (1, "Alice", 90000),
    (2, "Bob", 75000),
    (3, "Carol", 85000)
], ["id", "name", "salary"])

df.show()
df.groupBy().avg("salary").show()
spark.stop()

51.3 Docker Compose — Multi-Node Local Cluster

A Docker Compose setup gives you a realistic multi-node Spark cluster on your laptop — one master container, multiple worker containers, and optional Jupyter notebook. Perfect for testing distributed behavior before deploying to production.

docker-compose.yml — Spark standalone clusterYAML
version: "3.8"

services:
  spark-master:
    image: bitnami/spark:3.5
    container_name: spark-master
    environment:
      - SPARK_MODE=master
      - SPARK_MASTER_HOST=spark-master
      - SPARK_MASTER_PORT=7077
      - SPARK_MASTER_WEBUI_PORT=8080
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"    # Master Web UI
      - "7077:7077"    # Master RPC
      - "4040:4040"    # Spark UI (when running jobs)
    volumes:
      - ./data:/opt/spark-data
      - ./jobs:/opt/spark-jobs

  spark-worker-1:
    image: bitnami/spark:3.5
    environment:
      - SPARK_MODE=worker
      - SPARK_MASTER_URL=spark://spark-master:7077
      - SPARK_WORKER_CORES=4
      - SPARK_WORKER_MEMORY=4g
    depends_on:
      - spark-master
    volumes:
      - ./data:/opt/spark-data

  spark-worker-2:
    image: bitnami/spark:3.5
    environment:
      - SPARK_MODE=worker
      - SPARK_MASTER_URL=spark://spark-master:7077
      - SPARK_WORKER_CORES=4
      - SPARK_WORKER_MEMORY=4g
    depends_on:
      - spark-master
    volumes:
      - ./data:/opt/spark-data

  # Optional: Jupyter with PySpark
  jupyter:
    image: jupyter/pyspark-notebook:spark-3.5.0
    container_name: jupyter-spark
    environment:
      - SPARK_MASTER=spark://spark-master:7077
    ports:
      - "8888:8888"    # Jupyter UI
    depends_on:
      - spark-master
    volumes:
      - ./notebooks:/home/jovyan/work
      - ./data:/opt/spark-data
Running the Docker clusterBash
# Start the cluster
docker-compose up -d

# Verify
docker-compose ps
# Master UI: http://localhost:8080
# Jupyter:   http://localhost:8888

# Submit a job to the Docker cluster
docker exec spark-master spark-submit \
  --master spark://spark-master:7077 \
  --deploy-mode client \
  /opt/spark-jobs/my_etl.py

# Scale workers dynamically
docker-compose up --scale spark-worker-1=4 -d   # Scale to 4 workers

# Stop cluster
docker-compose down

51.4 Spark Standalone Cluster — On-Premise

Spark's built-in cluster manager. No external dependencies (no Hadoop, no YARN, no K8s). Simplest multi-node production setup for dedicated Spark-only clusters.

Standalone cluster setup — step by stepBash
# ============================================
# ON MASTER NODE
# ============================================

# 1. Install Java + download Spark on ALL nodes (master + workers)
sudo apt install openjdk-17-jdk -y
wget https://downloads.apache.org/spark/spark-3.5.3/spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3.tgz
tar -xzf spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3.tgz
export SPARK_HOME=/opt/spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3

# 2. Configure workers list
cat > $SPARK_HOME/conf/workers << EOF
worker-node-1
worker-node-2
worker-node-3
EOF

# 3. Configure environment
cat > $SPARK_HOME/conf/spark-env.sh << EOF
export SPARK_MASTER_HOST=master-node
export SPARK_MASTER_PORT=7077
export SPARK_MASTER_WEBUI_PORT=8080
export SPARK_WORKER_CORES=8
export SPARK_WORKER_MEMORY=32g
export SPARK_WORKER_DIR=/data/spark/work
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk-amd64
EOF

# 4. Configure spark-defaults.conf
cat > $SPARK_HOME/conf/spark-defaults.conf << EOF
spark.master                     spark://master-node:7077
spark.serializer                 org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer
spark.sql.adaptive.enabled       true
spark.sql.shuffle.partitions     200
spark.eventLog.enabled           true
spark.eventLog.dir               /data/spark/events
spark.history.fs.logDirectory    /data/spark/events
EOF

# 5. Copy Spark installation to all worker nodes
for node in worker-node-1 worker-node-2 worker-node-3; do
  rsync -avz $SPARK_HOME/ $node:$SPARK_HOME/
done

# 6. Start the cluster
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-master.sh          # Start master process
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-workers.sh         # SSH to each worker and start worker process

# 7. Verify
# Master Web UI: http://master-node:8080
# Should show all 3 workers with 8 cores, 32 GB each

# 8. Start History Server (optional but recommended)
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-history-server.sh  # http://master-node:18080

# 9. Submit a job
spark-submit \
  --master spark://master-node:7077 \
  --deploy-mode client \
  --executor-cores 4 \
  --executor-memory 16g \
  --num-executors 6 \
  my_etl.py

# 10. Stop cluster
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-workers.sh
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-master.sh

51.5 YARN Cluster — Hadoop Integration

If you have an existing Hadoop cluster, Spark runs as a YARN application. No separate Spark master needed — YARN's ResourceManager handles resource allocation.

Spark on YARN setupBash
# Prerequisites: Hadoop cluster running with YARN
# HADOOP_HOME, HADOOP_CONF_DIR must be set

export HADOOP_CONF_DIR=/etc/hadoop/conf    # Contains yarn-site.xml, core-site.xml, hdfs-site.xml

# Submit Spark job to YARN
spark-submit \
  --master yarn \
  --deploy-mode cluster \              # cluster = Driver inside YARN; client = Driver on submit machine
  --queue production \                 # YARN queue for resource limits
  --num-executors 20 \
  --executor-cores 4 \
  --executor-memory 16g \
  --driver-memory 8g \
  --conf spark.yarn.maxAppAttempts=2 \
  --conf spark.yarn.am.memory=2g \
  --conf spark.yarn.submit.waitAppCompletion=true \
  s3://bucket/jobs/my_etl.py

# Monitor: YARN ResourceManager UI → http://rm-host:8088
# View logs: yarn logs -applicationId application_1234567890_0001

51.6 Spark on Kubernetes

Cloud-native deployment using K8s Pods for Driver and Executors. Two approaches: (1) spark-submit with K8s master (built-in), (2) Spark Operator (recommended for production — CRD-based declarative management).

Spark on Kubernetes — both approachesBash + YAML
# ============================================
# APPROACH 1: spark-submit with K8s master
# ============================================

# Build Docker image with your app
$SPARK_HOME/bin/docker-image-tool.sh \
  -r my-registry -t 3.5.3 -p ./Dockerfile build
docker push my-registry/spark:3.5.3

# Create K8s ServiceAccount + RBAC
kubectl create serviceaccount spark-driver -n spark-prod
kubectl create clusterrolebinding spark-role \
  --clusterrole=edit \
  --serviceaccount=spark-prod:spark-driver

# Submit
spark-submit \
  --master k8s://https://k8s-api-server:443 \
  --deploy-mode cluster \
  --name my-etl \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.container.image=my-registry/spark:3.5.3 \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.namespace=spark-prod \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.authenticate.driver.serviceAccountName=spark-driver \
  --conf spark.executor.instances=10 \
  --conf spark.kubernetes.executor.request.cores=4 \
  --conf spark.executor.memory=16g \
  local:///opt/spark/jobs/my_etl.py

# ============================================
# APPROACH 2: Spark Operator (recommended for production)
# ============================================

# Install Spark Operator via Helm
helm repo add spark-operator https://kubeflow.github.io/spark-operator
helm install spark-operator spark-operator/spark-operator \
  --namespace spark-operator \
  --set webhook.enable=true \
  --set sparkJobNamespace=spark-prod
SparkApplication CRD — declarative job definitionYAML
# spark-job.yaml — apply with: kubectl apply -f spark-job.yaml
apiVersion: sparkoperator.k8s.io/v1beta2
kind: SparkApplication
metadata:
  name: revenue-etl
  namespace: spark-prod
spec:
  type: Python
  mode: cluster
  image: my-registry/spark:3.5.3
  mainApplicationFile: local:///opt/spark/jobs/revenue_etl.py
  sparkVersion: "3.5.3"
  sparkConf:
    "spark.sql.adaptive.enabled": "true"
    "spark.sql.shuffle.partitions": "2000"
    "spark.serializer": "org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer"
  driver:
    cores: 2
    memory: "8g"
    serviceAccount: spark-driver
    nodeSelector:
      node-type: on-demand        # Driver on stable nodes
  executor:
    cores: 4
    instances: 20
    memory: "16g"
    memoryOverhead: "4g"
    nodeSelector:
      node-type: spot             # Executors on spot nodes (cheap)
  restartPolicy:
    type: OnFailure
    onFailureRetries: 3

51.7 AWS EMR — Managed Spark on AWS

Amazon EMR (Elastic MapReduce) is AWS's managed Spark service. It provisions EC2 instances, installs Spark, manages YARN/HDFS, and integrates natively with S3, Glue Catalog, and IAM.

AWS EMR cluster creationAWS CLI
# Create EMR cluster with Spark
aws emr create-cluster \
  --name "Production-Spark-Cluster" \
  --release-label emr-7.0.0 \
  --applications Name=Spark Name=Hive Name=JupyterEnterpriseGateway \
  --instance-groups \
    InstanceGroupType=MASTER,InstanceCount=1,InstanceType=m5.2xlarge \
    InstanceGroupType=CORE,InstanceCount=5,InstanceType=r5.4xlarge \
    InstanceGroupType=TASK,InstanceCount=20,InstanceType=r5.4xlarge,BidPrice="0.40" \
  --ec2-attributes \
    KeyName=my-key,SubnetId=subnet-abc123,\
    InstanceProfile=EMR_EC2_DefaultRole \
  --service-role EMR_DefaultRole \
  --configurations file://spark-config.json \
  --auto-scaling-role EMR_AutoScaling_DefaultRole \
  --region us-east-1 \
  --log-uri s3://my-bucket/emr-logs/

# spark-config.json
# [
#   {
#     "Classification": "spark-defaults",
#     "Properties": {
#       "spark.sql.adaptive.enabled": "true",
#       "spark.serializer": "org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer",
#       "spark.sql.shuffle.partitions": "2000"
#     }
#   }
# ]

# Submit job to EMR
aws emr add-steps --cluster-id j-XXXXXXXXXXXXX \
  --steps Type=Spark,Name="Revenue ETL",\
ActionOnFailure=CONTINUE,\
Args=[--deploy-mode,cluster,--executor-memory,16g,s3://bucket/jobs/etl.py]

51.8 GCP Dataproc — Managed Spark on Google Cloud

GCP Dataproc clustergcloud CLI
# Create Dataproc cluster
gcloud dataproc clusters create production-spark \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --zone=us-central1-a \
  --master-machine-type=n2-standard-8 \
  --worker-machine-type=n2-highmem-16 \
  --num-workers=10 \
  --num-secondary-workers=20 \       # Preemptible workers (cheap)
  --image-version=2.1-debian11 \       # Includes Spark 3.5
  --optional-components=JUPYTER \
  --properties="spark:spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true" \
  --initialization-actions=gs://bucket/init-scripts/install-deps.sh

# Submit job
gcloud dataproc jobs submit pyspark \
  gs://bucket/jobs/revenue_etl.py \
  --cluster=production-spark \
  --region=us-central1 \
  --properties=spark.executor.memory=16g,spark.executor.cores=4

51.9 Databricks — Fully Managed Platform

Databricks provides the highest-level managed Spark experience — no cluster provisioning, no JVM config, no YARN management. Create a cluster in the UI or via API, and start writing code in notebooks. Available on AWS, Azure, and GCP.

Databricks cluster creation via APIBash + JSON
# Create cluster via Databricks REST API
curl -X POST https://<workspace>.cloud.databricks.com/api/2.0/clusters/create \
  -H "Authorization: Bearer ${DATABRICKS_TOKEN}" \
  -d '{
    "cluster_name": "production-etl",
    "spark_version": "14.3.x-scala2.12",
    "node_type_id": "i3.xlarge",
    "num_workers": 10,
    "autoscale": {
      "min_workers": 2,
      "max_workers": 50
    },
    "spark_conf": {
      "spark.sql.adaptive.enabled": "true",
      "spark.sql.shuffle.partitions": "2000",
      "spark.databricks.delta.optimizeWrite.enabled": "true"
    },
    "aws_attributes": {
      "first_on_demand": 1,
      "availability": "SPOT_WITH_FALLBACK",
      "spot_bid_price_percent": 100
    }
  }'

# Or simply: Databricks UI → Compute → Create Cluster
# → Select runtime version, instance type, worker count → Start
# → Open notebook → Write PySpark code → Run
# Zero infrastructure setup. Cluster auto-terminates on idle.

51.10 Post-Setup Checklist — First Things After Cluster Is Running

#Verification StepCommand / URL
1Spark Master UI accessible?http://master:8080 (Standalone) or http://rm:8088 (YARN)
2All workers registered?Master UI shows expected worker count and resources
3Submit a test jobspark-submit --master ... $SPARK_HOME/examples/src/main/python/pi.py
4Spark UI for job visible?http://driver:4040 shows job stages and tasks
5Storage connectivityspark.read.parquet("s3://...") or hdfs://... works
6Event logging enabled?spark.eventLog.enabled=true + History Server running
7AQE enabled?spark.sql.adaptive.enabled=true in spark-defaults
8Kryo serializer set?spark.serializer=org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer
9Monitoring connected?Prometheus scraping /metrics/prometheus endpoint
10Security configured?TLS enabled, authentication enabled, ACLs set
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Production Setup Recommendation For new projects in 2024-2025: use Databricks if budget allows (fastest time-to-value, near-zero ops). Use AWS EMR / GCP Dataproc for cost-effective cloud Spark without Databricks premium. Use Spark on Kubernetes if you already have a K8s platform and want infrastructure standardization. Use YARN only if you have an existing Hadoop cluster. Use Standalone only for dedicated Spark-only bare-metal clusters. For local development: pip install pyspark + local[*] mode is all you need.
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Environment Mismatches The #1 cluster setup issue: Java version mismatch between nodes. Spark 3.5 supports Java 8, 11, and 17 — but ALL nodes (master + workers) must use the same Java version. Python version mismatches between driver and executors also cause silent failures. Use Docker images or cluster bootstrap scripts to guarantee identical environments everywhere.
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you set up a Spark cluster for a new team?" — "It depends on the environment. For a cloud-native team on AWS: EMR with spot instances for executors — 10-minute setup, auto-scaling, native S3 integration. For a team already on Kubernetes: Spark Operator with SparkApplication CRDs — declarative, GitOps-friendly, namespace isolation. For maximum productivity: Databricks — zero infra management, notebooks, auto-clustering, Delta Lake built-in. For local dev: pip install pyspark with local[*] mode. In all cases, I'd configure AQE, Kryo serializer, event logging, and Prometheus metrics from day one — these are the baseline production configs that every Spark cluster should have."
Part 24B — Section 51 (Extended)

Multi-Machine Spark Cluster Setup

Complete bare-metal / VM guide — network planning, /etc/hosts, passwordless SSH, Java + Python installation, Spark binary distribution, shared config, HDFS setup, Hive Metastore, History Server, and automated provisioning with Ansible.

51B — Multi-Machine Cluster (Step-by-Step)

B.1 Cluster Planning — Machines, Roles, and Network

Before touching a terminal, plan the cluster topology. Define which machines serve which roles, how much RAM/CPU each has, and what network layout to use.

B.2 Step 1 — OS Preparation (All Nodes)

Run these commands on every machine in the cluster (master + all workers + edge node). Use the same OS version everywhere — Ubuntu 22.04 LTS or RHEL 8/9 recommended.

OS preparation — run on ALL 5 nodesBash
#!/bin/bash
# ============================================
# RUN ON ALL NODES (master + workers + edge)
# ============================================

# 1. Update OS
sudo apt update && sudo apt upgrade -y

# 2. Install Java (OpenJDK 17 — same version on ALL nodes!)
sudo apt install openjdk-17-jdk -y
java -version  # Verify: openjdk version "17.x.x"

# Set JAVA_HOME
echo 'export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk-amd64' >> ~/.bashrc
echo 'export PATH=$JAVA_HOME/bin:$PATH' >> ~/.bashrc
source ~/.bashrc

# 3. Install Python 3.11 (same version on ALL nodes!)
sudo apt install python3.11 python3.11-venv python3-pip -y
sudo update-alternatives --install /usr/bin/python3 python3 /usr/bin/python3.11 1
python3 --version  # Verify: Python 3.11.x

# 4. Install PySpark dependencies
pip3 install numpy pandas pyarrow

# 5. Configure /etc/hosts on ALL nodes (consistent name resolution)
sudo tee -a /etc/hosts << EOF
192.168.1.10  spark-master
192.168.1.11  spark-worker-1
192.168.1.12  spark-worker-2
192.168.1.13  spark-worker-3
192.168.1.20  spark-edge
EOF

# 6. Verify connectivity from each node
ping -c 2 spark-master
ping -c 2 spark-worker-1
ping -c 2 spark-worker-2
ping -c 2 spark-worker-3

# 7. Create spark user (consistent across all nodes)
sudo useradd -m -s /bin/bash spark
sudo passwd spark       # Set same password everywhere (or use key-only auth)
sudo usermod -aG sudo spark

# 8. Create standard directories
sudo mkdir -p /opt/spark /data/spark/work /data/spark/events /data/spark/logs
sudo chown -R spark:spark /opt/spark /data/spark

# 9. Disable swap (important for stable memory behavior)
sudo swapoff -a
sudo sed -i '/ swap / s/^/#/' /etc/fstab

# 10. Set open file limits (Spark opens many files during shuffle)
sudo tee -a /etc/security/limits.conf << EOF
spark  soft  nofile  65536
spark  hard  nofile  131072
spark  soft  nproc   32768
spark  hard  nproc   65536
EOF

B.3 Step 2 — Passwordless SSH (Master → All Workers)

Spark's start-workers.sh script uses SSH to remotely start worker processes. The master must be able to SSH to every worker without a password prompt.

SSH key setup — run on master node as spark userBash
# ============================================
# RUN ON MASTER NODE ONLY (as spark user)
# ============================================
su - spark

# 1. Generate SSH key pair (no passphrase)
ssh-keygen -t ed25519 -f ~/.ssh/id_ed25519 -N ""

# 2. Copy public key to ALL nodes (including master itself)
ssh-copy-id spark@spark-master      # Master needs to SSH to itself too
ssh-copy-id spark@spark-worker-1
ssh-copy-id spark@spark-worker-2
ssh-copy-id spark@spark-worker-3
ssh-copy-id spark@spark-edge

# 3. Verify passwordless SSH to each node
ssh spark@spark-worker-1 "hostname && java -version"
ssh spark@spark-worker-2 "hostname && java -version"
ssh spark@spark-worker-3 "hostname && java -version"
# Should print hostname + Java version WITHOUT password prompt

# 4. Fix SSH config for batch connections (optional, speeds up startup)
cat >> ~/.ssh/config << EOF
Host spark-*
    StrictHostKeyChecking no
    UserKnownHostsFile /dev/null
    ConnectTimeout 5
EOF
chmod 600 ~/.ssh/config
⚠️ Common Pitfall — SSH Failures If start-workers.sh fails with "Permission denied" or "Host key verification failed": (1) Verify ssh-copy-id was run for EACH worker. (2) Check ~/.ssh/authorized_keys permissions on workers: must be chmod 600. (3) Check ~/.ssh directory: must be chmod 700. (4) Check /etc/ssh/sshd_config: PubkeyAuthentication yes must be set. (5) The spark user must exist on ALL nodes with the SAME username.

B.4 Step 3 — Install and Distribute Spark

Download, configure, and distribute Spark to all nodesBash
# ============================================
# RUN ON MASTER NODE (as spark user)
# ============================================
su - spark
cd /opt

# 1. Download Spark
wget https://downloads.apache.org/spark/spark-3.5.3/spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3.tgz
tar -xzf spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3.tgz
mv spark-3.5.3-bin-hadoop3 spark
export SPARK_HOME=/opt/spark

# 2. Set environment variables (add to ~/.bashrc)
cat >> ~/.bashrc << 'EOF'
export SPARK_HOME=/opt/spark
export PATH=$SPARK_HOME/bin:$SPARK_HOME/sbin:$PATH
export PYSPARK_PYTHON=python3
export PYSPARK_DRIVER_PYTHON=python3
EOF
source ~/.bashrc

# 3. Configure workers list
cat > $SPARK_HOME/conf/workers << EOF
spark-worker-1
spark-worker-2
spark-worker-3
EOF

# 4. Configure spark-env.sh
cat > $SPARK_HOME/conf/spark-env.sh << 'EOF'
#!/usr/bin/env bash
export JAVA_HOME=/usr/lib/jvm/java-17-openjdk-amd64
export SPARK_MASTER_HOST=spark-master
export SPARK_MASTER_PORT=7077
export SPARK_MASTER_WEBUI_PORT=8080
export SPARK_WORKER_CORES=12          # Leave 4 cores for OS + DataNode
export SPARK_WORKER_MEMORY=48g        # Leave 16 GB for OS + DataNode + overhead
export SPARK_WORKER_DIR=/data/spark/work
export SPARK_LOG_DIR=/data/spark/logs
export SPARK_LOCAL_DIRS=/data/spark/local   # Shuffle spill directory (use fast SSD)
export SPARK_DAEMON_MEMORY=2g         # Memory for master/worker daemons
export PYSPARK_PYTHON=python3
EOF
chmod +x $SPARK_HOME/conf/spark-env.sh

# 5. Configure spark-defaults.conf
cat > $SPARK_HOME/conf/spark-defaults.conf << EOF
spark.master                        spark://spark-master:7077
spark.eventLog.enabled              true
spark.eventLog.dir                  /data/spark/events
spark.history.fs.logDirectory       /data/spark/events
spark.serializer                    org.apache.spark.serializer.KryoSerializer
spark.sql.adaptive.enabled          true
spark.sql.adaptive.coalescePartitions.enabled  true
spark.sql.adaptive.skewJoin.enabled true
spark.sql.shuffle.partitions        2000
spark.sql.autoBroadcastJoinThreshold 268435456
spark.executor.memory               16g
spark.executor.cores                4
spark.executor.memoryOverhead       4g
spark.driver.memory                 8g
spark.driver.maxResultSize          4g
spark.network.timeout               600s
spark.rpc.askTimeout                600s
EOF

# 6. Configure log4j2 (reduce verbose logging)
cp $SPARK_HOME/conf/log4j2.properties.template $SPARK_HOME/conf/log4j2.properties
sed -i 's/rootLogger.level = info/rootLogger.level = warn/' $SPARK_HOME/conf/log4j2.properties

# 7. Distribute Spark to ALL worker nodes (identical installation)
for node in spark-worker-1 spark-worker-2 spark-worker-3 spark-edge; do
  echo "Syncing Spark to $node ..."
  rsync -avz --delete /opt/spark/ spark@$node:/opt/spark/
  # Also sync bashrc for environment variables
  scp ~/.bashrc spark@$node:~/.bashrc
  ssh spark@$node "source ~/.bashrc && echo 'Spark installed on $(hostname)'"
done

# 8. Create spark-local directories on workers (fast SSD path for shuffle)
for node in spark-worker-1 spark-worker-2 spark-worker-3; do
  ssh spark@$node "sudo mkdir -p /data/spark/local /data/spark/work /data/spark/events /data/spark/logs && sudo chown -R spark:spark /data/spark"
done

B.5 Step 4 — Start the Cluster

Starting and verifying the Spark clusterBash
# ============================================
# RUN ON MASTER NODE (as spark user)
# ============================================

# 1. Start Spark Master
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-master.sh
# Verify: http://spark-master:8080 shows Master UI
# Should display: "Workers: 0, Cores: 0, Memory: 0"

# 2. Start all Workers (SSH to each node in conf/workers)
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-workers.sh
# This SSHs to spark-worker-1, spark-worker-2, spark-worker-3
# and starts a Worker process on each

# Verify: http://spark-master:8080 now shows:
# Workers: 3
# Cores in use: 0 / 36 (12 per worker × 3)
# Memory in use: 0 / 144 GB (48 GB per worker × 3)

# 3. Start History Server (for viewing completed job logs)
$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-history-server.sh
# Verify: http://spark-master:18080 shows History Server UI

# 4. Quick verification — run Pi calculation
spark-submit \
  --master spark://spark-master:7077 \
  --deploy-mode client \
  --executor-cores 4 \
  --executor-memory 8g \
  --num-executors 3 \
  $SPARK_HOME/examples/src/main/python/pi.py 1000
# Should complete in ~30 seconds and print Pi ≈ 3.14159...

# 5. Test from EDGE NODE (client mode)
ssh spark@spark-edge
spark-submit \
  --master spark://spark-master:7077 \
  --deploy-mode client \
  /opt/spark/examples/src/main/python/pi.py 1000
# Driver runs on edge node, executors on workers

B.6 Step 5 — Add HDFS (Optional — For Data Locality)

If your data lives on the cluster (not S3/GCS), install HDFS on the same nodes for data locality — tasks scheduled where data blocks reside.

HDFS setup on the same 5-node clusterBash
# ============================================
# INSTALL HADOOP ON ALL NODES
# ============================================
# Download Hadoop (on master, then rsync to all)
wget https://downloads.apache.org/hadoop/common/hadoop-3.3.6/hadoop-3.3.6.tar.gz
tar -xzf hadoop-3.3.6.tar.gz -C /opt/
export HADOOP_HOME=/opt/hadoop-3.3.6

# core-site.xml (ALL nodes)
cat > $HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/core-site.xml << 'EOF'

  
    fs.defaultFS
    hdfs://spark-master:9000
  

EOF

# hdfs-site.xml (ALL nodes)
cat > $HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/hdfs-site.xml << 'EOF'

  
    dfs.replication
    2          
  
  
    dfs.namenode.name.dir
    /data/hdfs/namenode
  
  
    dfs.datanode.data.dir
    /data/hdfs/datanode
  
  
    dfs.blocksize
    134217728   
  

EOF

# workers file (DataNodes)
cat > $HADOOP_HOME/etc/hadoop/workers << EOF
spark-worker-1
spark-worker-2
spark-worker-3
EOF

# Distribute Hadoop to all nodes
for node in spark-worker-1 spark-worker-2 spark-worker-3; do
  rsync -avz /opt/hadoop-3.3.6/ spark@$node:/opt/hadoop-3.3.6/
  ssh spark@$node "sudo mkdir -p /data/hdfs/datanode && sudo chown -R spark:spark /data/hdfs"
done

# Format NameNode (ONCE on master — only on first setup!)
sudo mkdir -p /data/hdfs/namenode && sudo chown spark:spark /data/hdfs/namenode
$HADOOP_HOME/bin/hdfs namenode -format

# Start HDFS
$HADOOP_HOME/sbin/start-dfs.sh
# NameNode UI: http://spark-master:9870
# Should show 3 Live DataNodes

# Test HDFS
$HADOOP_HOME/bin/hdfs dfs -mkdir -p /data/warehouse
$HADOOP_HOME/bin/hdfs dfs -put my_data.csv /data/warehouse/
$HADOOP_HOME/bin/hdfs dfs -ls /data/warehouse/

# Now Spark can read HDFS data with locality
# spark.read.parquet("hdfs://spark-master:9000/data/warehouse/orders/")

B.7 Step 6 — Hive Metastore Setup (For Table Catalog)

The Hive Metastore provides a SQL table catalog for Spark SQL. Without it, you can only read files by path. With it, you get CREATE TABLE, SHOW TABLES, spark.table("orders"), and bucketing metadata support.

Hive Metastore with PostgreSQL backendBash + XML
# ============================================
# ON MASTER NODE — Install Hive Metastore
# ============================================

# 1. Install PostgreSQL (Metastore backend DB)
sudo apt install postgresql -y
sudo -u postgres createuser hive
sudo -u postgres createdb metastore -O hive
sudo -u postgres psql -c "ALTER USER hive WITH PASSWORD 'hive_password';"

# 2. Download Hive (only need metastore, not full Hive)
wget https://downloads.apache.org/hive/hive-3.1.3/apache-hive-3.1.3-bin.tar.gz
tar -xzf apache-hive-3.1.3-bin.tar.gz -C /opt/
export HIVE_HOME=/opt/apache-hive-3.1.3-bin

# 3. Download PostgreSQL JDBC driver
wget -P $HIVE_HOME/lib/ \
  https://jdbc.postgresql.org/download/postgresql-42.7.1.jar

# 4. Configure hive-site.xml
cat > $HIVE_HOME/conf/hive-site.xml << 'EOF'

  
    javax.jdo.option.ConnectionURL
    jdbc:postgresql://localhost:5432/metastore
  
  
    javax.jdo.option.ConnectionDriverName
    org.postgresql.Driver
  
  
    javax.jdo.option.ConnectionUserName
    hive
  
  
    javax.jdo.option.ConnectionPassword
    hive_password
  
  
    hive.metastore.warehouse.dir
    /data/warehouse
  

EOF

# 5. Initialize Metastore schema
$HIVE_HOME/bin/schematool -dbType postgres -initSchema

# 6. Start Metastore service
nohup $HIVE_HOME/bin/hive --service metastore &
# Listens on port 9083

# 7. Configure Spark to use Hive Metastore
# Copy hive-site.xml to Spark conf
cp $HIVE_HOME/conf/hive-site.xml $SPARK_HOME/conf/
# Add to spark-defaults.conf:
echo "spark.sql.catalogImplementation  hive" >> $SPARK_HOME/conf/spark-defaults.conf

# Now Spark SQL supports CREATE TABLE, SHOW TABLES, etc.
# spark.sql("CREATE TABLE orders (...) USING parquet")
# spark.sql("SELECT * FROM orders")

B.8 Step 7 — Automate with Ansible (Production)

For production clusters, automate everything with Ansible — OS prep, Java/Python install, Spark distribution, configuration templating, and service management. This ensures repeatable, version-controlled cluster provisioning.

Ansible playbook for Spark cluster provisioningYAML (Ansible)
# inventory.ini
[spark_master]
spark-master ansible_host=192.168.1.10

[spark_workers]
spark-worker-1 ansible_host=192.168.1.11
spark-worker-2 ansible_host=192.168.1.12
spark-worker-3 ansible_host=192.168.1.13

[spark_edge]
spark-edge ansible_host=192.168.1.20

[all:vars]
ansible_user=spark
spark_version=3.5.3
java_package=openjdk-17-jdk

# ---
# spark-cluster.yml — main playbook
---
- name: Setup Spark Cluster — All Nodes
  hosts: all
  become: yes
  tasks:
    - name: Install Java
      apt: name={{ java_package }} state=present

    - name: Install Python
      apt: name=python3.11,python3-pip state=present

    - name: Configure /etc/hosts
      template: src=hosts.j2 dest=/etc/hosts

    - name: Create spark user and directories
      user: name=spark shell=/bin/bash groups=sudo

    - name: Download and extract Spark
      unarchive:
        src: https://downloads.apache.org/spark/spark-{{ spark_version }}/spark-{{ spark_version }}-bin-hadoop3.tgz
        dest: /opt/
        remote_src: yes
        creates: /opt/spark-{{ spark_version }}-bin-hadoop3

    - name: Symlink Spark
      file: src=/opt/spark-{{ spark_version }}-bin-hadoop3 dest=/opt/spark state=link

    - name: Deploy spark-env.sh
      template: src=spark-env.sh.j2 dest=/opt/spark/conf/spark-env.sh mode=0755

    - name: Deploy spark-defaults.conf
      template: src=spark-defaults.conf.j2 dest=/opt/spark/conf/spark-defaults.conf

    - name: Deploy workers file
      template: src=workers.j2 dest=/opt/spark/conf/workers
      when: inventory_hostname in groups['spark_master']

    - name: Set file limits
      pam_limits: domain=spark limit_type={{ item.type }} limit_item=nofile value={{ item.value }}
      loop:
        - { type: soft, value: 65536 }
        - { type: hard, value: 131072 }

- name: Setup SSH keys for Master
  hosts: spark_master
  become: yes
  become_user: spark
  tasks:
    - name: Generate SSH key
      openssh_keypair: path=~/.ssh/id_ed25519 type=ed25519

    - name: Distribute SSH key to all nodes
      authorized_key:
        user: spark
        key: "{{ lookup('file', '~spark/.ssh/id_ed25519.pub') }}"
      delegate_to: "{{ item }}"
      loop: "{{ groups['all'] }}"

# Run: ansible-playbook -i inventory.ini spark-cluster.yml

B.9 Step 8 — Cluster Management Commands Reference

OperationCommand (run on master as spark user)
Start master$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-master.sh
Start all workers$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-workers.sh
Start master + workers$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-all.sh
Stop all workers$SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-workers.sh
Stop master$SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-master.sh
Stop everything$SPARK_HOME/sbin/stop-all.sh
Start History Server$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-history-server.sh
Add a single worker$SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-worker.sh spark://spark-master:7077 (run on the new node)
Decommission a workerKill worker process → Spark reassigns tasks. No data loss (executors are stateless).
Check cluster healthcurl http://spark-master:8080/json/ (JSON API for master status)
View worker logstail -f /data/spark/logs/spark-spark-worker-*.out (on worker node)
Master Web UIhttp://spark-master:8080
Job Spark UIhttp://driver-host:4040 (during job execution)
History Server UIhttp://spark-master:18080

B.10 Step 9 — Add New Worker Node to Running Cluster

Add a 4th worker node dynamicallyBash
# ============================================
# ON THE NEW MACHINE (spark-worker-4: 192.168.1.14)
# ============================================

# 1. Run OS preparation (same as Step B.2 — Java, Python, /etc/hosts, spark user)
# ... (all the same commands as B.2) ...

# 2. Add to /etc/hosts on ALL existing nodes
for node in spark-master spark-worker-1 spark-worker-2 spark-worker-3 spark-edge; do
  ssh spark@$node "echo '192.168.1.14  spark-worker-4' | sudo tee -a /etc/hosts"
done

# 3. From master: copy SSH key and sync Spark installation
ssh-copy-id spark@spark-worker-4
rsync -avz /opt/spark/ spark@spark-worker-4:/opt/spark/
ssh spark@spark-worker-4 "sudo mkdir -p /data/spark/{local,work,events,logs} && sudo chown -R spark:spark /data/spark"

# 4. Update workers file on master
echo "spark-worker-4" >> $SPARK_HOME/conf/workers

# 5. Start worker on the new node (from master via SSH)
ssh spark@spark-worker-4 "/opt/spark/sbin/start-worker.sh spark://spark-master:7077"

# OR start from the new node itself:
# ssh spark@spark-worker-4
# $SPARK_HOME/sbin/start-worker.sh spark://spark-master:7077

# 6. Verify: http://spark-master:8080 now shows 4 Workers
# New worker is immediately available for new jobs
# Running jobs keep using existing executors until next stage

B.11 Step 10 — Firewall and Port Reference

Open these ports between cluster nodes. If using a firewall (ufw, iptables, security groups), these must be allowed.

PortServiceDirectionNotes
7077Spark Master RPCWorkers → MasterWorker registration, job submission
8080Spark Master Web UIBrowser → MasterCluster status dashboard
8081Spark Worker Web UIBrowser → WorkerPer-worker status
4040Spark Application UIBrowser → DriverJob stages/tasks (active job)
18080History ServerBrowser → MasterCompleted job logs
7078-7099Executor RPC (dynamic)Executors ↔ DriverTask dispatch and results
33000-34000Block Manager portsExecutors ↔ ExecutorsShuffle data transfer
22SSHMaster → WorkersWorker start/stop scripts
9000HDFS NameNode RPCAll → MasterIf using HDFS
9870HDFS NameNode Web UIBrowser → MasterIf using HDFS
9083Hive MetastoreAll → MasterIf using Hive catalog
Firewall rules (ufw example)Bash
# On ALL nodes — allow cluster traffic from the subnet
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.1.0/24 to any port 7077
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.1.0/24 to any port 7078:7099 proto tcp
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.1.0/24 to any port 33000:34000 proto tcp
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.1.0/24 to any port 22
sudo ufw allow from 192.168.1.0/24 to any port 4040

# On MASTER only — expose Web UIs
sudo ufw allow 8080    # Master UI
sudo ufw allow 18080   # History Server
sudo ufw allow 9870    # HDFS UI (if applicable)

sudo ufw enable
sudo ufw status

B.12 Troubleshooting Multi-Machine Setup

ProblemSymptomFix
Workers not registeringMaster UI shows 0 workers after start-workers.shCheck SSH (ssh spark@worker-host). Check firewall port 7077. Check spark-env.sh SPARK_MASTER_HOST is resolvable from workers.
"Connection refused" on submitspark-submit fails connecting to masterVerify master is running (jps → shows Master). Check port 7077 open. Check /etc/hosts on client.
Java version mismatchUnsupportedClassVersionErrorInstall SAME Java version on ALL nodes. java -version must match everywhere.
Python version mismatchPySpark UDF errors, pickle failuresSet PYSPARK_PYTHON=python3.11 in spark-env.sh. Same Python on driver AND executors.
"Cannot assign address"Worker fails to bind RPC portSet SPARK_LOCAL_IP in spark-env.sh to the node's actual IP (not 127.0.0.1).
Shuffle fetch failuresFetchFailedException during jobsCheck network between executors (ports 33000-34000). Check SPARK_LOCAL_DIRS disk space.
Worker OOM killed by OSWorker process disappears silentlyCheck dmesg | grep -i oom. Reduce SPARK_WORKER_MEMORY. Ensure swap is disabled.
Event log errorsHistory Server emptyCheck spark.eventLog.dir path exists on ALL nodes (or use HDFS/NFS shared path).
DNS resolution failsHostname not foundVerify /etc/hosts has ALL node entries on ALL machines. Or use IP addresses in conf.
Clock skew warningsKerberos/auth failures, odd timestampsInstall NTP: sudo apt install ntp and sync all nodes. Max allowed skew: 5 minutes.
⚙️ Engineering Insight — Multi-Machine Best Practices (1) Use configuration management (Ansible/Puppet/Chef) — never hand-configure more than 3 nodes. (2) Shared event log directory — use NFS mount or HDFS path for spark.eventLog.dir so the History Server can access logs from all jobs. (3) Separate shuffle SSD — use dedicated NVMe drives for SPARK_LOCAL_DIRS; shuffle I/O on the OS disk kills performance. (4) NTP sync — install chrony or ntpd on all nodes; clock skew breaks Kerberos and makes log correlation impossible. (5) Monitor with Prometheus — add node-exporter on all machines for OS-level metrics (CPU, RAM, disk I/O, network) alongside Spark metrics.
⚠️ Common Pitfall — Network Split Between Nodes If one worker loses network connectivity to the master, the master marks it as "DEAD" after spark.worker.timeout (default 60s). All executors on that worker are killed, and running tasks are re-scheduled on other workers. If the worker recovers, it re-registers automatically. For production, use bonded NICs (LACP) for network redundancy and set spark.network.timeout=600s to tolerate brief network blips.
🎯 Interview Tip "How would you set up Spark on 10 bare-metal machines?" — "I'd plan the topology first: 1 master + 1 edge + 8 workers. On all nodes: install the same Java/Python version, configure /etc/hosts for name resolution, create a spark user, and set file limits. On the master: generate SSH keys and distribute to all workers for passwordless SSH. Download Spark once on master, configure spark-env.sh (worker cores/memory), spark-defaults.conf (AQE, Kryo, event logging), and the workers file. Rsync the entire Spark directory to all nodes. Start master, then start-workers.sh which SSHs to each worker. Verify via Master UI at port 8080. For production, I'd automate with Ansible and add HDFS for data locality, Hive Metastore for SQL catalog, and Prometheus for monitoring. Key gotchas: same Java/Python everywhere, firewall ports 7077 + 33000-34000, and NTP sync."
